File.Name: 4215d_4215-038.jpg
Notes: Beautiful waxy buds of Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) are more colorful than the flowers that will soon open. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2004)

File.Name: 4215e_4215-045.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Sour Grass aka Bermuda Buttercup (Oxalis pes-caprae). It's a spreading non-native weed in the mountains. It has clover-like leaves and a pleasant tart taste that kids like, even those who won't nibble other wild plants. The irony is that it contains oxalic acid that really is cumulative and toxic in quantity. My digital camera can't get the color right. It should be much yellower with less obvious honey-guide lines. Is this an infrared light thing? (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2004)

File.Name: 4215f_4215-081.jpg
Notes: Blushing flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). Native ants are working these flowers. Look at the bottom-center to see an ant entering the urn-like flower. These shrubs also develop interesting leaf galls caused by specialized aphids that exude sweet honeydew that the ants harvest. Ants and aphids sometimes have a connection. Are the ants in charge? (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2004)

File.Name: 4216g_4216-164.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Cortinarius mushroom - maybe C. fulmineus or kin in the Bulbopodium subgenera. You can see remnants of the cobwebby "cortina" veil that covers the immature gills and tears free when the cap opens, stained red-brown from falling spores. Look close and you might see some of the tiny critters who call this mushroom home! (See [Previous] photo.) (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4216h_4216-164.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Tiny mites or spiders or insects crawling over the gills of of the same Cortinarius mushroom as the [Previous] photo - maybe C. fulmineus or kin in the Bulbopodium subgenera. I think they have 6 legs, but I've been attacked today by 6-legged immature ticks, so that's not enough to decide between spider and insect. The webs on the gills are NOT spider webs. They are part of the cobwebby "cortina" veil that covers the immature gills and tears free when the cap opens. They are stained red-brown from the spores. What a world for these tiny critters! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4216i_4216-156.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Here's a lesson in Cortinarius anatomy that I never quite understood before. This is maybe C. fulmineus or kin in the Bulbopodium subgenera with a viscid cap and bulbous stem. (It's hard to tell in a mature mushroom like this.) All Cortinarius mushrooms have a cortina of cobwebby hairs that covers the immature gills as a "partial veil". This one also has a cobwebby "universal veil" that covered the whole immature cap. You can see the difference here between the hairs dangling down the stem and the hairs that have fallen away from the base. All the hairs are colored red-brown from the falling spores. I don't collect these for the table, but they are diverse and common and beautiful and interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; C. fulmineus; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius; cortina
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; C. fulmineus; Bulbopodium; Cortinarius; cortina

File.Name: 4216a_4216-118.jpg
Notes: First beautiful flowers of Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora). This interesting native is a green root-parasite of chaparral shrubs. You usually have to crawl to find it, but I was lucky today. I guess this one is growing from the roots of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) that was always nearby. Is this a three-way relationship between plant-fungus-shrub? It's related to Paint Brush (Castilleja), and most of the color comes from leafy bracts. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; Bigberry Manzanita; Paint Brush

File.Name: 4216b_4216-169.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii). Today was overcast with some raindrops, perfect for these delicate wildflowers. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4216c_4216-187.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Wooly Paint Brush (Castilleja foliosa) growing on a sandstone outcrop. The bright red "petals" are colorful leafy bracts that enclose the slender yellow-green flowers that are visible here. There is also a form with yellow bracts, but it wasn't quite ready to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4216d_4216-147.jpg
Notes: The first flower on this Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum). This sub-shrub has sharp needle-like leaves. The surprisingly large and delicate flowers are rolled up in an elegant way. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4216e_4216-128.jpg
Notes: New fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) fern growing from a sandstone outcrop covered with Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii). The ferns seem to come from nothing. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4216f_4216-164.jpg
Notes: Tiny mites or spiders or insects crawling over the gills of of this Cortinarius mushroom that I uprooted today - maybe C. fulmineus or kin in the Bulbopodium subgenera with a viscid cap and bulbous stem. I think they have 6 legs, but I've been attacked today by 6-legged immature ticks, so that's not enough to decide between spider and insect. The webs on the gills are NOT spider webs. They are part of the cobwebby "cortina" veil that covers the immature gills and tears free when the cap opens. They are stained red-brown from the spores. What a world for these tiny critters! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4217h_4216-130.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] I think this is Hygrophorus sordidus. The cap is cauliflower-size, and this is only part of it. I don't know what that gray-black color is, but I'm glad it's not on me! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4217g_4216-107.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] I think this is a Hygrophorus - maybe H. sordidus? Others were nearby under oak and manzanita. This cap is almost devoured by the cobwebby mycelium of an unkwown fungus invader. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4217a_4216-175.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii). It was cloudy with some raindrops yesterday. I shot this photo against the overcast sky, low-key for a delicate wildflower. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4217b_4217-196.jpg
Notes: Urn-shaped flowers of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis). It's easy to confuse this with our earlier flowering Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca), but this shrub has a burl and hairier stems and several nutlets in the fruit rather than one large nut. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2004) It was gloomy and windy today, hard to get keeper photos. I hope this is the prelude to real rain tomorrow as predicted. We need it. This is our third dry year in a row.

File.Name: 4217c_4215-064.jpg
Notes: Thick yellow flowers of California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica). The leaves have an intense Bay Laurel taste and are useful for cooking if you don't overdo it. It's related to avocado in the Lauraceae family. The summer fruit are REALLY intense! (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2004)

File.Name: 4217d_4215-055.jpg
Notes: Tiny wasp trying to escape an early flower of Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata). These flowers seem to have a narcotic effect on their pollinators. See [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2004)

File.Name: 4217e_4215-075.jpg
Notes: Twining stems of Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) can support themselves well enough to climb upward over new growth on a recently burned area in the mountains. It`s a common native that sometimes takes over as a fire-follower. (San Marcos Pass, 15 February 2004)

File.Name: 4217f_4217-199.jpg
Notes: First female pistillate flowers of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii). Like most local plants with separate sexes, the male flowers appear well before the females. No comment, but it is a stumper. I have photos of the male plants at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=4878042 and [Previous] from that. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2004)

File.Name: 4218g_4217-205.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Small coin-size cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms during a dry spell. The green seedling to the right is Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata). (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2004)

File.Name: 4218h_4217-209.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Small coin-size cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms that I moved a few feet to a sunnier place to photograph "ex situ". It's a dry year. Note the aborted caps at the base and the odd pups, like growing arms from your arm! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2004)

File.Name: 4218a_4218-213.jpg
Notes: Boat-like Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves floating in raindrops on our car roof. We had over an inch of much needed rain today in the Santa Barbara mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2004)

File.Name: 4218b_1213-038.jpg
Notes: This week in history - It's been raining all day, so it's a chance to look in my archives. Three years ago this week, we had six inches of snow here in the Santa Barbara mountains, enough that I got to call school for a "snow day" and stay home! This photo is the view down our driveway. You can see a California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) bent over the road. There would be more broken trees, but we had over a foot of snow the year before that really clobbered our forest! The woods are still a mess, choked with fallen branches. I learned an important lesson from these storms: pines shed snow, bay trees bend, and oaks break. It wouldn't take too many years of this extreme weather to replace our native oak woodland with a forest more like Oregon. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2001)

File.Name: 4218c_3219-049.jpg
Notes: This week in history - It's been raining all day. The rest of today's photos are from our hike a year ago this week at the Arroyo Hondo Preserve in the Santa Ynez Mountains foothills. New flowers of Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia?) getting ready to open. It's typical that these flowers in the Morning-Glory family (Convolvulaceae) twist like this in-bud. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 February 2003)

File.Name: 4218d_3219-026a.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Tauschia arguta, sometimes called Southern Umbrellawort - what a horrible common name! It's in the Parsley family (Apiaceae), and the summer seeds have an interesting caraway taste. I love that maroon party ball at the base of the umbel! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 February 2003)

File.Name: 4218e_3219-074.jpg
Notes: A dangling catkin of bright male flowers of Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa). Look close at the upper right leaf for an insect egg on a stalk. It must be spring! "Birds do it, bees do it, even mushrooms and trees do it..." (Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 February 2003) See my stumper at http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/04apr03.html

File.Name: 4218f_3219-061.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Meadow Rue (Thalictrum fendleri var. polycarpum) lack petals but not beauty. They are hard to photograph because the head-high stems seem to sway even without wind. They grow in the foothill canyons. I haven't found them up here in the mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 February 2003)

File.Name: 4219i_4219-234.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] When good 'shrooms go bad... This once-delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom is showing its age. These have been in the ground since the New Year, see http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=4246813 and [previous]. I NEVER harvest them all for the table. Gotta think about next year! (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4219h_4216-136.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Convoluted gills of the large Hygrophorus sordidus mushroom in the [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4219g_4216-131.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] I think this large mushroom is Hygrophorus sordidus. The cap is cauliflower-size. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2004)

File.Name: 4219a_4219-232.jpg
Notes: A perfect raindrop reflecting the world of this Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) leaf. Lupines do that. It rained yesterday and it might rain tomorrow. Today was overcast and absolutely still, like the forest was holding its breath. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2004)

File.Name: 4219b_4219-218.jpg
Notes: Chickweed (Stellaria media) is an introduced annual weed, but by now it's more native than I am. It's edible, with a spicy taste that goes well with Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata). (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2004)

File.Name: 4219c_4219-222.jpg
Notes: Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica or kin) mushroom growing between brackets of Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor aka Coriolus versicolor) on an old oak log. This odd mushroom freshens up whenever it rains, but almost disappears in between. The little specks are tiny Springtail (Collembola) insects. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2004)
ScientificName: Tremella mesenterica; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor; Collembola
associatedTaxa: Tremella mesenterica; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor; Collembola

File.Name: 4219d_4219-248.jpg
Notes: New frond of Goldback Fern (Pentagramma triangularis) still holding yesterday's raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2004)

File.Name: 4219e_4219-250.jpg
Notes: Tiny perennial California Bedstraw (Galium californicum) plants make their way out from under the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2004)

File.Name: 4219f_4219-244.jpg
Notes: Lots of new seedlings or immatures of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) sprouting along the creek. But hey baby, it's a wild world. So much will happen between hopeful sprouting and flowering. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2004)

File.Name: 4220a_4220-296.jpg
Notes: Face to "face" with a six-inch long Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) negotiating a steep descent over a moss covered sandstone outcrop in the drizzle today. He or she? - No s/he's both! "It" is the biggest terrestrial slug in North America. Note the dark eye-spots on the stalks up front and the breathing hole on the side. (And never mind the raindrop on the lens.) This one was shy at first and withdrew all those stalks, but it started moving again after 10 minutes or so with me just waiting. I expect to find banana slugs on rainy days like this, but somehow these critters survive our dry summers. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2004) Yipes, looking back at this photo it looks like one critter eating another! That is creepy. It's become my after-school Hike Club rite-of-passage to "kiss a banana slug". Doesn't hurt, but it is kind of like kissing rubber cement. I worry about critters like these as the world becomes simpler. Fans root for banana slugs at UCSC, and so do I. I think of them as an "indicator species" of where I want to live. So far I'm happy here in the Santa Barbara mountains.

File.Name: 4220b_4220-291.jpg
Notes: Face to face with a six-inch long Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) negotiating a steep descent over a moss covered sandstone outcrop in the rain today. Notice the black eye-spots on the upper stalks but not the lower feelers. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2004)

File.Name: 4220c_4220-283.jpg
Notes: It must be Spring! (*sorry*) These are the twisted spring-like tendrils of Wild Cucumber aka California Man-Root (Marah fabaceus). They are just starting to flower up here in the mountains. I just noticed the occasional transition from right-hand to left-hand, and I wonder if there is a pattern. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2004)

File.Name: 4220d_4220-269.jpg
Notes: Bird's Foot Fern (Pellea mucronata). "Mucronate" means that it has a sharp point, and you can see the points on the little leaves, along with a few raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2004)

File.Name: 4220e_4220-260.jpg
Notes: Living and dead wood on a large trunk of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the light rain today. It looks and feels like human skin, complete with wrinkles. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2004)

File.Name: 4220f_4220-270.jpg
Notes: Spent urn-shaped flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) on the ground - and lots more, like a natural potpourri. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2004)

File.Name: 4221g_4219-254.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Another Cortinarius mushroom, probably Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus. They have beautiful colors when fresh, but they all look the same with age. I've seen (and posted) lots of Cortinarii. It was drizzling when I took this photo and it's still drizzling, with real rain forcast for tonight and tomorrow. Somehow the mushrooms know they can't disperse their spores in the rain so they wait. I'm hoping for many new mushrooms (including Amanitas) soon! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2004)

File.Name: 4221h_4219-255.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Another Cortinarius mushroom, probably Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus. The fertile gills are still covered with veils of slime and cobwebby hairs to protect the spores. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2004)

File.Name: 4221a_4221-001.jpg
Notes: Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) is starting to flower. There is a stumper in this photo. The flower on the right has it's lavender-blue male stamens open. The later flowers on the left have their female pistils ready, and the stamens are already withered and brown. Why do the male parts usually develop first in MOST (if not all) plants? (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2004) It's been drizzling all day, and the forecast is for real rain tonight. Only fotolog could get me out laying on the wet ground trying to cover my camera lens (etc.) in the rain today!

File.Name: 4221b_4221-023.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Succulent Lupine (Lupinus succulentus) flowers in the drizzle today. This is the most common lupine along our mountain roads, but I suspect much of it comes from seed packs. Ditto with California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica). We may never know the original distribution of these pretty wildflowers. They are still nice to see along the roads! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2004)

File.Name: 4221c_4221-007.jpg
Notes: Chaparral Lotus (Lotus grandiflorus) flowers in the drizzle today. This grows on the south-facing slopes, but I rarely find it on the summits. This botanical Lotus is not related to the Sacred Lotus (Nelumbo nucifera) of Buddhism. They do have their charm. I like how the old flowers turn reddish in age. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2004)

File.Name: 4221d_4221-049.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Holly-Leaved Cherry aka Islay (Prunus ilicifolia) in the rain today. The fruit come in the summer, and they are edible and tasty, though you don't get much because of the large pits. The pits are edible too if you prepare them right, but they can be poisonous! They were an important native American food. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2004)

File.Name: 4221e_4221-019.jpg
Notes: I think all lupines catch raindrops. This is native Little Lupine aka Lindley Lupine (Lupinus bicolor). It's kind of weedy and likes to grow along roads and waste areas. Is this how the microscope was discovered? (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2004)

File.Name: 4221f_4221-027.jpg
Notes: I was out in the mountains in the drizzle today, and the chaparral is flowering! The white flowers are BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) and the red fruit are Toyon or Christmas Berry (Heteromeles arbutifolia) still waiting for the birds. The blur in the corner is a raindrop on my lens. This land burned to the ground in the 1990 Painted Cave Fire, and here it is today. It's hard to grab this much life in 500 pixels, so I usually stick with close shots. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2004)

File.Name: 4222h_4219-243.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Looking down on brackets of Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor aka Coriolus versicolor) and a green sprout of Wood Mint (Stachys bullata). (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2004)
ScientificName: Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor; Stachys bullata
associatedTaxa: Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor; Stachys bullata

File.Name: 4222g_4219-224.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica or kin) mushroom growing between brackets of Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor aka Coriolus versicolor) on an old oak log. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2004)
ScientificName: Tremella mesenterica; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor
associatedTaxa: Tremella mesenterica; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor

File.Name: 4222a_4221-042a.jpg
Notes: Looking into the closed flower of an African Daisy (Gazania linearis) in the rain. I focused on the raindrops on the tips of the ray-flowers of the composite head. These flowers close at night and stay closed on rainy days, but I got to look inside. It's a beautiful introduced weed that is becoming too common along the roads into our mountains. Was it introduced after the 1990 Painted Cave Fire that burned this to the ground? (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2004) It's been too dark and rainy to get out with my camera today. Just as well. I have work to do. I go back to school tomorrow after a wonderful vacation week with time to hike every day and take lots of photos. *sigh*

File.Name: 4222b_4221-039.jpg
Notes: Looking down into the closed flower of an African Daisy (Gazania linearis) in the rain. These are the the disk-flowers in the center of this composite head, like the inside flowers of a sunflower. The flowers close at night and stay closed in the rain, but I got to look inside. It's a beautiful weed that shouldn't be in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2004)

File.Name: 4222c_4221-037.jpg
Notes: Long petals of African Daisy (Gazania linearis) with raindrops. These are really the ray-flowers of this composite head, like the outside yellow "petals" of a sunflower. The flowers close at night and stay closed in the rain, but I got to look inside. It's a beautiful weed that shouldn't be in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2004)

File.Name: 4222d_4221-043.jpg
Notes: New flower bud of African Daisy (Gazania linearis) in light rain. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2004)

File.Name: 4222e_4221-056.jpg
Notes: Looking up at the pines and chaparral on Fremont Ridge in light rain. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2004)

File.Name: 4222f_4221-032.jpg
Notes: Sweet-smelling white flowers of BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) covering the hills like snow with Santa Barbara and Goleta barely visible in the distance. This is looking down at the canyon where the 1990 Painted Cave Fire began, near the top of the "Old Pass Road". That's what wildfire does for chaparral in the long run! Even so, I'm glad it's raining, it's our third dry year in a row. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 February 2004)

File.Name: 4223a_4223-085.jpg
Notes: First flowers of California Peony (Paeonia californica), with yesterday's raindrops. It's an odd chaparral perennial herb that never opens much more than this, and you have to lay on the ground to see the walnut-sized flowers. It has been collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. I love the Ranunculus-like foliage. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 February 2004) Wouldn't you know it was a brilliant clear morning today when I go back to school after a vacation week of gloomy drizzle. No problem, I love the rain, and today was an "In-Service" day so I found some time to shoot a few photos in the woodland near Lake Cachuma on my way to school.

File.Name: 4223b_4223-078.jpg
Notes: Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti, maybe intermediate to var. hoffmannii). Note how the stamens clasp the style and the green glands at the base of the fused petals. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 February 2004)

File.Name: 4223c_4223-090.jpg
Notes: First flower of Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii var. integrifolia), with a raindrop. The species is often included in wildflower seed packs. The native flowers are smaller but they bring rich color to the woodland. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 February 2004)

File.Name: 4223d_4223-098.jpg
Notes: First flowers I've seen of California Buttercup (Ranunculus californicus). My digital camera seems to have trouble with yellows, and I don't think I can to justice to the varnished look of these woodland flowers. I'll keep trying. Rub a flower on your forehead - if it leaves a stain, you're in love! (Of course I am - with family and fotolog!) (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 February 2004)

File.Name: 4223e_4223-093.jpg
Notes: California Maiden-Hair Fern (Adiantum jordanii) growing in the woodland under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). The Southern Maiden-Hair Fern (A. capillus-veneris) with more angular leaves is more likely on rock outcrops and waterfalls. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 February 2004)
CommonNames: California Maiden-Hair Fern; Coast Live Oak; Southern Maiden-Hair Fern
ScientificName: Adiantum jordanii; Quercus agrifolia; A. capillus-veneris
associatedTaxa: Adiantum jordanii; Quercus agrifolia; A. capillus-veneris

File.Name: 4223f_4223-070.jpg
Notes: Wouldn't you know that it was a brilliant clear morning today when I go back to school after a week a of gloomy drizzle! No problem, I love the rain, and today was an "In-Service" day so I found some time to catch a few photos. This is an "Old Man Cactus" (there are many kinds) and another Opuntia in our garden still holding yesterday's raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2004)

File.Name: 4225a_4225-144.jpg
Notes: Raindrops on the window at school in the hard rain today, 4-1/2 inches so far back at home. I want to get out there with my camera - but not yet! (Santa Ynez Valley, 25 February 2004)

File.Name: 4227a_4226-198.jpg
Notes: These Polypore "Bracket fungi" - maybe Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor aka Coriolus versicolor) - grow on dead oak wood. They are common in the woods, but they freshen up with glowing colors that surprise me after every rain. I love those comb-like hairs on the edges. There's so much to see in the woods when you look close! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 February 2004)

File.Name: 4227b_4226-191.jpg
Notes: Beautiful gills of a Snowy Waxy Cap (Hygrophorus borealis or kin) mushroom growing under oaks. It has waxy gills and a dry cap. It may be edible, but there's not enough flesh to matter. Beauty is enough. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 February 2004)

File.Name: 4227c_4226-208.jpg
Notes: Delicious new Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms are already appearing after recent rains. They don't really have "gills" like most mushrooms. The shallow blunt "false gills" are easy to scrape away. I'll be looking for more this weekend! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 February 2004)

File.Name: 4227d_4226-209.jpg
Notes: New flower spike of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius), with the characteristic palmate leaf in the background. It won't be long now! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 February 2004)

File.Name: 4228a_4228-031.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) along our creek. These seem to be female pistillate flowers, which shoots my theory that males usually appear first. Willows have catkins that look wind-pollinated, but they produce nectar to attract insects. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2004)

File.Name: 4228b_4228-064.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii). (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2004)

File.Name: 4228c_4228-015.jpg
Notes: The glossy new leaves of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) are pretty, but I never noticed how beautiful the translucent stems are. Enjoy, don't touch. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2004)

File.Name: 4228d_4228-014.jpg
Notes: Fern fractals - new fronds of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta). (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2004)

File.Name: 4228e_4228-051.jpg
Notes: Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom hiding in the oak leaves. It seems to be making its own light. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2004)

File.Name: 4228f_4228-001.jpg
Notes: This Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) looks like it came fresh from the oven. The cap is usually fissured like this, and the stem is yellow above and red deeper down. We had a lot of rain in the past week, but I'm mostly finding old mushrooms. Somehow the 'shrooms know when it's raining and too wet to release spores. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2004)

File.Name: 4229h_4228-046.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Young Cortinarius cylindripes (or kin) mushroom has brilliant colors and the remains of a slimy "universal veil" that covers and protects the mushroom before it opens. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2004)

File.Name: 4229g_4228-039.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] I think this is an old Cortinarius cylindripes (or kin). The pretty violet gills are fading to brown with age. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2004)

File.Name: 4229a_4229-108.jpg
Notes: First flower of Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum). I hope there will be many more flowers (and photos)to come, but the first is special. It's a classic hummingbird flower starting to flower just when the hummers are starting to visit! (San Marcos Pass, 29 February 2004)

File.Name: 4229b_4228-035.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) coming from the distinctive round umbrella-like leaves. (Early leaves have different shapes.) The leaves AND flowers make a tasty salad. It does well in the garden. Please harvest a few leaves rather than the whole plant so they will keep producing! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2004)

File.Name: 4229c_4228-040.jpg
Notes: Very slimy Cortinarius cylindripes (or kin) mushroom poking up through the oak leaves and catching the sun just right. It lacks the scaly slime-bands of C. collinitus. The slime is a "universal veil" that covers and protects the mushroom before it opens. We have many Cortinarius species that look about the same when old. But the young caps are very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2004)

File.Name: 4229d_4228-053.jpg
Notes: Perfect geometry of the gills of this Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom. I posted another photo of the same mushroom yesterday, check the archives. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2004)

File.Name: 4229e_4228-102.jpg
Notes: Zeller's Bolete (Boletus zelleri) is one of our most colorful mushrooms, but it's easy to walk right over them because all you see is the smoky almost-black "fuscous" cap. It has pores instead of gills, like all boletes, but these are especially colorful. The pores of this specimen did not stain blue when bruised. (San Marcos Pass, 29 February 2004)

File.Name: 4229f_4229-088.jpg
Notes: Large Cup Fungus (Peziza badia or kin). The young caps are neat little round cups, but they expand and split with age. The spore-bearing surface is on the outside. It's like the earth is sprouting ears to listen to our murmurings. (San Marcos Pass, 29 February 2004)

File.Name: 4305a_4304-173.jpg
Notes: Well-named flowers of Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii ssp. insulare). Note how the flower buds point UP, the open flowers point DOWN, and they point UP again when finished. These are primrose relatives in the Primulaceae family. (Figueroa Mountain, 4 March 2004)

File.Name: 4305b_4304-207.jpg
Notes: Delicate flowers of Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) remind me of ballet dancers. Hiking through the spiny shrubs also takes some dancing! (Figueroa Mountain, 4 March 2004)

File.Name: 4305c_4304-204.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Coffee Fern (Pellea andromedifolia) grows mostly on rock outcrops. (Figueroa Mountain, 4 March 2004)

File.Name: 4305d_4304-177.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Lindley's Lupine (Lupinus bicolor). This species is highly variable around here. I haven't seen any flowers at home yet, but they are usually much smaller and less colorful. It's easy to think "lupine" and move on, but nature is more interesting when you notice the differences. (Figueroa Mountain, 4 March 2004)

File.Name: 4305e_4304-215.jpg
Notes: Gnarly burl of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). I see mocking faces here! (Figueroa Mountain, 4 March 2004)

File.Name: 4305f_4304-193.jpg
Notes: I took my after-school Hike Club kids up Figueroa Mountain this week. This is our view looking across the Santa Ynez Valley towards the Santa Ynez Mountains. Everything is green after recent rains. (Figueroa Mountain, 4 March 2004)

File.Name: 4306g_4306-018.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Unknown mushrooms coming up from the burl of a Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)

File.Name: 4306h_4306-008.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Yet another Cortinarius mushroom ("YAC"?), but check the [Previous] photo to see why I thought it was an Agaricus. It doesn't have a real ring other than the cobwebby "cortina", but the stem is split to suggest one. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)

File.Name: 4306i_4306-005.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] I was sure this was an Agaricus. I could even see the hint of a ring underneath. Nope, it's another Cortinarius. The only hint is the rusty-colored edge of the cap. See the [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)

File.Name: 4306a_4306-061.jpg
Notes: First flowers of the volunteer Peach Tree (Prunus persica?) that decided to grow behind our house. I reckon someone tossed a peach-pit. We usually get a few small fruit that the birds harvest first. This photo is hand-held with natural light, another lucky shot. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)

File.Name: 4306b_4306-033.jpg
Notes: New shoot of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) gives hope for summer flowers to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)

File.Name: 4306c_4306-013.jpg
Notes: First flower of Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata). This was a lucky hand-held shot (out of many) in weak light when I only had my low "mushroom tripod". This is an unusual native in our mountains that really belongs further north. We also have desert plants like Great Basin Sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata) and tropical plants like Fremontia (Fremontodendron californicum). The limited distribution of these remnant species says a lot about the swings of our climate history. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)
CommonNames: Shiny-Leaved Barberry; Great Basin Sagebrush; Fremontia
ScientificName: Berberis pinnata; Artemisia tridentata; Fremontodendron californicum
associatedTaxa: Berberis pinnata; Artemisia tridentata; Fremontodendron californicum

File.Name: 4306d_4306-012.jpg
Notes: Beautiful new leaves of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum). Enjoy, don't touch. I hike through Poison Oak constantly. It doesn't bother me unless I really ask for it. Maybe that's why? (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)

File.Name: 4306e_4306-031.jpg
Notes: New "fiddlehead" of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens). Edible, but this one looks gnarly and mysterious rather than inviting. This summer-flowering perennial fern is unusual in our summer-dry mountains. This is the first new growth I've seen. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)

File.Name: 4306f_4306-040.jpg
Notes: Developing flower head of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) with the sun behind. You can see the pin-like gland-tipped hairs that carry tiny drops of fragrant resin. The sticky leaves make a decent tea if you add a bit of honey. I sometimes roll a leaf and suck on the wad. It's tasty but astringent. This native sage does very well in the dry end of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)

File.Name: 4307a_4307-081.jpg
Notes: New flowers in the garden - Freesia? I don't understand the anatomy, and they seem to have seven petals which is not right. Pretty though. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2004)

File.Name: 4307b_4306-059.jpg
Notes: First flower of this Daffodil (Narcissus sp.) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)

File.Name: 4307c_4307-066.jpg
Notes: Flowers and developing fruit of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii). This photo is hand-held in low-light with a breeze. A bit fuzzy, but there's something peaceful about the sunlight filtered through those leaves that I love. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2004)

File.Name: 4307d_4305-263.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Popcorn Flower (Plagiobothrys nothofulvus?). This one has purple dye in the leaf veins that leaves a stain when you rub a leaf on your fingernail. There is a very confusing complex of species in Plagiobothrys and Cryptantha in the Borage family (Boraginaceae). "Popcorn Flower" is good enough for me for all of them! (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 March 2004)

File.Name: 4307e_4305-268.jpg
Notes: First flowers I've seen of Common Fiddleneck (Amsinkia menziesii var. intermedia). It's native but kind of weedy. There's a confusing group of polypoid relatives near the beach. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 March 2004)

File.Name: 4307f_4305-273.jpg
Notes: I had to lay flat and squint sideways at my camera LCD screen to frame this shot of an open California Peony (Paeonia californica) flower. The flowers always face the earth, and this is as open as they get. Native peonies usually hide under the chaparral, but this area near Lake Cachuma burned a couple of years ago. The perennial roots have been collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. I'd rather see flowers. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 March 2004)

File.Name: 4310a_4310-115.jpg
Notes: Homemade paper with a California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) flower pressed in the grain. This is from Julie`s "Art From Scrap" elective class at my school, caught as I walked past on my way to the office. I`m much too busy with school right now, with grades this week and science fair next week. Fotolog is also too busy tonight. Julie`s pretty poppy-paper cheered me up! (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 March 2004)

File.Name: 4311a_4306-064.jpg
Notes: We collected a broken piece of this Silver Cholla (Opuntia echinocarpa) cactus in the high desert near our Lone Pine cabin. It's doing well in the garden. It doesn't jump, but I'm still careful walking past it in shorts! (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)

File.Name: 4311b_4306-029.jpg
Notes: New shoots of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) are vulnerable in so many ways to deer and rabbits and insects, not to mention shovels and neighbors. A single plant like this can grow twelve feet high with dozens of perfect lily flowers. I hope this one makes it! (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004)

File.Name: 4311c_4306-043.jpg
Notes: Beautiful new leaves of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum), shiny with new resin. Enjoy, don't touch. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2004) See my Poison Oak stumper at http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/20mar98.html

File.Name: 4311d_4307-058.jpg
Notes: Common Bedstraw aka Goose Grass (Galium aparine) coming up in the woods. The whorled leaves are distinctive. The stems remind me of grasshopper legs! Said to be introduced, but it's so common even in the backcountry that it's native now. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2004)

File.Name: 4311e_4307-076.jpg
Notes: Thick flower clusters on a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree. They are having a good year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2004)

File.Name: 4311f_4307-072.jpg
Notes: Native moss advancing across a sandstone outcrop. It looks like a war map. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2004)

File.Name: 4313c_2321-024.jpg
Notes: Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) with flowers. Uncommon and beautiful. This is from the archives. (Figueroa Mountain, 21 March 2002) I'm busy this weekend, so today's photos are all from the vault.

File.Name: 4313d_1413-057.jpg
Notes: Vernal pond slowly drying up, but still supporting a ring of interesting plants around the edge. These ephemeral ponds are an important habitat for interesting flowers and amphibians. This is from the archives. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2001)

File.Name: 4313e_1419-051.jpg
Notes: Tidy Tips (Layia platyglossa) flowers really do have tidy tips! This is from the archives. (Figueroa Mountain, 19 April 2001)

File.Name: 4313f_1413-169.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Owl's Clover (Castilleja densiflora). Most of the color is from the leafy bracts not the flowers. I believe the name comes from the Burrowing Owls (Athene cunicularia) who sometimes nest with the flowers. I remember fields of these flowers, but now they are occasional. What about the owls? This is from the archives. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2001)

File.Name: 4321a_2404-112.jpg
Notes: New flower of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) sticking its lower lip out at me. The stamens really do bend like that. Sometimes you get a drop of nectar if you suck on a flower. Hooray it's spring and big school projects are done for a while! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2004)

File.Name: 4321b_4320-016.jpg
Notes: Oregon Fawn Lily (Erythronium oregonum - I think) is a very delicate lily in the Oregon woods. Other related species have interesting names like "Dog's-tooth Violet," "Trout Lily," and "Adder's Tongue." (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 April 2002) This is for lotusgreen - see http://www.fotolog.net/lotusgreen/?photo_id=7614634

File.Name: 4322a_4321-059.jpg
Notes: It's spring and even the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees are flowering. Look close at these male pollen-bearing catkins and notice those reddish structures that don't quite look like stamens. They are really tiny wasp galls. See my [previous] photo for a closer look. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2004)

File.Name: 4322b_4321-075.jpg
Notes: There is so much life around us that we don't notice! I was trying to get a photo of the beautiful catkins on our flowering Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. It's hard even with a slight breeze, and my tripod doesn't help hold the tree still! Then I noticed that most of the catkins are galled with wasp larvae. Gall wasps insert their eggs and a dose of RNA to re-program the tree to produce the abnormal gall growth that nourishes the larvae within. Different wasps initiate different kinds of growth in different plant structures. This particular oak tree by our mailbox is heavily colonized with catkin-galls. There are (at least) two different kinds of galls in this photo. The large red peanut-shaped gall on the stem of the catkin in the center is made by the gall wasp Callirhytis congregata (or kin). It is polythalamous, which means there are many larvae hosted inside. The reddish horn-shaped galls on individual stamens are home to a different gall wasp, maybe the Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiousus or kin). This one is monothalamous with just one larva per gall. Oak tree catkins seem so ephemeral, yet they support generations of life we rarely notice. That's biodiversity in action. Are gall wasps important? Do they interfere with oak reproduction? Is the world poorer without them? (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2004)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Callirhytis congregata; polythalamous; Dryocosmus dubiousus; monothalamous
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Callirhytis congregata; polythalamous; Dryocosmus dubiousus; monothalamous

File.Name: 4322c_4321-087.jpg
Notes: Delicate flowers of Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine). This pretty wildflower in the Saxifrage (Saxifragaceae) family hides in the woods and is rare - at least it's rarely common. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2004)

File.Name: 4322d_4321-060.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the woods at home. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2004)

File.Name: 4322e_4321-049.jpg
Notes: Perfect new leaves of deciduous Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus). These blackberry-relatives are common in Oregon, but they are scarce here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I rarely find fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2004)

File.Name: 4322f_4321-067.jpg
Notes: Waxy urn-shaped flowers of my favorite tree - Madrone (Arbutus menziesii). Our trees are in full flower now, but the flowers are usually out of reach. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2004)

File.Name: 4323a_4321-053.jpg
Notes: Blue flowers of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) against the blue sky. We have many species of California Lilac (Ceanothus), but this is our most common blue-flowered form that varies from dark blue to almost white. It's also known as Red Heart for the deep-red wood in the burl that is prized by local woodworkers. It has inch-long spines so it's painful to bushwack. It is the main host of the spectacular Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) with wings up to seven inches across; sometimes I find the magic silk cocoons. The flowers make soap suds you can wash your hands with. I think every plant has its stories, and this native has many! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2004)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; California Lilac; Red Heart; Ceanothus Silk Moth

File.Name: 4323b_4321-056.jpg
Notes: A flower cluster of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). Look close to see how the individual flowers in the cluster make perfect pentagons and pentagrams. Phi is here! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2004)

File.Name: 4323c_4320-010.jpg
Notes: Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii ssp. insulare) at home. The first flowers are starting to fade, but there are more coming. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2004)

File.Name: 4323d_4321-043.jpg
Notes: Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti) flowers in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2004)

File.Name: 4323e_4320-022.jpg
Notes: Pretty new foliage of California Tea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes). This is an uncommon perennial in the Pea Family (Fabaceae) that grows under the oaks in our woodland. The common name is interesting, but I've never tried making tea. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2004)

File.Name: 4323f_4321-081.jpg
Notes: Catkins on this Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree don't fit our usual idea of flowers, but they are beautiful and sexy in their own way. (Anyone else remember the "catkin scene" in Ken Russell's "Women in Love" movie??) Look close to see the red structures on the stamens that are really tiny wasp galls. See my [previous] two photos for a closer look. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2004)

File.Name: 4325a_1413-147.jpg
Notes: Common Monkey Flower (Mimulus guttatus) growing by a marshy seep. I have school conferences this week. No time for photos, but it's relaxing to pick through the archives. I know all these flowers are out there now! (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2001)

File.Name: 4325b_1413-134.jpg
Notes: Pretty Crinkled Wild Onion (Allium crispum) has wavy inner petals that you can see in this photo. They are edible and tasty, but even more beautiful. Leave them be. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2001)

File.Name: 4325c_1419-080.jpg
Notes: Hoary Nightshade (Solanum umbelliferum var. incanum) has furry white stems. (Figueroa Mountain, 19 April 2001)

File.Name: 4325e_1323-066.jpg
Notes: Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree with new leaves and flowers. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 March 2001)

File.Name: 4325f_1420-069.jpg
Notes: Unusually large Blue Oaks (Quercus douglasii) with new leaves and flowers. This native deciduous oak makes beautiful orchard-like woodlands that are wonderful to explore. (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 April 2001)

File.Name: 4326a_4326-186.jpg
Notes: Nature is beautiful and brutal at once. The pretty flower is a Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) aka Brodiaea for the squeamish. Then there's a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae?) that has captured a Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) and is sucking it dry. That`s life. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004)
CommonNames: Blue Dick; Brodiaea; Crab Spider; Bee Fly

File.Name: 4326b_4326-180.jpg
Notes: Bright flowers of Johnny-Jump-Up (Viola pedunculata). These prety wildflowers have a secret. Follow a single flower stem down to the base and pinch it off and chew on it. It tastes like wintergreen! (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004)

File.Name: 4326c_4326-194.jpg
Notes: I think this is Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi ssp. maritimum). These pretty flowers have a long spur in the back like the peak of a witches cap. They are not common. That's California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the background. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004)

File.Name: 4326d_4326-248.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea). It's a classic red hummingbird flower that tags the hummer with stamen or style to transfer pollen while it feeds on the nectar within. This will always be one of my favorite local wildflowers to photograph, again and again. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004)

File.Name: 4326e_4326-204.jpg
Notes: Lots of California Buttercups (Ranunculus californicus) with afternoon light (and wind). (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004)

File.Name: 4326f_4326-224.jpg
Notes: Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) is a party flower. The stems and leaves have backwards pointing bristles that stick to your clothes like velcro. The pretty flowers on the brittle stems make an easy decoration for a hike or fiesta. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004)

File.Name: 4327a_4326-241.jpg
Notes: A flower of Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi ssp. maritimum) tangled up with a stalk of Lacepod (Thysanocarpus curvipes). It's definately spring here in central California. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004) I'm off early tomorrow on a school trip backpacking across Joshua Tree National Monument. I'll be busy, but I reckon I'll get *a few* desert wildflower photos! We return on Friday.

File.Name: 4327b_4326-216.jpg
Notes: Owl's Clover (Castilleja densiflora). I believe this flower is named for the small Burrowing Owls (Speotyto cunicularia?) that like to nest in abandoned ground squirrel burrows in the same grassy habitat. I used to see whole fields of these flowers on my way to school twenty years ago. Now I'm happy to find a few. What about the owls? I guess that's progress. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004)

File.Name: 4327c_4326-162.jpg
Notes: Chinese Houses (Collinsia heterophylla), with beetles if you look close. The whorled flowers look like lupines at first, but they are really quite different. They are in the Figwort Family (Scrophulariaceae), related to penstemon and paintbrush. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004)

File.Name: 4327d_4326-199.jpg
Notes: Pretty seeds of Lacepod or Fringepod (Thysanocarpus curvipes) in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. The winged seeds catch the sun so nice in the afternoon. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004)

File.Name: 4327e_4326-220.jpg
Notes: Looking up at new leaves of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata). (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004)

File.Name: 4327f_4326-162.jpg
Notes: Cheerful flowers of Foothill Ash (Fraxinus dipetala). This uncommon native shrub never grows far from the river, but it's not a riparian plant like willow or cottonwood. (Santa Ynez Valley, 26 March 2004)

File.Name: 4403a_4330-381.jpg
Notes: I'm not sure what this little cactus is, maybe Fish-hook Cactus (Mammillaria tetrancistra)? At first I thought the bright red structures were fuschia-like flowers. D'oh, of course they are fruit, but they look so out of place. This whole cactus is smaller than my fist, and the beautiful smooth fruit are an inch or more long. It's hard to see, but there are one or more long reddish fish-hook-like spine coming from each tubercle. It's not common. (Joshua Tree, 30 March 2004)

File.Name: 4403b_4331-442.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris). We found many plants with buds, but this was the only one with flowers. And what flowers! It's a "prickley pear" type cactus without spines, though it has irritating bristles if you get too close. Sometimes nature just goes over the top! (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)

File.Name: 4403c_4331-477.jpg
Notes: Beautiful flowers of Desert Bluebells (Phacelia campanularia) growing along a rocky desert wash. (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)

File.Name: 4403d_4401-693.jpg
Notes: Ripe berries of Desert Mistletoe (Phoradendron californicum) growing on native Catclaw (Acacia greggii) with late afternoon light. The thorny shrubs still look dead, but I spotted a few new leaves starting to appear. We saw several pairs of Phainopepla birds eating the berries. (Joshua Tree, 1 April 2004)

File.Name: 4403e_4401-653.jpg
Notes: This is a whole plant of Purple Mat (Nama demissum) growing in a desert wash. The flowers are the biggest part! (Joshua Tree, 1 April 2004)

File.Name: 4404a_4401-608.jpg
Notes: Beautiful flowers of Desert Mallow (Sphaeralcea ambigua). It's a color my digital camera can't quite capture. Today's photos are all desert wildflowers from my school hike in Joshua Tree National Park last week. The desert is flowering! (Joshua Tree, 1 April 2004)

File.Name: 4404b_4330-373.jpg
Notes: This native Phacelia was blooming everywhere, usually growing under and up through another plant like yucca or juniper. (Maybe Phacelia distans or P. tanacetifolia?) (Joshua Tree, 30 March 2004)

File.Name: 4404c_4401-624.jpg
Notes: Fist-size flower of Basket Evening Primrose aka Devil's Lantern or Lion-in-a-Cage (Oenothera deltoides) growing in a sandy desert wash. Great names for this amazing large desert wildflower! (Joshua Tree, 1 April 2004)
CommonNames: Basket Evening Primrose; Devil's Lantern; Lion-in-a-Cage

File.Name: 4404d_4330-374.jpg
Notes: Looking into a flower of Mojave Yucca (Yucca schidigera). There are hundreds of these large thick flowers on each massive flower stalk. They look sexy and alien up close like this. (Joshua Tree, 30 March 2004)

File.Name: 4404e_4331-433.jpg
Notes: New flower stalk of Parry's Nolina (Nolina parryi, used to be N. wolfii?). This is a yucca-like plant that is different from both true yuccas and Joshua Trees. It has long thin leaves and a short thick trunk. I only found it on rocky outcrops. No flowers yet, but the buds and papery bracts are impressive. (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)

File.Name: 4404f_4331-452.jpg
Notes: Unusual flowers of Green Ephedra aka Mormon Tea (Ephedra viridis). This shrub is a Gymnosperm that is more related to pines than usual flowers with ovaries. Reproduction happens with these soft cones (strobili) rather than flowers. The only leaves are the dry scales barely visible by the cones on the left. This plant is male, and the female cones are on separate plants. It's an ancient herb for making a stimulating tea. I've also read that our North American shrubs do not contain the controversial alkaloid ephedrine. Please leave a comment or link if you know about this. (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)

File.Name: 4405g_4401-580.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Cryptobiotic soil crust in the Joshua Tree desert. This living soil is a slow-growing mix of cyanobacteria, fungi, algae, mosses, and lichens. It helps soil stability, water infiltration, and fertility. Don't walk on it. (Joshua Tree, 1 April 2004)

File.Name: 4405a_4331-529.jpg
Notes: Beautiful flowers of Bladder Sage (Salazaria mexicana) remind me of baby birds waiting for food. This is a shy scraggly plant that I usually found hiding within some other shrub. It's fragrant. The reddish calyxes inflate into paper-bag-like sacks after flowering that resemble Chinese lanterns. (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004) These are more photos today from our school backpack trek across Joshua Tree National Park last week. The desert is flowering! Check the archives for lots more.

File.Name: 4405b_4331-518.jpg
Notes: I think we all got "chollad" at least once on our school hike across Joshua Tree National Park last week. I carried pliers to remove the spines! This Pencil Cholla (Opuntia ramosissima) was often the culprit. Did you know the sharp spines are also so beautiful? (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)

File.Name: 4405c_4329-213.jpg
Notes: Leaves of Mojave Yucca (Yucca schidigera) always have these distinctive coarse peeling fibers along the edge. Native Americans used the fibers for rope, sandals, and cloth. (Joshua Tree, 29 March 2004)

File.Name: 4405d_4331-535.jpg
Notes: These Brown-eyed Primrose (Camissonia claviformis) flowers are in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. The pretty flowers don`t really close during the day, but they seem to freshen up at dusk. (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)

File.Name: 4405e_4331-416.jpg
Notes: What a surprise to find this brilliant Desert Paintbrush (Castilleja angustifolia) blooming in a dry desert wash. It's deceptive. The real flowers are the slender green tubes. All the red color comes from leafy bracts. (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)

File.Name: 4405f_4331-498.jpg
Notes: A rest stop on our school backpack trek across Joshua Tree National Park. Great country at just the right time for wildflowers. There are Joshua Trees, Yuccas, Nolinas, and many treasures in these rocks. (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)

File.Name: 4406a_4331-517.jpg
Notes: Some kind of Popcorn Flower (Plagiobothrys or Cryptantha in the Borage (Boraginaceae) family). The are many species that are very hard to identify. Here in the desert I don't really care! (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)

File.Name: 4406b_4331-453.jpg
Notes: It's not right to see just one flower of Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix glabrata) - these flowers were carpeting the desert floor as we approached Joshua Tree National Park last week, but they became scarce in the park itself at higher elevation. Maybe another week will make the difference. Most of these flowers are all yellow ray flowers, but a few have this magenta center that sure looks like disk flowers. It looks like the inner stamens/styles are bending over to protect the center. There's lots to learn about the most common flowers. (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)

File.Name: 4406c_4331-519.jpg
Notes: Silver Cholla (Opuntia echinocarpa) is the most gnarly cactus to bump into in this desert, but it sure is prety in the afternoon light. I think we all got "chollad" at least once on our school hike across Joshua Tree National Park last week. I carried pliers to remove the spines! (Joshua Tree, 1 April 2004)

File.Name: 4406d_4401-627.jpg
Notes: Beautiful seed pods of Dapple-pod Locoweed (Astragalus lentiginosus var. fremontii?) growing in a sandy wash. (Joshua Tree, 1 April 2004)

File.Name: 4406e_4328-024.jpg
Notes: Usually I take 10+ photos of every flower using a tripod and varying exposure. On our Joshua Tree hike I had to go handheld and conserve chip space and batteries, so I usually tried to do it in "one shot". This photo of Checker Fiddleneck (Amsinckia tessellata) isn't what I wanted, but it's dramatic! (Joshua Tree, 28 March 2004)

File.Name: 4406f_4330-398.jpg
Notes: I reckon I have to take at least one sunset photo while camping in Joshua Tree National Park. The plant is Mojave Yucca (Yucca schidigera). (Joshua Tree, 30 March 2004)

File.Name: 4409a_4331-465.jpg
Notes: I think this is a young Chuckawalla (Sauromalus obesus) lizard. They lose the spots and striped tail as they mature. The species name is appropriate. These potbellied lizards like to crawl into cracks in the rocks and inflate so you can't pull them out. We managed to tease this one out with a stick. Native Americans hunted these for food. (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004) Todays' photos are all critter shots from our Joshua Tree school hike a week ago. Time to get back to the woods at home, and I have a Spring Break vacation week to do it!

File.Name: 4409b_4331-490.jpg
Notes: You don't need to hear or see the rattle to recognize a rattlesnake! The triangular shape of the head and "horns" over the eyes is warning enough. My first thought was Sidewinder (Crotalus cerastes), but it's well over three feet long. Is this the notorious Mojave Green Rattlesnake (Crotalus scutulatus)? It never coiled to attack, though it did rattle. It climbed over the rock and edged-in sideways into a crack under another rock. I think that shrub is Wedgeleaf Goldenbush (Ericameria cuneata or E. cooperi). (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)
CommonNames: Sidewinder; Mojave Green Rattlesnake; Wedgeleaf Goldenbush
ScientificName: Crotalus cerastes; Crotalus scutulatus; Ericameria cuneata; E. cooperi
associatedTaxa: Crotalus cerastes; Crotalus scutulatus; Ericameria cuneata; E. cooperi

File.Name: 4409c_4331-540.jpg
Notes: This little scorpion (Scorpionida) decided to join us for our last meal on the trail. Local scorpians are not usually dangerous, but you won't forget the sting! We declined his invitation. (Joshua Tree, 31 March 2004)

File.Name: 4409d_4330-334.jpg
Notes: We saw lots of butterflies on our desert hike, but they sure are hard to identify away from home. Is this a checkerspot or fritillary? The delicate antenae and long proboscis are impressive. (Joshua Tree, 30 March 2004)

File.Name: 4409e_4328-018.jpg
Notes: Unknown Tent Caterpillar web (Lasiocampidae) in the desert. I don't know the host shrub. These colonial caterpillars give me the creeps. (Joshua Tree, 28 March 2004)

File.Name: 4409f_4401-668.jpg
Notes: Unknown desert caterpillar, maybe a Sphinx Moth? Compare my photo at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=314202. If you assumed this is the head, then its camouflage worked. This is the rear end of this photo-shy 3 inch long caterpillar as it tried to get away. (Joshua Tree, 1 April 2004)

File.Name: 4410h_4410-040.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] Brand new adult Cicada (Cicadidae) insect emerging from its skin. Our cicadas seem to click rather than sing. I haven't heard them yet, so maybe this is one of the first to emerge. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2004)

File.Name: 4410g_4410-031.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Orange Blackberry Rust on new shoots of California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) is caused by a fungus. (Maybe Arthuriomyces peckianus or Gymnoconia nitens?) The infected growth is weak and spindly, the leaves are small and too far apart and there are no spines. This is in our woods, but it's also a disease of commercial blackberries. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2004)

File.Name: 4410a_4409-189.jpg
Notes: Here's a lucky shot. The flower is Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha). I'm pretty sure that fuzzy insect hovering on the right is the right is one of the humpbacked Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) that cause the "oak apples" and other galls in our native oaks. (I have many photos of galls in the archives, eg (A HREF="http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=1236813">here and [previous].) These tiny wasps are weak fliers. The wind was blowing from left to right, and it's entirely possible this wasp is moving backwards! That's why the first oaks upwind of a field usually have the most galls. No, I didn't see it when I took the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2004)

File.Name: 4410b_4410-042.jpg
Notes: Brand new adult Cicada (Cicadidae) insect emerging from its skin. Our cicadas seem to click rather than sing. I haven't heard them yet, so maybe this is one of the first to emerge. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2004) Here's one of my favorite quotes: "And the day came when the risk it took to remain tight inside the bud was more painful than the risk it took to blossom." - Anais Nin

File.Name: 4410c_4410-026.jpg
Notes: First flower of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa). The stamens look like little lollipops! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2004)

File.Name: 4410d_4410-024.jpg
Notes: California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) flower and buds. I rarely find fruit on our native blackberries. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2004)

File.Name: 4410e_4409-212.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) by our mailbox. The real flowers are the slender red tubes with the curled stigma at the tip. Most of the color comes from leafy bracts. This species has glandular flowers but not below. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2004)

File.Name: 4410f_4405-013.jpg
Notes: Beware - the poppy fields of Oz are right outside the front door! These poppies are California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) with the disk below the flower and seed pod, planted at school. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 April 2004)

File.Name: 4411a_4411-081.jpg
Notes: This Fire Poppy (Papaver californicum) is the other "California Poppy" that only grows as a fire-follower after recent wildfire burns. I rarely find it even when I'm looking. It has nodding buds, crinkly petals, and a seed pod shaped like Papaver somniferum and kin. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2004) Todays' photos are all from a place that burned last summer in a small wildfire along West Camino Cielo Road near Lizard Mouth. The seeds of these fire-follower wildflowers can stay in the soil for a hundred years just waiting for a fire to clear the chaparral so they can sprout again and seed the soil for next time. It's a good Easter message of death and rebirth. Spring Break - wahoo! I have school vacation this week, and I already have many more good photos than I can post here! We have some local trips planned this week for more photos.

File.Name: 4411b_4411-063.jpg
Notes: I don't usually think of Ladybug Beetles (probably Hippodamia convergens?) as pollinators, but here is one doing the job for Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii, probably var. inezianus). The flower looks made for this beetle which is getting dusted with pollen while slurping the sweet nectar from the green gland at the base, like a car wash in reverse. Note how the flowers change in a slow dance between early stamens (above) and later pistils (below) ready. It is a fire-follower growing in a place that burned last year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2004)

File.Name: 4411c_4411-088.jpg
Notes: Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) really does have large flowers an inch or more across, lots of them. It's a fire-follower that sometimes shows up on road cuts and firebreaks. It is coming up everywhere in a place that burned last year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2004)

File.Name: 4411d_4411-066.jpg
Notes: Pretty flowers of Yellow-throated Phacelia (Phacelia brachyloba). Usually I never see this fire-follower, but they are coming up everywhere in a place that burned last year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2004)

File.Name: 4411e_4411-144.jpg
Notes: Delicate flowers of Whispering Bells (Emmenanthe penduliflora). It is a fire-follower growing in a place that burned last year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2004)

File.Name: 4411f_4411-095.jpg
Notes: Suncup (Cassisonia sp., maybe C. intermedia or C. hirtella?). It's in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae family, and the species are really hard to tell apart. The small yellow flowers have an "inferior ovary" below the flower that turns red and swells and twists with new seeds. It is a fire-follower growing in a place that burned last year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2004)
ScientificName: Cassisonia; C. intermedia; C. hirtella; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Cassisonia; C. intermedia; C. hirtella; Onagraceae

File.Name: 4412a_4412-228.jpg
Notes: This Southern Alligator Lizard (Elgaria multicarinatus) is not happy. They have a nasty disposition and a sepulchral face to match with those gnarly tooth-like scales along the upper jaw. I was walking along the river trying to keep my camera dry when I heard a squeek and a splash. When I looked over, this lizard was peering up over this rock. I didn't connect the two events at first. But after I took the photo I tried to tease the lizard up with a stick, and it dived into the water and swam down under a rock. I've never seen a lizard do this before! (Yes, I thought of that Simpsons episode with the bird-eating lizards!) (Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2004) Today's photos are all critter shots. What a treat to be on school vacation this week in the fullness of spring!

File.Name: 4412b_4412-181.jpg
Notes: Beautiful waxy flowers of Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata). But look closer. Can you spot the camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae?) poised to attack its prey? Look due left of the hollow center of the flower cluster. By now I reckon some other insect also failed to notice and is now dinner or host. "Nature red of tooth and claw" as Tennyson wrote. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2004)

File.Name: 4412c_4411-123.jpg
Notes: Ladybird Beetle (probably Hippodamia convergens?) working a mass of Popcorn Flowers (Cryptantha intermedia or kin, no purple dye in the leaves). But what is it looking for? The opening of those tiny flowers is much too narrow and deep for this Volkswagon-like beetle! This is a place that burned last year, see yesterday's photos in the archive. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2004)

File.Name: 4412d_4411-195.jpg
Notes: I believe these are Buprestid (Buprestidae, maybe genus Acmaeodera?) beetles gorging on the nectar and/or pollen of a Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) flower in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family. The beetles have beautiful metallic colors that glitter in the sunlight. They seem to get intoxicated by the mallow flowers and often curl up inside the blooms at night. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2004)

File.Name: 4412e_4412-225.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus, compare the [Previous] photo) butterfly working new flowers of Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium) near the river. This butterfly is looking threadbare and missing a tail. Do the adults overwinter, or is it already so worn by hard life? (Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2004)

File.Name: 4412f_4412-204.jpg
Notes: This Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) wasn't interested in flowers, but it really wanted to drink from the damp sand along the Santa Ynez River at Manzanita Lane and it let me get close. The markings are subtly different from the [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2004)

File.Name: 4413a_4413-272.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum). Everything about these native flowers is "over the top," especially the almost 2 inch long stamens and style! Large bees (and flies!) landing on the lower lip of the flower can bend them down to achieve pollination due to some interesting engineering with levers that Archimedes would appreciate. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2004)

File.Name: 4413b_4413-255.jpg
Notes: These developing seeds of Snowball or Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) remind me of hot crossed buns! There's also a geometry stumper here. The flowers have 5 petals and 5 sepals, but the fruit are arranged like the triangular number 6 = 1+2+3. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2004)

File.Name: 4413c_4413-276.jpg
Notes: Delicate flowers of Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides). They start with nodding buds, then small white flowers that turn pink with age. Alas, the common name for the genus is Farewell-to-Spring, and it already feels that way in our woods. This is a dry year and some late-season plants are already starting to appear. It`s an omen for summer. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2004)

File.Name: 4413d_4413-230.jpg
Notes: Insect galls on leaves of Squaw Bush (Rhus trilobata). It's a poison oak relative, but the middle leaf does not have a separate stalk and it has a distinctive smell. I believe these galls are caused by Leaf Gall Psyllid (Pachypsylla) insects. It's interesting how they cluster on only a few stems. (Mostly at the base of the plant?) (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2004)

File.Name: 4413e_4413-239.jpg
Notes: Looks like coral and crinoids, but it's not under the sea. The reddish flowers are Holly-leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) and the tassels are the silky seed heads of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha) which climbs over the shrubs to flower on top. It's not surprising that clematis was a native-American hair tonic! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2004)

File.Name: 4413f_4413-256.jpg
Notes: Flowers - LOTS of flowers - on Deerweed (Lotus scoparius). The small yellow pea-flowers turn reddish when they are done. This common subshrub starts with small leaves, but soon drops them and depends on its green stems for photosynthesis. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2004)

File.Name: 4414a_4412-162.jpg
Notes: This might be my very favorite native wildflower in our mountains - the shrub Snowdrop Bush or Storax (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus). The flowers are fragrant and beautiful and elegant and different. It's related to a species from southern Europe and the Middle East, and "gum storax" is an ancient commodity. It has very spotty distribution in our mountains and no other local relatives. I'm interested in these isolated single-species families and genera in our local flora with no obvious close relatives. Why are they here at all? (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2004) I'm posting early today. Julie and I are off early tomorrow to the Santa Barbara backcountry for a day or two, wahoo!

File.Name: 4414b_4411-151.jpg
Notes: Tiny beetle clambering over the flowers of native Islay or Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia). I can't count how many thousands of flowers there are on this single shrub. What a world to inhabit! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2004)

File.Name: 4414c_4412-184.jpg
Notes: Pretty flowers of Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum), aka Brodiaea for the squeamish. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2004)

File.Name: 4414d_4412-212.jpg
Notes: First flowers of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus). It seems early for these pretty flowers, but here they are. (Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2004)

File.Name: 4414e_4412-171.jpg
Notes: Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) is kind of weedy along trails and disturbed areas in our mounains, but the rose-related flowers (Rosaceae) have their charm. The wide stamens remind me of those fat matches that restaurants used to give out. Nature is often familiar, but there are many variations on those few themes! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2004)

File.Name: 4414f_4412-196.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius) growing on a sandy flat by the river. It's a classic hummingbird flower with no place to land. (Santa Ynez River, 12 April 2004)

File.Name: 4415a_4414-399.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Wallflowers (Erysimim capitatum or kin) framed by flowers of Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum). This was late in the day on our way home from the Carrizo Plain along the Pozo-La Panza road over the La Panza Range behind San Luis Obispo. It burned a few years ago. Dang, the Pozo Saloon was closed. There sure is a lot of country to explore during a one-day drive from home! (Coast Range, 14 April 2004)

File.Name: 4415b_4414-351.jpg
Notes: We were too late for the desert bloom in this dry year, but we still found surprises on our trip to the dry interior behind Santa Barbara County yesterday. I think of larkspur/delphinium as a plant of cool wet places like our north-slope canyon at home. But I found this Byron Larkspur (Delphinium recurvatum?) hiding under Iodine Bush (Allenrolfea occidentalis) on the alkali flats along the edge of Soda Lake. Ain't nature grand - and tough! (Carrizo Plain, 14 April 2004)

File.Name: 4415c_4414-356.jpg
Notes: Joined beetles make eye contact from separate tables. The large beetles are 1+ inch long. They have soft bodies and wing-covers shorter than the abdomen, so my first thought is Carrion or Burying Beetles (Silphidae, maybe Nicrophorus?). I believe the flowers are almost-finished Munz's Tidy-tips (Layia munzii) on the alkali flats around Soda Lake. (Carrizo Plain, 14 April 2004)
CommonNames: Carrion or Burying Beetles; Munz's Tidy-tips

File.Name: 4415d_4414-383.jpg
Notes: Is this feeding or fighting or foreplay? Maybe all three at once! These large (3/4 inch) Scarab or June Beetles (Scarabaeidae - maybe Paracotalpa?) look like giant ladybugs. There were lots of them frolicking on the flowers on the alkali flats, but they mostly had other things in mind. The flowrs might be Coreopsis callipsidea(?) with spreading outer phyllaries. (Carrizo Plain, 14 April 2004)

File.Name: 4415e_4414-363.jpg
Notes: I can't identify this small 1/2-inch butterfly, but it is probably a female Blue (Lycaenidae). There were lots of them working the fading flowers of Munz's Tidy-tips (Layia munzii?) on the alkali flats along the shore of Soda Lake. (Carrizo Plain, 14 April 2004)

File.Name: 4415f_4414-381.jpg
Notes: Alkali flats on the east end of Soda Lake in the Carrizo Plain National Monument. That's Soda Lake and the Caliente Range in the distance. What a harsh environment! This alien country is one mountain range beyond the east side of Santa Barbara County, but it feels like another world. Alas we got there too late for most wildflowers, though we did find a few in this most unpromising place on the edge of Soda Lake. The lake is completely dry in this dry year. Of course I tasted it - very salty, pleasant at first with a bitter aftertaste that I still notice. Pretty soon I had a white crust growing on my hands and camera lens. A nearby abandoned ranch had the name "KCL", so maybe it's mostly potassium chloride? Despite the alkali, there were flowers and life on the crusty soil! I believe the flowers in this photo are Alkali Goldfields (Lasthenia ferrisiae). The rest of today's photos (except the last) are nearby. (Carrizo Plain, 14 April 2004)

File.Name: 4416a_4414-314.jpg
Notes: Pretty puffed-up seed pods of Black Locoweed (Astragalus lentiginosus var. nigricalycis). I've read that this species is good feed for livestock, unlike some other species that give the genus its interesting name. These locoweeds are a large complex group! I think we found the same species in Joshua Tree earlier this spring, compare http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=7654655. (Carrizo Plain, 14 April 2004) Today`s photos are all from our Wednesday trip across the Carrizo Plain National Monument that is one mountain range beyond the eastern edge of Santa Barbara County. See http://www.ca.blm.gov/bakersfield/carrizoplain.html. It's interesting that there is NO Carrizo Grass (Phragmites australis) on the Carrizo Plain! We were too late for most wildflowers in this amazing landscape, but we managed to find a few. These interesting desert plants are all growing miles away from the alkaline Soda Lake in the center of the valley featured in yesterday`s photos.
Locality: Phragmites australis) on the Carrizo Plain! We were too late for most wildflowers in this amazing landscape

File.Name: 4416b_4414-327.jpg
Notes: Hoary Nightshade aka Blue Witch (Solanum umbelliferum var. incanum) has whitish stems covered with short branched hairs. I also find it closer to home on dry flats along the upper Santa Ynez River. (Carrizo Plain, 14 April 2004)

File.Name: 4416c_4414-305.jpg
Notes: Nettle Lupine (Lupinus hirsutissimus is covered with stiff spines, but it doesn't really sting like a nettle. This plant was only about 6 inches high in this dry year. They are more impressive after real rain. (Carrizo Plain, 14 April 2004)

File.Name: 4416d_4414-292.jpg
Notes: Mojave Sun Cup (Camissonia campestris) is an Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) relative with two orange dots at the base of each petal. This is not the Mojave Desert, but just over the Caliente Mountains on the eastern edge of Santa Barbara County, which shows how desert-like this country is. (Carrizo Plain, 14 April 2004)

File.Name: 4416e_4414-348.jpg
Notes: Furry insect galls on Fourwing Saltbush (Atriplex canescens). I believe these are caused by the Saltbush Gall Midge (Asphondylia atriplicis), a tiny fly in the Cecidomyiidae family of the order Diptera. The large white galls are an inch or more across. (Carrizo Plain, 14 April 2004)
ScientificName: Atriplex canescens; Asphondylia atriplicis; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Atriplex canescens; Asphondylia atriplicis; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: 4416f_4414-317.jpg
Notes: Seed pods of Diablo Locoweed aka milkvetch (Astragalus oxyphysus) lined up like troops. The large plant lives up to its name and is toxic to livestock, but we found it common in the dry fields of the Carizzo Plain. Ranchers try to control this native perennial, but maybe its more common now that this land is a National Monument? (Carrizo Plain, 14 April 2004)

File.Name: 4417a_4410-005.jpg
Notes: California Fremontia or Flannelbush (Fremontodendron californicum) is an uncommon native. This one is in our garden. The large sculpted flowers seem out of place in the chaparral. The plant is in the mostly tropical Cacao (Sterculiaceae) family, related to cola and chocolate. It's a stumper why this shrub and a few other natives with tropical connections are in our flora.
