(San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2004)

File.Name: 4417b_4415-404.jpg
Notes: We rescued this Blue Belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) from our cat. It posed for a few minutes and then ran off. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2004)
CommonNames: Blue Belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard

File.Name: 4417c_4413-288.jpg
Notes: Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius) covered with flowers. We have several shrubby lupines in the Santa Barbara area, but this is the only one that grows on the Pass close to home. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2004)

File.Name: 4417d_4411-115.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Wild Cucumber (Marah fabaceus). It's also called Manroot because it grows from a huge but not quite man-sized underground tuber. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2004)

File.Name: 4417e_4411-136.jpg
Notes: Large flowers of Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata). This is a climbing native vine that sometimes takes over after wildfires. Note the tiny insects - thrips? (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2004)

File.Name: 4417f_4411-142.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida). The plant is covered with long stiff hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2004)

File.Name: 4418a_4418-058.jpg
Notes: First flower in our canyon of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus). This blackberry-relative is common and almost weedy in the Sierras and Oregon, but it's scarce here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I rarely find the edible but thin fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2004)

File.Name: 4418b_4418-031.jpg
Notes: Pretty manzanita-like flowers of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) in our shady north-slope woodland in the Santa Barbara mountains. This shrub belongs in the Pacific Northwest. It`s not common here, and I rarely find fruit. I think it's remarkable that we also have desert plants like Great Basin Sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata) and tropical plants like Fremontia (Fremontodendron californicum) just a few miles away! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2004) Check the archives: Sagebrush - http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=831923 and Fremontia - http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=7689154
CommonNames: Wild Huckleberry; Great Basin Sagebrush; Fremontia
ScientificName: Vaccinium ovatum; Artemisia tridentata; Fremontodendron californicum
associatedTaxa: Vaccinium ovatum; Artemisia tridentata; Fremontodendron californicum

File.Name: 4418c_4418-044.jpg
Notes: Some wicked weeds are invasive, but others have already become part of the local flora and there's not much to do about it. How can you hate a plant with a name like Mouse-ear Chickweed (Cerastium glomeratum)? (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2004)

File.Name: 4418d_4418-038.jpg
Notes: A dry storm front passed over last night with lots of wind but none of the rain we need. There are drifts of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) catkins everywhere. This is the sheet-metal roof of our water tank. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2004)

File.Name: 4418e_4417-008.jpg
Notes: Ripe fruit of Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia) have a slippery coating with a pleasant acidic taste, but you should suck and spit - don't chew! This shrub always grows near the beach. In the mountains we have its cousin Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) that has folded "taco shell leaves" instead of flat leaves like this. (Santa Monica Mountains, 17 April 2004)

File.Name: 4418f_4417-004.jpg
Notes: Strange Giant Coreopsis (Coreopsis gigantea) is one of those rare plants that is really "rarely common". When you manage to find it, you find lots of it. It has large yellow composite flowers and carrot-like foliage on top of tree-like trunks. This plant is at Point Magu on the west end of the Santa Monica Mountains. We stopped for a few photos on the way to my parents' house yesterday. The wind was howling, and this was the best I could do. It also grows closer to home, eg at Surf Beach near Lompoc and west of Refugio. (Santa Monica Mountains, 17 April 2004)

File.Name: 4419a_2408-150.jpg
Notes: Oregon Fawn Lily (Erythronium oregonum - I think) is a very delicate lily in the Oregon woods. Other related species have interesting names like "Dog's-tooth Violet," "Trout Lily," and "Adder's Tongue." (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 8 April 2002) I'm busy with school, so today's photos are from an April trip to my parents' home in the Oregon woods two years ago to fix a plumbing problem. You can read the story at: http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/12apr02a.html

File.Name: 4419b_2404-070.jpg
Notes: Western Trillium or Wake-Robin (Trillium ovatum) fowers in the Oregon woods. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 April 2002)

File.Name: 4419c_2404-020.jpg
Notes: Fairy Slipper (Calypso bulbosa) orchid blooming in the Oregon woods. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 April 2002)

File.Name: 4419d_2405-185.jpg
Notes: Some kind of Stonecrop (Crassula) succulents and Polypody (Polypodium) ferns growing on a mossy outcrop in Oregon. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 April 2002)

File.Name: 4419e_2405-151.jpg
Notes: New needles on a twig of Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) in the Oregon woods. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 April 2002)

File.Name: 4419f_2405-130.jpg
Notes: Fertile stalks of Giant Horsetail (Equisetum telmateia ssp. braunii). (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 April 2002) I'm too busy with school, so today's photos are from an April trip to the Oregon woods two years ago. These are all wildflowers and plants I haven't found at home yet.

File.Name: 4423a_4423-270.jpg
Notes: These Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) wildflowers are in the Iris (Iridaceae) family. They may look plain at first compared to garden iris, but the closer you look the more charming they are. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 April 2004)

File.Name: 4423b_4423-273.jpg
Notes: Snug as a bug in a... I call it Farewell-to-Spring or Clarkia, but it's also known as Four Spot or Winecup Fairyfan. Either way, this native flower is Clarkia purpurea ssp. quadrivulnera. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 April 2004)
CommonNames: Farewell-to-Spring; Clarkia; Four Spot; Winecup Fairyfan

File.Name: 4423c_4423-261.jpg
Notes: Another Farewell-to-Spring known as Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica). Alas, it's already starting to feel like "farewell to spring" here in central California. It seems early this dry year. I had to pluck foxtails from my socks after my after-school hike today. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 April 2004)

File.Name: 4423d_4423-265.jpg
Notes: Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) flowers in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family. The stamens are fused into a central column around the female styles. Insects like this beetle love them. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 April 2004)

File.Name: 4423e_4423-229.jpg
Notes: California Fremontia or Flannelbush (Fremontodendron californicum) is an uncommon native with large yellow flowers that develop beautiful red-orange tones when they finish and shrivel up. This one is in the school garden. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 April 2004)

File.Name: 4423f_4423-277.jpg
Notes: California Maiden-Hair Fern (Adiantum jordanii) growing in the woodland. The black stems are so smooth you can wear them if you have pierced ears! (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 April 2004)

File.Name: 4424a_4424-050.jpg
Notes: Intimate tendrils of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2004)

File.Name: 4424b_4424-028.jpg
Notes: Lucky shot of a Skipper (Hesperidae) butterfly unrolling its tongue in the air about to penetrate a flower of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera). Work that lasso cowboy! Skippers look more like moths than butterflies, but they are active in daylight and have club-shaped antennae. There are many species. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2004)

File.Name: 4424c_4424-040.jpg
Notes: I found this dead but intact Broad-footed Mole aka California Mole (Scapanus latimanus) along my trail in the woods today. Everything about this critter is remarkable - the short front legs with broad hand-like feet, those amazing fingernails, the long naked sensitive snout, eyes wide shut, and a short nearly hairless tail. They really do have velvety mohair-like "moleskin" that lies down every way. Moles mainly feed on soil insects and worms, too bad they burrow those tunnels under our crops to find them! The yellow spots on the face are hatching fly eggs, and there are other long-stalked eggs on his forehead. I guess we'll never know why this mole was dead on my trail, but nature is doing her stuff to remove it. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2004)

File.Name: 4424d_4424-006.jpg
Notes: These interesting galls on the petioles of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) are not in my book (Ronald A. Russo, "Plant Galls of the California Region", 1979). Those two single flowers in the axil are startling, especially since the normal large flower clusters are all finished. "Genetic modification" is nothing new. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2004)

File.Name: 4424e_4424-021.jpg
Notes: Bright orange uredia of a fissured stem gall on Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea). It's caused by the Coyote Bush Rust Gall Fungus (Puccinia evadens) rather than an insect, though I've read there is a Cecidomyiid midge whose larvae only feed on the orange spores of this gall fungus. Branches beyond the gall usually die, but the galls often sprout short-lived thickets of "witches' broom" growth. "GM crops" are nothing new for these critters! (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2004)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Coyote Bush Rust Gall Fungus
ScientificName: uredia; Baccharis pilularis; consanguinea; Puccinia evadens; Cecidomyiid
associatedTaxa: uredia; Baccharis pilularis; consanguinea; Puccinia evadens; Cecidomyiid

File.Name: 4424f_4424-059.jpg
Notes: Darn, I didn't manage to capture the caterpillar inside this leaf, but I did get his spoor! The plant is the unusual California Tea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes) perennial in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. It's getting ready to flower. The caterpillar inside folded this leaflet over and is now fastening down the edges to make a cozy shelter inside. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2004)

File.Name: 4425a_4425-194.jpg
Notes: Tendrils of Dodder (probably Cuscuta californica?) enticing a flower of California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica?). (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2004) Dodder is a parasitic plant with orange stems and no leaves or roots that sucks life from its host plant. It's related to Morning Glory, but it's now placed in its own family (Cuscutaceae). It's starting to spread over the fire-followers in an place in our mountains along West Camino Cielo Road that burned last year. Pretty, but creepy. I wouldn't camp next to it. I think of the movie Invasion of the Body Snatchers. It sure was hot hiking on that fire-black soil today! This photo is softened with a drop of sweat on my camera lens. It's a sweat-filter, nothing digital about it! As I write this, there's a new wildfire near where I took today's photos. Highway 154 is closed. It's very dry already and I'm worried about summer.
Locality: Cuscutaceae). It's starting to spread over the fire-followers in an place in our mountains along West Camino Cielo Road that burned last year. Pretty
ScientificName: Cuscuta californica; Rafinesquia californica; Cuscutaceae; Invasion of the Body Snatchers
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta californica; Rafinesquia californica; Cuscutaceae; Invasion of the Body Snatchers

File.Name: 4425b_4425-182.jpg
Notes: Flowers of Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca). This is an unusual native poppy and bleeding-heart relative that only grows after wildfires. It's a perennial with poppy-like leaves and flower stems taller than I am. I see things in the flower cluster: praying, dancing, diving... This unusual plant is a fire-follower. The remarkable thing for me is that it disappears after a few years in one place to wait for another fire with seeds in the ground. I've found it for the last few years on an old burn east of my home. This year there are none. But I found this single plant on a recent burn west of my home. How do they know? How long can they wait? (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2004)

File.Name: 4425c_4425-113.jpg
Notes: I spotted this beautiful lizard on a rock, but he ran away when I approached with my camera. I sat and waited for ten minutes and he came back and posed for me. Maybe that's the fourth rule, Sandman - sit and wait! Is it a Blue Belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis)? He is almost black with brilliant colored scales on the sides. It sure isn't typical. Compare my photo at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=7689112. The dewlap throat pouch is impressive, but he lost it later when he relaxed. He was finishing an angry push-up in this photo. Is this a T-Rex thing to build up the forearms? I took this photo in a place in our mountains that burned last year. Maybe this lizard became so black to match the burned soil color? I sure don't understand all the other colors! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2004)
CommonNames: Blue Belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard

File.Name: 4425d_4425-089.jpg
Notes: Unknown caterpillar on a flower stalk of Tauschia arguta. It's sometimes called Southern Umbrellawort, but what a terrible name for this beautiful native plant in the Carrot (Apiaceae) family! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2004) Update - Thanks for pointing me in the right direction, madoak. I'm pretty sure this is an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon). I'm glad they have the good sense to visit the natives instead of the horrible invasive weed fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) that many locals call anise. Both plants are in the Carrot (Apiaceae) family which this butterfly prefers. I think it's time for a common name change! (As well as a "natural regime" change - fennel is taking over large parts of California.) I have a photo of the adult butterfly at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=769528
CommonNames: Southern Umbrellawort; Carrot; Anise Swallowtail; fennel; Carrot
ScientificName: Tauschia arguta; Apiaceae; Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Tauschia arguta; Apiaceae; Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: 4425e_4425-086.jpg
Notes: This pretty flower of Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) has a lethal trap for the unwary. That's some kind of Crab Spider (Thomisidae?) poised to attack. The spider is not as well camouflaged as some others I find. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2004)

File.Name: 4425f_4425-170.jpg
Notes: I use "Mountain Dandelion" as my generic name for these beautiful dandelion-like seed heads that only appear after the flowers are done so I can't collect them to identify. Maybe it's a Malacothrix species? (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2004)

File.Name: 4426a_4425-109.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida). This is a head-high shrub that usually has brilliant poppy flowers all summer long. It should be a garden shrub, but it favors hot rocky places so maybe it doesn't take to domestication? My Olympus digcam usually has trouble with bright yellow, but this photo is just right. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2004)

File.Name: 4426b_4425-147.jpg
Notes: This is another kind of California Poppy aka Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa). Look close and see that it doesn't have a disk at the base of the seed pod like the official California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica), compare the [Previous] photo. I find this always-native species on dry rocky slopes and recent burns like this. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2004)
CommonNames: California Poppy; Tufted Poppy; California Poppy

File.Name: 4426c_4424-003.jpg
Notes: This is the usual orange California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica), planted at school. It has that large flat disk below the flower and seed pod. These perennial poppies have been so widely sown from seed packets and Caltrans that it's hard to tell what's native any more. There are other native California poppies that don't have the disk, see the [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 April 2004)

File.Name: 4426d_4425-104.jpg
Notes: Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) really does have large flowers an inch or more across, lots of them. It`s a fire-follower that sometimes also shows up on road cuts and firebreaks. You can see the sticky glandular hairs that cover this plant. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2004)

File.Name: 4426e_4425-164.jpg
Notes: New peanut-sized flowers of Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina). These head-high shrubs have a very strong sage smell that is not quite pleasant. They favor burns and disturbed places - often the easiest trail. But bushwacking through a stand of them is almost too much. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2004)

File.Name: 4426f_4425-155.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) growing from a mass of parasitic Dodder (probably Cuscuta californica?). See the [Previous] photo. I was looking for a morning-glory flower ensnared with dodder, but I couldn't find one. D'oh, these flowers grow too fast! This native species has enlarged calyx-like bracts at the base of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2004)

File.Name: 4430a_4429-337.jpg
Notes: I'll grant you three wishes if you let me go! *Uh* Get a photo? Have a fun after-school Hike Club with my school kids at the river? Nobody gets hurt? I guess I got my wishes, and so did this crawdad we caught in the river yesterday! (Santa Ynez River, 29 April 2004)

File.Name: 4430b_4429-361.jpg
Notes: Beautiful flower of Farewell-to-Spring (Clarkia unguiculata) has petals with a prominent blade and a narrow claw at the base. It really does feel like farewell-to-spring after the 100+ degree heat earlier this week. (Santa Ynez River, 29 April 2004)

File.Name: 4430c_4429-358.jpg
Notes: Beetle larvae on Farewell-to-Spring (Clarkia unguiculata). See [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez River, 29 April 2004)

File.Name: 4430d_4430-422.jpg
Notes: Some kind of Spittle Bug (Aprophoridae?) makes these "loogies" on branches of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea). The nymphs live inside and get some protection. I know I wouldn't want to eat it to get the nugget inside! These "spitballs" were common along our school bike ride to Lompoc today, but I never see them on this common shrub at home. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 April 2004)

File.Name: 4430e_4430-432.jpg
Notes: New seeds of Box Elder (Acer negundo var. californicum). It's a maple-relative that grows along the Santa Ynez River below Buellton. Why doesn't it grow further upstream? (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 April 2004)

File.Name: 4430f_4430-427.jpg
Notes: I took my after-school Hike Club kids to explore the river on Thursday. The car in front of us stopped and backed up to run over this harmless and beneficial Gopher Snake (Pituophis melanoleucus) that was basking on the road. I showed great retstraint. We talked, we collected the snake, and we skinned it out. This is a close photo of the fresh skin and meat after we finished. What would you do with a van full of school kids in this situation?? (Santa Ynez Valley, 29 April 2004)

File.Name: 4501a_4501-013.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) aren`t very colorful, but they have great geometry. This photo shows the typical "scorpioid inflorescence" of all Phacelia species in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family - curled up like a scorpion`s tail or a fiddle neck. Can you spot the aphid on the right edge? (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2004) I'm busy with school this weekend - and month. Today's photos are all from the dry side of our garden at home. They each feature an insect critter *somewhere*! I started this f'log in June 2003. It's not quite "a year in the woods" yet, but I'm starting find some of the same wildflowers. Compare http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=346545.

File.Name: 4501b_4501-037.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2004)

File.Name: 4501c_4501-027.jpg
Notes: A wild Bee or Bumblebee (Apidae) hanging on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2004)

File.Name: 4501d_4501-042.jpg
Notes: Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae) hanging from its web. They engineer beautiful webs in the blackberries and other understory shrubs in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2004)

File.Name: 4501e_4501-051.jpg
Notes: Beetles love these native Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) flowers in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family. I often find these little spotted beetles curled up inside the flowers when they open in the morning. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2004)

File.Name: 4501f_4501-054.jpg
Notes: The first flower I've found of the well-named Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus), one of my favorite wildflowers that's just starting to flower at home. It's an upside-down Mariposa Lily that never opens more than this. Look at the bud in the upper-left, and see that some critter has made a hole to get inside to the nectar glands inside. You can see the outline of one of the glands between the sepals of the mature flower on the right. The petals are hairy inside, making a little trap to keep insects inside once they get there. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2004)

File.Name: 4502a_4502-105.jpg
Notes: A whorl of flowers of Wood Mint (Stachys bullata). I've been trying to get a keeper photo of this common woodland sage-relative, but it's hard to capture the porcelain-like quality of the petals. I like this one. Then I noticed the long-legged spider hiding under the backlit leaf on the left. Beautiful nature holds deadly traps for the unwary! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2004) Today's photos are more critter shots. It's hard to find a flower without critters at this time of year!

File.Name: 4502b_4502-063.jpg
Notes: Checkerspot butterfly - maybe the Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona) or kin? It is working Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). I love the golden antenna knobs! It seems to be a weak flier. I saw another one flutter past me backwards in the wind. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2004)
CommonNames: Checkerspot butterfly; Chalcedon Checkerspot; Canyon Sunflower

File.Name: 4502c_4502-068.jpg
Notes: A pretty Hairstreak (Lycaenidae) butterfly resting on a new flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). I photograph these butterflies at rest with wings up, but my butterfly books only show the top side of the wings as they look dead and pinned in a collection. I'd rather just collect their light and leave them to flutter by again. Maybe this is a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californicum)? (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2004)

File.Name: 4502d_4502-091.jpg
Notes: It's a month too early for these flowers of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica), but here they are. Look close to see insect and spider drama among the flowers! Last year I photographed goldenrod in July! It's as dry as I can remember for this time of year. Is this early flowering an omen for a nasty summer? (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2004)

File.Name: 4502e_4502-096.jpg
Notes: I think I saw orange wings when this Grasshopper (Acrididae) flew into the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree right in front of me. The bright spots on the oak leaves are the characteristic "stellate hairs" that look like little starfish with a hand lens. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2004)

File.Name: 4502f_4502-065.jpg
Notes: Some kind of Dusky-wing Skipper working a composite flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). Skippers are in-between butterflies and moths. They have a moth-like appearance, but they fly during the day and have knobby antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2004)

File.Name: 4503a_4502-133.jpg
Notes: There's a fire in the backcountry today, see the [Previous] photo. The wind blew the smoke towards the ocean between my house and the setting sun. I only had a few minutes when I noticed the light, but I managed to grab my camera and run outside for this photo on the dry side of our garden. This is Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) with reddish sunset fire light. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2004) Seeing the wildfire driving home, I suddenly thought about computer backups. I've been burning CDs ever since and still have more to do!

File.Name: 4503b_4502-118.jpg
Notes: Fire on the mountain in May - yipes! This was my view driving home from school at 3:30 today. It looks unstoppable climbing up the San Rafael Mountains that border the backcountry to the right, but by sunset this first big wildfire of the season seems to be over. Whatever the fire crews did, thanks! This photo is looking across the upper end of Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2004) There's a stumper here that is the stuff of myth. Why does the smoke at the bottom becomes a cumulus cloud at the top of the plume? The answer is that fire - all combustion - produces water vapor (and CO2) as a by-product. Hold a glass full of ice over a candle flame and you can see for yourself that water drops condense on the outside. On a bigger scale, wildfires make thunderstorms. Fire and water are yin and yang! Here's another fire stumper: why does this fire plume look like a "mushroom cloud"?
Locality: and CO2) as a by-product.  Hold a glass full of ice over a candle flame and you can see for yourself that water drops condense on the outside.  On a bigger scale

File.Name: 4503c_4503-154.jpg
Notes: Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona or kin) butterfly drinking deep from a flower of Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium). Life is good! The leaves of this fine shrub make tea, incense, and a smoke that lives up to its name. We went to the river this afternoon for a late swim. The "Adventure Pass" guardians were still on duty, but we just said no. You should too. (Santa Ynez River, 2 May 2004)

File.Name: 4503d_4503-143.jpg
Notes: You have to squint at the screen to see it, but this butterfly has TWO tongues! I reckon it is one of the many subspecies of the Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona). I have another photo two clicks back in my f'log, but these butterflies near our swimming hole at the Santa Ynez River this evening have more black above and brighter colors below than those at home in the mountains. This one is geting a drink - two drinks! - from the gravel near the water. (Santa Ynez River, 2 May 2004)

File.Name: 4504a_4502-108.jpg
Notes: Flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). It`s a shrubby composite with daisy-like flowers that is very common near home, almost a weed. Check my [Previous] photos to see how important this plant is for local insects. I find it hard to photograph with my Olympus digital camera which gives the bright yellow flowers a greenish-cast I can`t get rid of. But this backlit flower is just right. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2004)

File.Name: 4504b_4501-058.jpg
Notes: We inherited a collection of native Stonecrop (Crassulaceae) succulents from a friend a few years ago. They are doing well in a rocky part of our garden, but we lost the name tags. Julie thinks this might be from San Miguel Island, so maybe it`s Dudleya candelabrum or D. greenei? Whatever it is, the flowers sure are pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2004)

File.Name: 4504c_4502-099.jpg
Notes: New flowers of California Tea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes). It's a perennial in the Pea (Fabaceae) family that is not common in our mountains. It's easy to walk past this perennial without noticing, but the flowers are beautiful when you look close. Interesting common name. I've never tried to make tea from this plant, but it does have a fragrance. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2004)

File.Name: 4504d_4502-089.jpg
Notes: Of course I like to photograph the big showy flowers like poppies and lupines, but my favorites are the tiny wildflowers that we rarely notice. This is an old flower (turned magenta) of Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides). They start with nodding buds, then small white flowers that turn magenta with age like this. I thought it was common Willow-herb (Epilobium sp.) when I took the photo. This delicate flower is pencil-eraser-size, and I had to touch it with my finger for my digital camera to get focus away from the background. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2004)

File.Name: 4504e_4501-028.jpg
Notes: Shiny new bronze-colored leaves of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree near home. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2004)

File.Name: 4504f_4502-109.jpg
Notes: Pretty little flowers of Nuttall's Snowberry aka Tripweed (Symphoricarpos mollis), a common understory plant in the woods at home. It lives up to its second name with thin but tough stems that grow across my trails. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2004)

File.Name: 4505a_4501-019.jpg
Notes: Looking close at a Flanders Field Poppy aka Shirley Poppy or Field Poppy (Papaver rhoeas) in our garden at home. It looks like a frosted muffin sitting in the middle! Today's photos are all garden shots from the weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2004)
CommonNames: Flanders Field Poppy; Shirley Poppy; Field Poppy

File.Name: 4505b_4501-010.jpg
Notes: Some kind of Euphorbia flowering in our garden. Yes, those really are flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2004)

File.Name: 4505c_4501-007.jpg
Notes: This little spider has spun a deadly trap inside an Iris flower in our garden. Julie calls it a "Dutch Iris" which is good enough for me. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2004)

File.Name: 4507a_4507-500.jpg
Notes: This is one of those lucky shots you get when you stay in one place and take LOTS of photos hoping to get a keeper! This is a small Fly (Diptera) coming in for a landing on the new flowers of Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in our garden. There are so many kinds of flies that you need a microscope to distinguish! This fly is in the Nematocera suborder that includes Mosquitos and Crane Flies. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2004)

File.Name: 4507b_4507-505.jpg
Notes: Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) flowers living up to their name. This relative of Mariposa Lily is probably my favorite native wildflower. It's doing well on the dry edge of our garden, but I don't think the flowers will last long in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2004)

File.Name: 4507c_4507-509.jpg
Notes: New berries of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum). This plant is in the dry part of our garden, but it does get some extra water. In this drought year, even this plant is producing only a few fruit. It will be a tough summer for wildlife; and the rest of us. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2004)

File.Name: 4507d_4507-494.jpg
Notes: This Hairstreak (Lycaenidae) butterfly is working the new flowers of Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2004)

File.Name: 4507e_4507-499.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working the new flowers of Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2004)

File.Name: 4507f_4506-253.jpg
Notes: This is one of the many subspecies of the Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona) that are very common in our mountains and garden right now. I know it's fuzzy, but this lucky shot solves a butterfly stumper! I posted a photo a few days ago that seems to show one of these butterflies with TWO tongues: see http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=7743910. Here you can see another butterfly zipping its bifurcated tongue together to get the good stuff from a perennial Daisy (Asteraceae) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2004)

File.Name: 4508a_4508-030.jpg
Notes: I believe this is a West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly getting ready to work a composite flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our garden. That amazing coiled tongue! The under-wings aren't really this green, they must be picking up some color reflected from the flower. The patterns remind me of the inside-covers of old books. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2004) I'm busy for the next few weeks with school stuff, including making my school slide show. No time for hikes, but it's wonderful what you can find in the garden just outside the door!

File.Name: 4508b_4508-024.jpg
Notes: I don't know what kind of Checkerspot Butterfly (Nymphalidae) this is on the native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our garden. It's like a flower on a flower! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2004)

File.Name: 4508c_4508-027.jpg
Notes: A different kind of Checkerspot Butterfly (Nymphalidae) on Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our garden. It's hard (for me at least) to identify these species without killing them. No thanks. It's pretty anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2004)

File.Name: 4508d_4508-022.jpg
Notes: Large seedhead of Mountain Dandelion (Agoseris heterophylla). The dandelion-like seeds turn up everywhere! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2004)

File.Name: 4508e_4508-012.jpg
Notes: Another Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona or kin) drinking deep from a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) on the edge of our garden. These beautiful butterflies are rather tame, and they let me get close while they feed. Most butterflies aren't so obliging! (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2004)

File.Name: 4508f_4508-006.jpg
Notes: Some kind of Skipper (Hesperidae) working a tubular flower of Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in our garden. Skippers are in-between butterflies and moths. They have a moth-like appearance, but they fly during the day and have knobby antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2004)

File.Name: 4509a_4509-036.jpg
Notes:
This must be Butterfly Day as well as Mother's Day! I hope all these butterfly moms and dads will make lots of new butterflies for next year! (Yes, I called my Mom!)

Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working the flowers of this non-native Sage (Salvia sp.) in our garden. Maybe it is Greek Sage (Salvia fructicosa)? You can almost see "fairy dust" on the wings! I reckon we just had a big hatch/emergence of these butterflies in our canyon. There were almost a dozen of these large butterflies working the garden flowers this morning. It's wonderful how they hang upside down from the flowers by the two tiny claws on each foot. I wonder if they use their (slight) weight on the lower limb of the flower to open up the throat for entry? (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2004)

File.Name: 4509b_4509-050.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working some non-native Sage (Salvia sp.) flowers in our garden. It's getting its proboscis ready for penetration. You bet it's sexy! Note how the tongue is made of two strands zipped together, you can see the crease. What a perfect 3D-spline-like curve that proboscis makes searching for entry to the flower. I reckon this is hydraulics rather than muscles. How do these critters have such control of that amazing organ? (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2004)

File.Name: 4509c_4509-047.jpg
Notes: I got this Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly two-fer photo today! There were almost a dozen of these beautiful large butterflies working the garden flowers this morning. Every once in a while, a pair of them would go into a dance for love or territory and spin around like a whirlwind, but I couldn't get the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2004)

File.Name: 4509d_4509-079.jpg
Notes: This Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly is using a trick to get nectar from the non-native Sage (Salvia sp.) flowers in our garden. Look close to see that its long proboscis/tongue is extended OVER the flower rather than through it, entering directly at the calyx to get right to the good stuff. Honey Bees and Carpenter Bees have short tongues, and they always cheat the flowers this way to get nectar without pollination. I'm surprised to find this butterfly using the same trick. All my other Swallowtail photos today show the proboscis entering through the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2004)

File.Name: 4509e_4509-069.jpg
Notes: This true Bug (Hemiptera) on the foreground flower seems to be watching the Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working the flowers on a Sage (Salvia sp.) in our garden. I imagine that`s like kayaking among whales. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2004)

File.Name: 4509f_4509-031.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working non-native Sage (Salvia sp.) flowers in our garden. Maybe this is Greek Sage (Salvia fructicosa)? I reckon we just had a big hatch/emergence of these butterflies in our canyon. There were almost a dozen of these large butterflies working the garden flowers this morning! (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2004)

File.Name: 4516a_4516-022.jpg
Notes: Poppy (Papaver somniferum) in the garden. What other plant has relieved - and caused - so much pain? (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2004) Too many photos! I'm putting my school slide show together, and I hardly got past the door this weekend. I'm totally distracted trying to sequence 3000+ photos (and music) down to half that size. I took a break and got a few photos in the garden. Just what I need - even more photos!

File.Name: 4516b_4516-012.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly hanging onto a Fuschia in our garden while it cheats by slipping in under the calyx rather than going past the official opening with stamens and style. It looks like hanging on to a big balloon! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2004)

File.Name: 4516c_4516-015.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly unrolling its tongue to work the Fuschia in our garden. Note hat it is cheating by going in under the calyx rather than through the official opening with stamens and style. Hmmm, where does it unroll that tongue from?? (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2004)

File.Name: 4516d_4516-004.jpg
Notes: This small cactus in our garden has flowers nearly as big as the plant! (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2004)

File.Name: 4516e_4516-019.jpg
Notes: A cactus (Opuntia) flower in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2004)

File.Name: 4516f_4516-021.jpg
Notes: A pair of Buprestid (Buprestidae, maybe genus Acmaeodera?) beetles engaged with food AND sex on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2004)

File.Name: 4604a_4527-328.jpg
Notes: These unusual flowers of Yerba Mansa (Anemopsis californica) in the Lizard's-Tail family (Saururaceae) look like a giant plantain. They are growing in a boggy pasture along the creek that feeds Diaz Lake near the town of Lone Pine on the eastern side of the High Sierras. The white "petals" are really petal-like bracts. This plant can cover acres of marshy land. It has a wonderful minty fragrance when you walk through it, and it has ancient medicinal uses. It grows wild in Santa Barbara near a few Chumash native American sites, so I suspect it was cultivated here. (Lone Pine, 27 May 2004) I'm almost done with my "busy time" at school. Todays photos are all from my school trip last week to the desert with our 8th grade graduating class. I finished the DMS slide show on Wednesday with 1,917 photos in 22 segments with music, sequenced into video using Final Cut Pro, 1 hour and 6 minutes. I wish I could show it here. Graduation was today. Tomorrow I plan to go for a walk in our woods at home and take a few new photos to post. Yahoo, it's summer!

File.Name: 4604b_4525-073.jpg
Notes: Flower of Desert Rose Rosa woodsii var. ultramontana) growing along the creek by our cabin in the desert. (Lone Pine, 25 May 2004)

File.Name: 4604c_4527-273.jpg
Notes: Tiny buckwheat flowers of Desert Trumpet (Eriogonum inflatum) seem to float in the air. (Lone Pine, 27 May 2004)

File.Name: 4604d_4527-284.jpg
Notes: Developing seeds of Desert Allysum aka Bush Peppergrass (Lepidium fremontii). This small shrub is usually covered with white mustard flowers at this time of year, but it seems that everything is done early in this drought year in California. (Lone Pine, 27 May 2004)

File.Name: 4604e_4527-276.jpg
Notes: Flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum var. polifolium). This is a desert variety of a shrub we also have at home. (Lone Pine, 27 May 2004)

File.Name: 4604f_4527-285.jpg
Notes: Large tumor-like insect galls engulfing a stem of fragrant Great Basin Sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata). I believe these are caused by Gall Midge flies Cecidomyiidae. All of today's photos are from the Alabama Hills on the eastern side of the Sierra Nevada Mountains at the base of Mt. Whitney. (Lone Pine, 27 May 2004)

File.Name: 4605a_4605-056.jpg
Notes: I caught this Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis), aka a Bumblebee or Hummingbird Moth for good reasons, working the flowers of Cleveland Sage (Salvia clevelandii) in our garden this morning. It's not just a lucky shot - I chased this critter all morning taking dozens of fuzzy photos to delete. It hovers like a hummingbird and never stands still. It's an odd moth with clear wings, that works the day shift, hovers like a hummer, and looks like a huge bee! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2004)

File.Name: 4605b_4605-026.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) working flowers of Cleveland Sage (Salvia clevelandii). This shrub is a California native near San Diego. This one is in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2004)

File.Name: 4605c_4605-051.jpg
Notes: Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona or kin) on a composite flower (maybe Geum?) in our garden. Look close to spot the yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae?) near the butterfly's left front foot. The spider was completely hidden between the florets of the flower. Several times I watched it run out at the butterfly, think twice about the size of the beast, then scurry back to hiding. Mud Dauber (Sphecidae) wasps that hunt spiders were also on the prowl. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2004)
CommonNames: Chalcedon Checkerspot; Crab Spider; Mud Dauber

File.Name: 4605d_4605-065.jpg
Notes: Large quarter-size Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) hovering over exuberant flowers of White Sage (Salvia apiana) in our garden. These large bees drill neat holes in the fascia boards under our eaves. They have my blessing. The sage flowers look like little dragons with male stamen hands up in the air. That other extended smooth part is the female pistil. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2004)

File.Name: 4605e_4605-069.jpg
Notes: Immature Long-Horned Grasshopper (Tettigoniidae) - maybe a katydid? - in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2004)

File.Name: 4605f_4605-034.jpg
Notes: Looks like Dorothy caught in the tornado to Oz! It's really seeds of Mountain Dandelion (Agoseris heterophylla) caught still in a spider web. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2004)

File.Name: 4606a_4520-086.jpg
Notes: Looking close at a Flanders Field Poppy aka Shirley Poppy or Field Poppy (Papaver rhoeas) in our garden at home, from the back with the sun behind. This flower became a symbol for the D-Day action on the beaches of Normandy 60 years ago today. My Dad was there. It's a good night to listen to "Where Have All The Flowers Gone" - Pete Seeger; Peter, Paul and Mary; Marlene Dietrich; lots more. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2004)
CommonNames: Flanders Field Poppy; Shirley Poppy; Field Poppy

File.Name: 4606b_4520-090.jpg
Notes: Looking close at a Flanders Field Poppy aka Shirley Poppy or Field Poppy (Papaver rhoeas) in our garden at home. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2004)
CommonNames: Flanders Field Poppy; Shirley Poppy; Field Poppy

File.Name: 4606c_4605-061.jpg
Notes: Seedpod of Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum) in the garden, like a world with a crown. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2004)

File.Name: 4606d_4605-023.jpg
Notes: Pretty flowers of Farewell-to-Spring aka Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica) on the dry side of our garden at home. I don't need help to see that spring is done. It's very dry this year and even our usual summer flowers are finishing early. Note that the stamens are arranged in two series. The inner four stamens have yellow pollen and are already open. The outer four stamens have blue pollen and thicker stalks and are just starting to open. The closer I look at our native flowers, the more questions I find. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2004)

File.Name: 4606e_4509-088.jpg
Notes: Large flowers of native Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri?) open at night, but they get the blahs in the morning, only to shrivel away with the rising sun. Big sphinx moths should carry pollen at night, but this early flower in our garden looks barren. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2004)

File.Name: 4606f_4606-076.jpg
Notes: Another Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona or kin) butterfly on a composite flower (maybe Geum?) in our garden, with a frustrated yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae?) just left of the butterfly's front right foot. Now that I've noticed these spiders that hide within the composite flowers, I see them everywhere. I can sometimes draw them out by tapping with a stick. See yesterday's photo at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=7876090. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2004)

File.Name: 4608g_4606-100.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters/?pid=7939895]Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona or kin) butterfly resting on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2004)

File.Name: 4608a_4606-081.jpg
Notes: The first Dahlia flower in our garden, looking from the back towards the sun. I love the "mood indigo" - almost black - phyllaries at the base of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2004)

File.Name: 4608b_4608-095.jpg
Notes: Elegant flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) are starting to bloom in the canyon. The uppermost leaves on the stem are fused to encircle the stem, you can barely see them in the upper left. The leaves are puberulent, but the stamens are hispid. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2004)

File.Name: 4608c_4608-101.jpg
Notes: Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona or kin) butterfly resting on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our canyon. It was a foggy morning and the sun was just breaking through at noon. I reckon it was sitting with wings spread like solar collectors trying to warm up. Look how it's propping it's wing up on the leaf on the left. Beautiful clown colors and speckled eyes, note the coiled up tongue. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2004)

File.Name: 4608d_4605-042.jpg
Notes: Dense-flowered Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) is an uncommon native orchid in our summer-dry woods. Note how the ovary twists one-half turn to present the flower upside down. It's always exciting to find this flower, especially in a drought year like this. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2004)

File.Name: 4608e_4606-037.jpg
Notes: Barely recognizable composite flowers of Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum) have a sweet maple-syrup scent. You can see the stamens/pistils of a few disk flowers poking up from the yellow centers of the dry flower heads. They make nice dry bouquets, but after a while the flowers always fall apart and make a mess. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2004)

File.Name: 4608f_4606-083.jpg
Notes: Horsetail aka Smooth Scouring Rush (Equisetum hyemale ssp. affine) in the little wetland (actually a well-disguised 4'x4' fiberglass tub) that catches the overflow from our hot/cold tub. The stems are in sections that fit together with these neat little crowns on the joints. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2004)

File.Name: 4609a_4609-153.jpg
Notes: Looking inside a flower of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in our canyon. Most of our native lilies have been attacked by parasites this year, but a few plants have survived to flower. Everything is happening early in this drought year in central California. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2004)

File.Name: 4609b_4609-152.jpg
Notes: First flower of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in our canyon. Most of our native lilies have been attacked by parasites this year, but a few plants have survived to flower. I'll photograph these beautiful flowers every day I can! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2004)

File.Name: 4609c_4609-123.jpg
Notes: Unknown butterfly that I can't quite match in my books - maybe an Edith's Checkerspot (Occidryas editha) or kin? It's resting on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) near the creek. Several of these butterflies were working the composite flowers, but it was hard to catch them at rest. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2004)

File.Name: 4609d_4609-127.jpg
Notes: I think this a some kind of Blue (Lycaenidae, subfamily Polyommatinae) butterfly. It was active, but I caught it resting on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our canyon. I love the striped antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2004)

File.Name: 4609e_4609-134.jpg
Notes: It's not all peace and love in nature. Here are two Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona or kin) butterflies going at it, sumo style, to see who gets this flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our canyon. I could hear their wings clashing. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2004)

File.Name: 4609f_4609-118.jpg
Notes: D`oh - this Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly was working a classic cheat on the Fuschia flowers in our garden, trying to go under the calyx straight to the nectar rather than through the throat of the flower where pollination might happen. Look at the very top of the flower to see how its tongue got turned away! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2004)

File.Name: 4610g_4610-236.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters/?pid=7949305]Very strange insect working the flowers of a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub. I reckon it's an Ichneumon Wasp (Ichneumonidae). It's thin as a needle, but over an inch long. It's holding its abdomen up at a jaunty angle, and what is that "thing" sticking out behind?? (Yipes - South Park / Eric Cartman thoughts!) (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2004)

File.Name: 4610a_4610-204.jpg
Notes: Looking into the sanctum sanctorum of a native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) flower. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2004)

File.Name: 4610b_4610-234.jpg
Notes: I spent some time today sitting with my camera by a flowering California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub, and I spotted this very strange insect working the flowers. I reckon it's an Ichneumon Wasp (Ichneumonidae). It's thin as a needle, but over an inch long. It's holding its abdomen up at a jaunty angle, and what is that "thing" sticking out behind?? (Yipes - South Park / Eric Cartman thoughts!) Ichneumon Wasps are parasitic wasps that lay their eggs in other insect larvae and ultimately kill them. As a group, they are important biological controls for harmful species. I've lived here on San Marocs Pass for 30+ years now, and I don't recall ever seeing this critter before. Is it important? That's the global bio-diversity issue in a nutshell. I'm glad I'm still surprised by what I find in the woods, almost every day when I look close! (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2004)
CommonNames: California Buckwheat; Ichneumon Wasp; Ichneumon Wasps

File.Name: 4610c_4610-196.jpg
Notes: Strange flowers of Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) look like they are at a revival meeting giving praise for being alive. The "arms" are really stamens. They start folded up on top to meet a pollinator, and then spread out of the way to allow cross-pollination from other flowers. (Male stamens often appear before female pistils.) This plant is growing in a place that burned last year on the Santa Ynez Mountains crest. That's the ocean in the background. This is an unusual native poppy and bleeding-heart relative that only grows after wildfires and then only lasts a few years. It's a perennial with poppy-like leaves and flower stems taller than I am. The remarkable thing is that it disappears after five ot six years in one place to wait for another fire with seeds in the ground. I've found it for the last few years on an old burn east of my home. Last year there were none. I found this single plant today on another recent burn west of my home. How do they know? How long can they wait? (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2004)

File.Name: 4610d_4610-215.jpg
Notes: This Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly knows what to do with these strange flowers of Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca), and it looks like the flowers are giving praise. This is an unusual native poppy and bleeding-heart relative that only grows after wildfires. See the [Next] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2004)

File.Name: 4610e_4610-240.jpg
Notes: Composite flowers of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) growing on a firebreak that was cleared last summer but not burned. It's also flowering at another site nearby that did burn last year. It's not a weed, but maybe it's happy growing in any cleared site that gets hot enough from fire or sun? (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2004)

File.Name: 4610f_4610-243.jpg
Notes: Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) really does have large flowers an inch or more across, lots of them. It`s a native fire-follower, but this plant is growing on a firebreak that was cleared last summer and not burned. It looks like a party mask with two eyes and the inflated bud for a nose! (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2004)

File.Name: 4611h_4609-110.jpg
Notes: [mykologos/?photo_id=8095158]This Artist's Conk (Ganoderma applanatum or kin) is a woody fungus growing near the ground on a dying Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in our woods. You can write messages on the white spore surface! This is about the only mushroom I can find during our dry California summer. I'm envious of all the other great photos in this group! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2004)

File.Name: 4611g_4611-256.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters/?photo_id=7953890]Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis), aka a Bumblebee or Hummingbird Moth for good reasons, working composite flowers in our garden. The little wild bee skedaddled when the huge bee-like moth showed up, but then turned around and held his ground (or flower). See http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=7900056 (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2004)

File.Name: 4611a_4610-257.jpg
Notes: Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis), aka a Bumblebee or Hummingbird Moth for good reasons, working the flowers of what I think is Dusty Miller (Senecio cineraria) in our garden. The plant was one of those gifts I get as a teacher from students. The little wild bee skedaddled when the huge bee-like moth showed up, but then turned around and held his ground (or flower). That's a different world where some critters you compete with are SO MUCH BIGGER! (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2004)

File.Name: 4611b_4611-247.jpg
Notes: Another small Checkerspot (Nymphalidae) butterfly visiting the native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our garden. I believe this is a Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta), related to other Checkerspots and Fritillaries. You can see the crescent pattern along the hind edge of the wings. I have several books about local butterflies, but it's hard to use the books without killing and mounting specimens. I'd rather guess from photos and be wrong. At least I can match the same butterfly again. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2004)

File.Name: 4611c_4611-260.jpg
Notes: Interesting flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the wild part of our garden. The rest of today's photos are critter shots, but these flowers always remind of little people holding arms and I don't know what else up in the air. We rescued this plant after it was washed out on a slide along the road during our El Nino wet year a while back. It's a magic herb for tea and incense. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2004)

File.Name: 4611d_4610-220.jpg
Notes: Two "True Bugs" (Hemiptera, maybe family Lygaeidae?) "doing it" in a field of Toyon aka Christmas Berry (Heteromeles arbutifolia) flowers. The upper one doesn't look happy. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2004)

File.Name: 4611e_4610-238.jpg
Notes: Some kind of Blue (Lycaenidae, subfamily Polyommatinae) butterfly working new flowers of Toyon or Christmas Berry (Heteromeles arbutifolia). The hidden upper wings are more blue than the bottom wings in this photo. It's hard to catch them at rest. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2004)

File.Name: 4611f_4610-231.jpg
Notes: Cute little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) standing on tip toes while it hovers over flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) using it's long proboscis to get to the sweet nectar. It flaps - actually vibrates - its wings so fast they don't even show up. I've read that the larvae prey on the young of solitary bees, and I often see these bees flying over bare ground looking for nests. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2004)

File.Name: 4612a_4612-008.jpg
Notes: 7903751 A small Wild Bee (Apoidea) approaching a candalabra of Chaparral Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) flowers, like docking to a starship. Nah, it was dumb luck, part of taking 150+ photos on my walk today, mostly deleted. I was lying on my back to photograph the flowers, and here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2004)

File.Name: 4612b_4612-037.jpg
Notes: 7903741 Cute little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) barely touching its tippy-toes while it hovers at a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2004)

File.Name: 4612c_4612-048.jpg
Notes: 7903739 California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly is our most beautiful native butterfly. It's also the official State Butterfly of California, but I reckon only lepidopterists know why. The beautiful iridescent "dog face" pattern is on the upper side of the wings, hidden in this photo, but I get a glimpse when it flies by. It's another catch-22 that these strong fliers always fold their wings when they land; and that's the only time I can get a photo. It's my summer quest to get a sharp photo of a Dogface in the air! (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2004)

File.Name: 4612d_4612-062.jpg
Notes: 7903733 West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly working flowers of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our canyon. What beautiful names for this butterfly! The sun is behind, which brings out the color in my photo. Other native Painted Lady butterflies have more white on the forewings. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2004)
CommonNames: West Coast Lady; Canyon Sunflower; Painted Lady
ScientificName: Vanessa annabella) butterfly working flowers of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides
associatedTaxa: Vanessa annabella) butterfly working flowers of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides

File.Name: 4612e_4612-043.jpg
Notes: 7903731 This Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly was working a Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flower in the canyon. I know its dark, but I learned a secret from this photo. First notice how the butterfly hangs from the stamens with those tiny claws to get into the flower. When it does so, that candelabra of pollen-bearing anthers rubs against the veins of the butterfly's hind wings. You can barely see the fuzzy reddish-brown pollen stains on the veins at the bottom of this photo. The female pistil also hangs down and is curved outward to pick up the pollen from other flower visits. Nothing smaller than a swallowtail butterfly could do it. Now that I know what to look for, I'll get a better photo in good light to show it. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2004)

File.Name: 4612f_4612-029.jpg
Notes: 7903725 Skipper (Hesperiidae) butterfly working a native thistle, probably California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum). Its proboscis makes me think of fly-fishing! I find native thistles beautiful and confusing because they are so variable in color and form. This thistle (and varieties) can vary from white to red to purple, and the invading weed Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) can fool you. Be sure you know what you're doing before you pull them as weeds in the back country! (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2004)
ScientificName: Hesperiidae; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Cirsium vulgare
associatedTaxa: Hesperiidae; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Cirsium vulgare

File.Name: 4613a_4612-026.jpg
Notes: 7907395 Beautiful flowers of native Fringed Indian Pink (Silene laciniata ssp. major). It's surprising to find these brilliant flowers in dry summer, but pollinators like hummingbirds need them to survive, and many spring flowers wouldn't be here without them. More summer flowers are coming, even in this drought year! (12 June 2004)
Locality: Silene laciniata ssp. major). It's surprising to find these brilliant flowers in dry summer

File.Name: 4613b_4613-075.jpg
Notes: 7907345 Ripe berries of Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia). They look delicious, but taste bitter. Not many fruit this year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2004)

File.Name: 4613c_4613-081.jpg
Notes: 7907287 Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) foliage turning brilliant red and starting to fall. We have winter-deciduous plants here in central California, but summer-deciduous plants like this gooseberry make more sense, especially in a drought year. They have no fruit this year. You can see a shriveled up flower on the right end of the branch that tried and failed. I'm seeing signs of drought everywhere, natives flowering early or not at all. I'll try to document it here, pretty or not. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2004)

File.Name: 4613d_4613-095.jpg
Notes:
7907281 Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) cruising a busy neighborhood of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) flowers. There's a mystery here.

The flowers have such long-exerted stamens and styles, but a very small lower-lip "landing platform" petal. What critter are they adapted for that can lever down those flower parts for pollination? The carpenter bee is big and heavy enough, but he always takes the shortcut under the calyx. I also watched a Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working the flowers who ALSO did the cheat. (Yes, I caught it in the act, but the photo is not sharp enough to post. Darn!) Hummingbirds are a candidate, but I haven't seen them visit these flowers. We also have an amazing golden Giant Flower-Loving Fly (Apioceridae, maybe genus Rhaphiomidas or Apiocera) that works these flowers and is over an inch long with an equally long proboscis. I haven't seen one for decades, but I remember watching them work these flowers.

Do these flowers have a pollinator? Maybe this is evolution in action? I'll keep watching! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2004)
CommonNames: Carpenter Bee; Woolly Blue Curls; Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Giant Flower-Loving Fly
ScientificName: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Trichostema lanatum; Hemaris diffinis; Apioceridae</>, maybe genus Rhaphiomidas; Apiocera
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Trichostema lanatum; Hemaris diffinis; Apioceridae</>, maybe genus Rhaphiomidas; Apiocera

File.Name: 4613e_4613-109.jpg
Notes: 7907266 Large inch-long Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) using its short rigid proboscis as a lever to lift the skirts - I mean calyx - of this flower of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum), so it can enter the base of the flower to get right to the nectar without helping pollinate the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2004)

File.Name: 4613f_4613-110.jpg
Notes: 7907261 Is it comfort food, or something more emotional? This inch long Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) is clasping a flower of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) like a "significant other". It's also cheating by entering at the base of the flower to get right to the sweet nectar without helping pollination by going through the flower tube to engage stamens and styles. Many potential insect pollinators know this trick, including butterflies. Is it instinct or does each insect learn on its own? (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2004)

File.Name: 4614a_4614-146.jpg
Notes: 7911490 Nature always surprises. We're starting a drought summer and many native plants are flowering and fruiting early or not at all. But it is a very good year for California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus). This is our real native blackberry, not the usual introduced Himalayan Blackberry (Rubus discolor) that everyone collects. I ate the first ripe purple berry I saw - and there's lots more for wildlife. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4614b_4614-161.jpg
Notes: 7911483 This deciduous Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) is giving up early in this drought year. I love the palette of red and green. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4614c_4614-143.jpg
Notes: 7911480 California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly "puddling" on dry ground, using its proboscis to get moisture or salts from the soil. That black hose is a drip line, but this critter was more interested in the dry soil and let me get close. These handsome butterflies patrol a "flyway" through the oak trees and garden, but they rarely visit our flowers. Do they ever feed? Is their main purpose in life to frustrate photographers? (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4614d_4614-167.jpg
Notes: 7911474 Unknown Skipper (Hesperiidae) butterfly working a native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) flower. I'm working to learn local butterflies, but so far "skipper" = "skip her" because they are such a difficult group! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4614e_4614-154.jpg
Notes: 7911468 I call this a "creek spider" because I always find them sitting on their orb-webs over the water in local creeks. Maybe it's a Long-Jawed Orb-Weaver (Tetragnathidae)? They become very slender when they hang with legs extended. There are only a few water holes left on our mostly dry creek. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4614f_4614-136.jpg
Notes: 7911459 A rust fungus infection erupting with spores on a leaf stalk of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum). The twig-like base and abrupt bend in the petiole are characteristic. I found this back in April and thought it was an insect gall, compare http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=7715026. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4615a_4614-231.jpg
Notes: 7916039 Sunset and picnic dinner on the old stone wall of "The Castle" yesterday evening. (See [Previous] photo.) Our kids and friends are starting to assemble for the Live Oak Music Festival next weekend just down the road. It's our annual "no-excuse" time to meet and catch up and share the best music there is - our campground acoustic jam session after the show ends! Check http://www.liveoakmusic.org/ for the rest of the lineup. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4615b_4614-199.jpg
Notes: 7915954 "Wake up to find out that you are the eyes of the world" - as the Dead used to sing. We had a picnic dinner at "The Castle" last night. It's the ruin of a mountain mansion in our mountains that was destroyed by wildfire in the 1930s. Only stone walls and chimneys and perfect views remain. This is looking through the stonework at the Santa Ynez Valley beyond. The bright spot in the distance (right eye) is Lake Cachuma. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4615c_4614-248.jpg
Notes: 7915893 New seed heads of Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) at sunset. Each seed has a long feather-like plumose tail that starts straight and then twists around and around. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4615d_4614-203.jpg
Notes: 7915863 California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) flowers catching the glow from the setting sun. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4615e_4614-183.jpg
Notes: 7915850 Beautiful flowers of Farewell-to-Spring (Clarkia unguiculata) catching some of the sunset glow on our picnic hike last evening. The petals look like someone took a scissors to the base, but that's how they are. These late spring flowers are mostly done. I was surprised to find them along our trail. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4615f_4614-179.jpg
Notes: 7915809 Feather-like seed heads of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha) catching sunset color. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2004)

File.Name: 4623a_4623-082.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of our native succulent Lanceleaf Liveforever aka Rock Lettuce (Dudleya lanceolata or kin) on a sandstone cliff in our canyon. These are poor common names for this beautiful plant! The elegant flowers look sculpted from wax. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2004)

We've had a house-full of twenty-something kids for the last two weeks. Great visit with my kids and friends, but it was so nice to get out in the woods alone with my camera today with no distractions!

File.Name: 4623b_4623-089.jpg
Notes: These wild bees seem almost intoxicated working this flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in our canyon. I can imagine rolling around a room-sized mass of sweet flowers that offer everything I need... The individual florets of the composite head are beautiful, note the dark tips on the petals. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2004)

File.Name: 4623c_4623-103.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Blue (Lycaenidae, subfamily Polyommatinae) butterfly resting on a leaf of the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in our garden. I love the orange antennae tips. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2004)

File.Name: 4623d_4623-113.jpg
Notes: A brilliant male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a composite flower in our garden this morning. I will keep chasing these butterflies until I catch a photo with wings open that shows the amazing iridescent "dog face" pattern on the upper front wings. Alas, they always close their wings when they land on a flower to feed. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2004)

File.Name: 4623e_4623-058.jpg
Notes: Large flower of native Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) in our garden. The flowers open at night, but they stay open on foggy mornings like today. The tree-like female stigma is impressive. Once you let these into your garden, you'll have them forever! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2004)

File.Name: 4623f_4623-067.jpg
Notes: Interesting flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana), with fog drip. The fog has been flowing over the mountains every night or early morning to bring us some welcome moisture. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2004)

File.Name: 4624a_4624-127.jpg
Notes: Face to face with a female California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a fiery composite flower in our garden. (Maybe Gaillardia?) Those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2004)

File.Name: 4624b_4624-132.jpg
Notes: Different strokes for different folks... Here's a female California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly fishing for nectar with her long proboscis, and a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) digging for pollen to add to her already bulging pollen baskets. The flower is a fiery composite in our garden, maybe Gaillardia? (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2004)

File.Name: 4624c_4624-134.jpg
Notes: Female California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) on the wild edge of our garden. The markings and pointed forewings are distinctive. I've been chasing the more colorful males around the garden for the last week or two, but several of these females appeared in the garden today and more along the creek to liven things up. The males and females seem to tussle and fly off together in a twirl. Yeah sure, get a photo of that! Well, I'll keep trying! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2004)

File.Name: 4624d_4624-117.jpg
Notes: Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly on a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). I believe it's a female, with two forewing spots rather than one. But check out that little wild bee with loaded pollen baskets in the lower left that is about to chase her away! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2004)

File.Name: 4624e_4624-121.jpg
Notes: Coral Bells (Heuchera sp.) in our garden. It's not native, but we have local relatives. Did you spot the little bee flying in at the bottom? (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2004)

File.Name: 4624f_4624-164.jpg
Notes:
Web-making caterpillars taking over a branch of Chaparral Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). Creepy. But what are they caterpillars OF? I've been searching my books. I believe these critters will become next years Chalcedon Checkerspots (Occidryas chalcedona or kin). I have many photos of the adults in the recent archive. It's been our most common butterfly lately, and now I see why. They seem to take over a branch or two, but don't kill the host plant. I won't curse these "tent-caterpillars" any more!

My source is the classic "Insects of Western North America" by E.O. Essig (Macmillan, 1936), who mentions "the webbing habit" of this species. It's remarkable that most butterfly books say very little about caterpillars, especially since most butterflies spend most of their lives as caterpillars. Wannabe naturalists (like me) take note: species are easy, but life-forms and relations between species are the real thing! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2004)

File.Name: 4625a_4625-195.jpg
Notes: New flower of Toloache (Datura wrightii etc.), aka "Jimson Weed" but that's really something else. It was probably introduced from Mexico long ago by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. The huge white flowers open at night, but this one is getting ready in the afternoon. Look! I see a mask of pain looking up wrapped in a shroud with too-many arms that have become claws... Nah, that's just me, and the times. It's a very beautiful flower. (Santa Ynez River, 25 June 2004)

File.Name: 4625b_4625-176.jpg
Notes: Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) is a remarkable composite (Asteraceae) plant with two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. I sometimes recognize the large leaves and weak stems of this perennial on shady trails in the mountains, though I rarely find flowers. But there was a whole field of blooming plants on a sandy flat near the river. Pretty flowers, but not much butterfly action except for a few skippers. Is "Sacapellote" a native American name? Is it a useful herb? (Santa Ynez River, 25 June 2004)

File.Name: 4625c_4625-178.jpg
Notes: Daisy-like flowers of Leafy Fleabane (Erigeron foliosus var. stenophyllus) growing on a sandy flat in woodland along the river. Did you spot the little fly working the upper-left flower? I didn't either until I saw it on my computer. (Santa Ynez River, 25 June 2004)

File.Name: 4625d_4625-191.jpg
Notes: Yellow Jacket Wasp (Vespula pensylvanica or kin) stripping the carcass of this large tadpole, like flaying a whale a century ago in Moby Dick days. I reckon the victim is an introduced Bullfrog (Rana catesbeiana) tadpole who made the mistake of getting caught in the wrong puddle. (Santa Ynez River, 25 June 2004)

File.Name: 4625e_4625-181.jpg
Notes: It's not beautiful death despite the pretty reflections. I found these crawdads (not shed skins) dead in the drying up water hole in the [Previous] photo. I suspect it's the classic environmental problem of eutrophication on a small scale: a bloom of algae dies; decomposers like bacteria and fungi get to work and use up the available oxygen; then everything dies in the now anoxic ("no oxygen") water. Life is harsh in a drought year. (Santa Ynez River, 25 June 2004)

File.Name: 4625f_4625-182.jpg
Notes:
I took a hike today along the Santa Ynez River between Manzanita Lane and White Rock. This is our river in a drought year. I thought about Africa. The remaining water is a thick green organic soup with dying fish and crawdads and tadpoles and lots of animal tracks. There's more water at a few other spring-fed pools where people go. It's a harsh summer. I decided to maintain a water trough in our canyon for the wildlife. (Santa Ynez River, 25 June 2004)

There's also a stumper here. Note that neat ring of rocks around the water hole. I also saw this in two more almost-dry water holes. I don't think kids or beavers are piling these rocks up. Que pasa? Eddies when the river flows?

File.Name: 4626a_4624-149.jpg
Notes: 7967305 As a naturalist, you look close and study the plants and critters so that you can spot what is NOT ordinary. Here is a tiny native bee flying to an open flower of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). But the flower is all wrong. The 5 petals are all spread out instead of being fused into the usual "two-lipped" flower with an upper part that holds the stamens and a lower part that spreads out below. (Compare [Previous] photo.) I'm waiting to see if the rest of the buds on this branch/plant develop the same way. Maybe new pollinators like this bee will be attracted to the dangling stamens? In a very small way, here is evolution in action! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2004)

File.Name: 4626b_4624-148.jpg
Notes: 7967285 This is how the beautiful native flowers of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) SHOULD look, but compare the flower in the [Next] photo. The flowers really have 5 petals, but they are joined together to make a "two-lipped" flower with an upper lip that arches over the stamens and a lower lip that spreads out below. These are a favorite hummingbird flower. See my photo in the archives at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=443938 (and [Previous] to that) to see a hummingbird in action. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2004)

File.Name: 4626c_4625-199.jpg
Notes: 7967282 Toloache (Datura wrightii etc.) is related to the infamous Jimson Weed (D. stramonium) that grows across the country. Current opinion is that it's not really native, but was introduced from Mexico long ago by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. Yes Sandman, it has seed pods with really big spikes. The white trumpet flowers are even bigger, 6-8 inches long, open at night to attract sphinx moths(?). The spent flowers hang on the plant until the spiky seed pod within expands and tears it away. It's an interesting and dangerous plant with deep roots in culture as well as the earth. (Santa Ynez River, 25 June 2004)

File.Name: 4626d_4624-140.jpg
Notes: 7967276 Green fruit of native California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) sure look like little avocados. That's not surprising since both are in the Laurel (Lauraceae) family. This is another native plant that is having a good year despite our extreme drought. The fruit have an edible nut inside (if you leach and roast it) surrounded by a thin layer of flesh that is pungent like the bay leaves only MORE so! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2004)

File.Name: 4626e_4624-146.jpg
Notes: 7967269 Ripening fruit of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii). Look close to see the pin-like glandular hairs on the spiny fruit. They will turn dark red as they mature, but these are only blushing. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2004)

File.Name: 4626f_4624-168.jpg
Notes: 7967268 Immature berries on Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the woods near our home. The berries should be pure white, but these are "sunburned" red on top. I usually find these berries in autumn, but everything is early in this very dry summer, so maybe that's why? (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2004)

File.Name: 4627a_4627-029.jpg
Notes: 7971126 Female California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly dipping in the font of life, which happens to be a beautiful flower of native succulent Lanceleaf Liveforever aka Rock Lettuce (Dudleya lanceolata or kin). It's an unusual butterfly, but it's been common in the garden for the last two weeks, sometimes 5 or 6 at a time. The stumper is that the caterpillars ONLY(?) grow on the native shrub False Indigo (Amorpha californica) which I haven't found in our canyon. (I have found it on the other side of the highway a mile or two away.) I'll keep looking, it must be here! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2004)
CommonNames: California Dogface; Lanceleaf Liveforever; Rock Lettuce; False Indigo
ScientificName: Zerene eurydice; Dudleya lanceolata; Amorpha californica
associatedTaxa: Zerene eurydice; Dudleya lanceolata; Amorpha californica

File.Name: 4627b_4627-031.jpg
