Notes: 7971115 Female California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a flower of our native succulent Lanceleaf Liveforever aka Rock Lettuce (Dudleya lanceolata or kin). So tender. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2004)
CommonNames: California Dogface; Lanceleaf Liveforever; Rock Lettuce

File.Name: 4627c_4627-005.jpg
Notes: 7971111 An unusual large fly visiting a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in our garden, maybe a Tachnid Fly (Tachinidae)? It has huge eyes and a pelican-like throat that doesn't quite make sense. It's probably a parasite/preditor that lays eggs on other insects and larvae that will hatch and devour them. I made a collection of local insects two decades ago with net and poison jar, and the flies (Diptera) were the most surprising and exciting. (I've forgotten so much!) (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2004)

File.Name: 4627d_4627-038.jpg
Notes: 7971102 What is it about food and procreation? I reckon this Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) flower is the social "singles bar" for these beetles to do it all. I believe the larger pair are Buprestid (Buprestidae) beetles. Hmmm, what about "Tom Jones" and "Tampopo" movies? (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2004)

File.Name: 4627e_4627-036.jpg
Notes: 7971099 I was laying on my back to get a photo of the amazing flowers of this Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum), when a Skipper (Hesperiidae) butterfly flew into the photo at the bottom with proboscis unrolling. It's an ancient problem that some critters get so specialized they become easy prey. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2004)

File.Name: 4627f_4627-012.jpg
Notes: 7971096 "Adult caterpillar" is an oxymoron, just like "immature caterpillar" is a pleonasm. Caterpillars are the larval forms that become adult butterflies. I'm pretty sure this "mature caterpillar" is the final molt or instar of native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) which happily has a taste for the introduced fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) that is invading our mountains and islands. I understand why there is no comprehensive field guide to caterpillars (yet). They look different everytime they molt. I'm collecting photos of this critter for a series. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2004)

File.Name: 4628a_4628-066.jpg
Notes:
7976443 This is the last photo in a series of six showing the stages of caterpillars of native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies happily munching the introduced Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) that is invading our mountains and islands.

The penultimate step for any caterpillar is to form a chrysalis or pupa. Note how it is slung like a rock climber in a hammock, to give some thrashing room! What are those two points on the "head", antennae?

Inside this shell, the caterpillar is literally dissolved and reformed as an adult butterfly. Beautiful wings and antennae will come from that slug! Pupae are incredibly vulnerable during this stage too. But I was lucky to spot this chrysalis. It had the right color and angle to look like a leaf bract on the host fennel plant.

Again I think of this quote from Anais Nin: "It takes courage to push yourself to places that you have never been before... to test your limits... to break through barriers. And the day came when the risk it took to remain tight inside the bud was more painful than the risk it took to blossom."

I've only seen one adult Anise Swallowtail in the garden so far this year, and I didn't get the photo. But visit the archives at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=769528 to see what it will be, God willing. Hmmm, there's a stumper here. Who laid the eggs that became these caterpillars and chrysalis? Did it happen this year or last? How do they overwinter? There's SO MUCH to learn about the natural world! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2004)

File.Name: 4628b_4627-010.jpg
Notes:
7976290 This is the fifth photo in a series of six showing the stages of caterpillars of native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies happily munching the introduced Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) that is invading our mountains and islands.

"Adult caterpillar" is an oxymoron, but at least this one is mature. Note how the sections of its body have expanded accordian-like and are nearly full. These fat mature caterpillars must be incredibly vulnerable to preditors like birds and wasps. That face looks painted on! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2004)

File.Name: 4628c_4627-059.jpg
Notes:
7976267 This is the fourth photo in a series of six showing the stages of caterpillars of native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies happily munching the introduced Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) that is invading our mountains and islands.

These caterpillars are doing real work of biological-control on the remaining fennel along our road. I wonder what they do when they get to the end of a stalk? The adult butterfly is called the "Anise Swallowtail", but it is native and feeds on other native plants in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family that are not invasive weeds. I approve of their taste in weeds, live long and prosper! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2004)

File.Name: 4628d_4628-065.jpg
Notes:
7976228 This is the third photo in a series of six showing the stages of caterpillars of native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies happily munching the introduced Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) that is invading our mountains and islands.

Caterpillars are eating machines that soon outgrow their outer skin or exoskeleton. This one has just crawled out of it's old skin and is waiting to "harden up" to do it again. I think of them as living accordians that expand as they eat until they have to move up to the next size. I think this is the final molt (with 2 or 3 before?). Look how the black bands shrink in the following photos. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2004)

File.Name: 4628e_4628-062.jpg
Notes:
7976196 This is the second photo in a series of six showing the stages of caterpillars of native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies happily munching the introduced Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) that is invading our mountains and islands.

I think this is the caboose-end of the caterpillar. I can't see the toes, but the other end looks larger. What a great Mr. Bill face! It's appropriate. Every critter needs to produce ONE offspring to replace it to maintain a stable population. Butterflies (and most criters) lay many more eggs because most will never make it. Caterpillars are targets for birds and parasitic wasps and flies and fungi and viruses. It's a wonder that any survive. Every butterfly is a survivor. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2004)

File.Name: 4628f_4628-058.jpg
Notes:
7976165 This is the first photo in a series of six showing the stages of caterpillars of native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies happily munching the introduced Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) that is invading our mountains and islands.

We've been vigilant about removing fennel in our canyon, but now I'm glad we missed this clump. I'll be sure to remove the seeds. The caterpillars start very small and look remarkably like bird droppings (and their own poop aka "frass"). I seem to find the smallest caterpillars on top of the plant, like these two. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2004)

File.Name: 4629a_4629-084.jpg
Notes: 7980394 We're in a drought summer, and most summer plants are already finishing a month (or two) early. But this native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) along our road apparently didn't get the message! That's as many honeysuckle flowers as I've ever seen in one cluster. It was cloudy and drippy today, but the sun finally came out for perfect afternoon light. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2004)

File.Name: 4629b_4629-072.jpg
Notes: 7980343 I've been chasing this Common Checkered Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly about the garden for a few days, and I finally got it. I'm glad it rests with wings open so I could find it in my books. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2004)

File.Name: 4629c_4629-079.jpg
Notes: 7980311 I was experimenting shooting these native White Sage (Salvia apiana) flowers into the sun, when a large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) flew into the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2004)

File.Name: 4629d_4629-080.jpg
Notes: 7980292 Exuberant flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) with the sun behind. It looks like a music festival! (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2004)

File.Name: 4629e_4629-083.jpg
Notes: 7980272 Thorny branch of native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2004)

File.Name: 4629f_4627-006.jpg
Notes: 7980264 Not quite sharp, but such a tender glimpse of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly suckling at a non-native Sage (Salvia sp.) flower in our garden. It makes me feel good. Maybe the flower is Greek Sage (Salvia fructicosa)? It has never stopped flowering all year, and it's a favorite with our butterflies, bees, and hummingbirds. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2004)

File.Name: 4630h_4630-121.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 8237693 We've had gloomy fog for a few days, so maybe that's why I found this unexpected Bolete (Boletus sp.) mushroom in our summer-dry woods today. I thought Boletus edulis at first because of the tan cap, but it had a thin reddish stem with yellow at the top. I don't know what it is. And it doesn't matter since another invading fungus has already covered the pores with mold. (See [Previous] photo.) (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2004)

File.Name: 4630g_4630-120.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 8237614 I found this unexpected Bolete (Boletus sp.) mushroom in our summer-dry woods today. Darn, it had another invading fungus that already covered the pores with mold. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2004)

File.Name: 4630a_4630-107.jpg
Notes:
7984189 It's been foggy and gloomy for the last few days, not clearing until late afternoon; and then the wind kicks in. Not the best photo weather, but the moisture must be good for our dry woods. Another benefit is that the Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flowers stay open all day. This plant appeared as a seedling in our garden, but I'm pretty sure it's not our native species. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2004)

Another benefit of the drippy weather is that I actually found a Boletus mushroom in the woods today! I posted the photos at http://www.fotolog.net/mykologos/?pid=8237693 and [previous].

File.Name: 4630b_4630-115.jpg
Notes: 7984130 Web-building caterpillars taking over flower cluster of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). I believe these critters will become next years Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona or kin) butterflies. I wonder why the caterpillars congregate on the outside of their web where they are potential prey for parasitic wasps and flies? They twitch violently if you get too close, so maybe that's protection enough. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2004)

File.Name: 4630c_4630-113.jpg
Notes: 7984110 Tiny web-building caterpillars taking over flower cluster of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). I believe these critters will become next years Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona or kin) butterflies. A few of the tiny slug-like caterpillars are visible in the upper-right. Creepy, but they only ensnare a few branches, and the plants seem to do fine. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2004)

File.Name: 4630d_4630-093.jpg
Notes: 7984070 Incoming Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) approaching a Sage (maybe the Salvia "Indigo Spires" hybrid?) flower in our garden. These are the noisy large nickle- to quarter-size black bees that drill neat holes in our fascia boards and rain sawdust on our barbecues. (I don't mind at all!) I watched them for a while today, hoping to get a hummingbird shot. They either go into the flowers or cheat by entering under the calyx. (See [Previous] two photos.) I reckon it's about 90% cheat. Is this approaching bee thinking about her strategy, or does it just depend on how she lands? (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2004)

File.Name: 4630e_4630-092.jpg
Notes: 7984027 This large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) is working a Sage (maybe the Salvia "Indigo Spires" hybrid?) flower in our garden in the "right" way. It's going through the flower to get to the nectar, thereby helping pollination. It looks like a tight fit! I love the way her antennae are swept back into a fashionable "V" shape. Compare the [Previous] photo to see another way these bees work the flowers. These bees mostly cheat, so I was happy to get this photo even if not quite sharp. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2004)

File.Name: 4630f_4630-094.jpg
Notes: 7983972 Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) cheating a Sage (maybe the Salvia "Indigo Spires" hybrid?) flower in our garden. This bee knows the trick of entering under the calyx to get right to the nectar without helping pollination. The bee looks so content, I can almost hear it purring. Compare the [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2004)

File.Name: 4701a_4701-143.jpg
Notes: 7988240 I found this large Dragonfly (Anisoptera, maybe a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum)?) tangled up in one of those BIG spider webs that spanned the dirt road we were hiking today. I've walked into these webs at night and *they don't break* - that's really spooky! When I broke this web, it twisted up and ensnared the dragonfly even more. I did my best to unsnarl it and the beautiful creature flew away to a nearby tree, I hope unharmed. How do those big Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spiders make these giant webs? Can they really subdue such large prey? (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)

File.Name: 4701b_4701-151.jpg
Notes: 7988182 Face to face with a beautiful large Dragonfly (Anisoptera) that I rescued from one of those BIG spider webs that spanned a dirt road. Look at those huge eyes. I had the sense it was looking right at me and understanding as I untangled it. Then it flew away, I hope unharmed. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)

File.Name: 4701c_4701-149.jpg
Notes: 7988163 I freed this beautiful large Dragonfly (Anisoptera) from a spider web. It was a BIG spider web that spanned a dirt road and was stronger than the fishing leader I use for trout. Until I got it untangled, I actually had this critter on a leash. Then it flew away, I hope unharmed. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)

File.Name: 4701d_4701-139.jpg
Notes: 7988110 Beautiful West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly working flowers of a composite in our garden. I think the flower is Dusty Miller (Senecio cineraria). It was one of those gifts I get as a teacher in a small pot that is really happy here and got big. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)
ScientificName: Vanessa annabella) butterfly working flowers of a composite in our garden. I think the flower is Dusty Miller (Senecio cineraria
associatedTaxa: Vanessa annabella) butterfly working flowers of a composite in our garden. I think the flower is Dusty Miller (Senecio cineraria

File.Name: 4701e_4701-132.jpg
Notes: 7988099 Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) unrolling it's proboscis and taking aim as it approaches a Cleveland Sage (Salvia clevelandii) flower in our garden. These wonderful hummingbird-like daytime moths hover at flowers as they feed and never really land, so I reckon they must get everything lined-up just right as they approach. Wow, do they ever crash? (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)

File.Name: 4701f_4701-126.jpg
Notes: 7988053 If you want to photograph insects or hummingbirds, plant a garden, especially in a drought year like this. Our garden merges into the natives. It's like a waterhole where everything must go. Did you spot the hummingbird slightly right of center? I won't try to guess what this female hummer is. We have Black-Chinned, Anna's, and Allen's hummingbirds fighting over this wee patch of garden. The beautiful reddish-brown rufous-colored Allen's hummers (rufous in front, but green on back) are unusual this year, and seem to be dominating the fray. They're too feisty to get close to me with my camera, but I'll keep trying. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)

File.Name: 4702a_4701-160.jpg
Notes:
7992183 Native American Chumash rock art in our mountains. This is a painted "pictograph" not a chipped "petroglyph". There are a dozen of these sites in our mountains, mostly hidden in remote places like this. I'm sure there are some that haven't been found yet. I keep looking. In time they will disappear, erased by the wind and the rain.

These rock art sites are usually regarded as secret and ceremonial, but there's a bedrock mortar cluster nearby where a whole village must have worked together to grind the mortar-holes so deep in the sandstone, so it wasn't very secret back then. (See [Previous] photo.) I don't know what the paintings mean. We lived just across the meadow 25 years ago, where our kids were born. I still dream about this magic place that was our backyard. What do you see? (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)

File.Name: 4702b_4701-166.jpg
Notes: 7992113 Part of a bedrock mortar where native American Chumash people ground acorns (etc.) under a huge Live Oak tree that still stands. That's my camera case for scale. These deep sandstone bowls are 1-2 feet across. It's remarkable how the holes merge together. The water shows how drippy the fog has been lately, though there is a perennial spring nearby. This is magic country. It's remarkable to sit under this tree and think that Chumash kids sat under the same tree and dreamed about their future. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)

File.Name: 4702c_4701-174.jpg
Notes: 7992072 Native Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) flowering in a small wetland below a spring in our mountains. It was foggy all morning, so the night-blooming flowers are still open in the afternoon. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)

File.Name: 4702d_4701-171.jpg
Notes: 7992061 First ripe fruit of Himalayan Blackberry (Rubus discolor). It's not native, but it has become the "wild blackberry" along the whole West Coast. I accept it. The fruit are tasty and good for wildlife, the brambles provide shelter for wildlife, and we don't really have a choice at this point. That's why it's important to stop NEW invaders like the [Previous] photo that aren't so tasty. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)

File.Name: 4702e_4701-176.jpg
Notes: 7992017 Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) has pretty yellow pea-flowers when it flowers, but it's an alien invasive shrub that shouldn't be here. Usually it follows the roads, but here it is along a mountain trail a good mile from any road, ripening all those potent seeds for next year. Did Caltrans start this? Learn these invaders and destroy them. They will simplify our rich wildlands. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)

File.Name: 4702f_4702-199.jpg
Notes: 7991970 Our dry woods are already full of fall colors. This is California Maiden-Hair Fern (Adiantum jordanii) growing in the woodland near Lake Cachuma. The spore-bearing parts are hidden under the curled lip of the leaves. Many natives are shutting down early in this drought summer. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 July, 2004)

File.Name: 4703a_4702-196.jpg
Notes:
7995790 There's not many new acorns in the mountains near home, so it was good to find these Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) shrubs with lots of new acorns in the woods near Lake Cachuma. Some critters (at least) will eat next fall. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 July, 2004)

The sun didn't come out until after 5:00 today, and then the wind kicked in. No problem. Today's photos are recent good ones from my "Ready" folder last week. I've been researching the new Nikon D70 that I'm about to buy, donated in part by school parents after my end-of-the-year school slide show a month ago. I'm so excited! Any advice?

File.Name: 4703b_4701-180.jpg
Notes: 7995756 New berries on my favorite native tree - Madrone (Arbutus menziesii). The trees in our canyon didn't even flower in this drought year. Nice to know there are fruit in another canyon nearby, the birds will need these. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2004)

File.Name: 4703c_4630-102.jpg
Notes: 7995735 Seedpods of Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum). (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2004)

File.Name: 4703d_4627-014.jpg
Notes: 7995695 Tiny caterpillar of the native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly happily munching the introduced and invasive Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) along our road. At this stage, the caterpillers look a lot like bird poop or droppings of the mature caterpillars. That's good camouflage! (27 June 2004)
Locality: Foeniculum vulgare) along our road. At this stage

File.Name: 4703e_4625-203.jpg
Notes: 7995669 Ripe fruit of Basket Bush aka "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata). It's a poison oak relative and look-alike, but the middle leaf does not have a separate stalk, it has berries rather than dry seeds, it has a distinctive "skunk-like" smell, and it does not make you itch. The stems were the preferred basket material for the "warp" of native American Chumash baskets. The berries are tart and tasty if you "suck and spit" - but don't suck too long or you notice the skunk. Don't suck poison oak seeds by mistake! Berries are scarce in our mountains this dry summer. I found these near the river. (Santa Ynez River, 25 June 2004)

File.Name: 4703f_4625-197.jpg
Notes: 7995602 Little beetle on a huge trumpet flower of Toloache (Datura wrightii). (Santa Ynez River, 25 June 2004)

File.Name: 4704a_4704-031.jpg
Notes: 7999955 Beautiful native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flowers in our shady canyon today. I waited for the afternoon sun to peek through the oaks to get this moment of perfect light. Today's photos are a series of lilies and butterflies. Check the [Previous] photos to understand these remarkable flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2004)

File.Name: 4704b_4704-058.jpg
Notes: 7999909 The beautiful native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) grows in our shady canyon. I waited for the afternoon sun to peek through the oaks and got this photo looking down into the shadows when the sun first appeared. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2004)

File.Name: 4704c_4704-048.jpg
Notes: 7999864 Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flowers in our canyon. These beautiful native lilies grow in our shady canyon, but I waited for the afternoon sun to peek through the oaks to get some photos. The female style is hanging down, and the stigma at the tip is not yet receptive with hairs to wipe the sticky pollen from visiting butterflys' wings to achieve consummation. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2004)

File.Name: 4704d_4704-045.jpg
Notes: 7999771 As the male anthers of this Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flower finish releasing pollen, the female pistil rises up to the same level in a very masculine way. The fertile stigma at the end of the style develops brush-like hairs to receive the pollen from the next butterfly that visits. All flowers are sexy, that's what they're for. But these native lilies are even more so when you know what to look for! (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2004)

File.Name: 4704e_4704-065.jpg
Notes: 7999731 Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly hanging on a Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flower in the canyon to get the sweet nectar. The male pollen-bearing anthers line up perfectly with the orange-brown dust on its wings. The female pistil is hanging down out of the way to avoid self-pollination, but it will soon change position. (See the [Next] photo.) You have respect this tattered butterfly who has been around and is still working the flowers despite that big chunk missing from his wing. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2004)

File.Name: 4704f_4704-014.jpg
Notes: 7999684 Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working a Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). That's cool, but note the orange-brown dust on its wings. The dust is pollen from the Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flowers in the canyon. The [Next] photo shows how it happens. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2004)
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Canyon Sunflower; Humboldt Lily
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Venegasia carpesioides; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Venegasia carpesioides; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum

File.Name: 4705a_4705-090.jpg
Notes:
8004458 Grand Coulee DAM! it's hard to upload (or do anything) on the Web tonight! I thought I uploaded this photo before the last. Anyway...

*Gulp* I did it. It's so easy to go to Samy's Camera in Santa Barbara and swipe the card to spend more than I've EVER spent for myself! (Cars and houses don't count - at least not my car.) I bought a Nikon D70 kit, 105mm micro lens, SB-600 flash, and enough goodies to raise the price to the *gulp* level. I couldn't resist setting out the new stuff on the table and some of my great old Nikon stuff that I hope I'll be able to use with this new camera. Most of this tableau is my good old stuff.

First impressions: big and heavy, I want to keep my Olympus 2040 for hikes and school to protect my neck (sorry, Julie); I don't understand the autofocus - it gets lost and nothing happens; I miss being able to hold the camera out and see the image on the screen to sneak up on butterflies; but I love that "ca-chink" sound when I press the button, it takes me back! Sure there's a learning curve. You'll see it here. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2004)

File.Name: 4705c_4705-079.jpg
Notes: 8004366 I took a *deep breath* and bought my new Nikon D70 this afternoon. I reckon it's good that I had to charge the battery first, but I didn't get very far in the manual. Here's my first test shot using my new Nikon 105mm micro close-up lens. It works! This pretty fly is a Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) working a composite flower in our garden. The photo is hand-held with everything set on "auto". I know, RTFM, I'll learn how to do it right! (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2004)

File.Name: 4705d_4704-072.jpg
Notes: 8004269 Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus) resting on a leaf in the garden. It's hard to catch these wasps at rest. They cruise the garden constantly looking for caterpillars to supply their neat paper nests suspended under the eaves. Yellow Jackets (Vespula) bother me, but these wasps don't. They do have a storm-trooper look about them. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2004)

File.Name: 4705e_4704-068.jpg
Notes:
8004140 California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly caught in a huge Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider web strung over the creek to catch these "flyway" guardians. That butterfly is already tattered enough without this ending, but she was also too tangled up (and blue) to rescue. Of course I tried. She only got more tangled. Sure I'd help the spider too. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2004)

"Caring is a reflex. Someone slips, your arm goes out.

A car is in the ditch, you join the others and push.

You live, you help."

- Ram Dass.

File.Name: 4705f_4704-019.jpg
Notes: 8004054 Flat spray of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) flowers with an incoming wild bee. This is down by the creek. Plants up the hill near home finished flowering a month ago in this dry summer, disturbingly early. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2004)

File.Name: 4706g_4706-105.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8083725 Male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a composite flower (maybe Gaillardia?) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2004)

File.Name: 4706a_4706-106.jpg
Notes:
8009392 What a punk face on this male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a composite flower (maybe Gaillardia?) in our garden! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2004)

I stayed close to the garden today with my new Nikon D70 camera and the manual close by, and played with the many camera settings. It was sunny and breezy all day, the wind is nudging this butterfly leeward! No need for a tripod when *everything* is moving and you measure depth-of field by a butterfly`s eye!

My new camera works, but it doesn`t do magic. Close-ups in the wind will always be hard. You need a high shutter speed to stop motion; a small f/stop for focus; and low ISO for image quality. These options are not compatible! Every photo is a compromise made for the occasion. I`m learning to control these options on my new camera to get better photos. Wahoo!

File.Name: 4706b_4706-104.jpg
Notes: 8009287 I always seem to see more with my camera than I do with my eyes! This is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly "puddling" on damp soil in our garden. These butterflies rarely visit our flowers, but they do take water and salts from the damp ground. What a neat tripod of sensory antennae and proboscis. I wonder what that soil "senses" like? I've been field-testing my new Nikon D70 camera with 105mm micro lens in the garden today. This photo shows how *narrow* the depth-of-field is at these close distances! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2004)

File.Name: 4706c_4706-121.jpg
Notes: 8009235 Little beetles on native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) trying to make more beetles. That one in the back doesn't quite understand the process. These little beetles have short wing-covers and a large protruding fleshy abdomen like a Rove or Carrion beetle. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2004)

File.Name: 4706d_4705-086.jpg
Notes: 8009176 If you raise Mexican Hat (Ratibida columnaris) flowers in your garden, then you know tiny these individual florets are! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2004)

File.Name: 4707g_4707-217.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8089764 Sometimes you just gotta scratch! Pretty little beetle inside a native flower of Weed`s Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus). (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2004)

File.Name: 4707a_4707-156.jpg
Notes: 8014816 Dandelion-like seedhead of Yellow-flowered Hawkweed (Hiericium argutum) growing on a rock ledge along our road. The soft yellow "sun" in the background is the composite flower. I usually walk past this straggling native plant and its tiny flowers without noticing, but I've been field-testing my new Nikon D70 and 105mm micro lens today. I`m learning. I'm seeing things I've never noticed before. I love my new camera! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2004)

File.Name: 4707b_4707-193.jpg
Notes: 8014775 This may be our most astounding wildflower - Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus). It's a native Mariposa Lily, not a weed, but named for one. It's remarkable because it starts to flower in July when everything is dry and done in central California, and those colors and hairs...! This lily was growing on an exposed ridge that was once cleared for a fire break and is now growing back. I took many photos, I'll do a series soon. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2004)

File.Name: 4707c_4707-206.jpg
Notes: 8014717 Feather-like seed plumes of Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) with the sun behind. Those plumes look kind of dingy brown when the sun is the other way. That's a lesson for life. Find the right way to look at the world or *everything* is dingy! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2004)

File.Name: 4707d_4707-154.jpg
Notes: 8014684 This is the first time I've captured a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly resting in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree where she belong. These handsome butterflies patrol their "flyways" through the oak trees, but they rarely land except to "puddle" on damp soil. (Do they eat?) This sister has been around. Fly on little wing! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2004)

File.Name: 4707e_4707-137.jpg
Notes: 8014647 I know where this Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly has been! It's in the garden now, but that heavy pollen deposit on her hindwing shows that she's been visiting the Humboldt Lilies (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in our canyon. See http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=7999731 for the rest of the story. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2004)

File.Name: 4707f_4707-144.jpg
Notes: 8014629 "So how windy was it today?" Windy enough to blow this Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly right off her perch! It's a good reminder how great manual focus is for outdoor closeups, at least with a real focus ring on your lens. Autofocus tries to follow the motion, which is impossible. With manual focus, you just focus ahead of time and grab her when she flies by and looks sharp. I'm learning my new Nikon D70 in less than perfect conditions. I like it! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2004)

File.Name: 4708a_4708-227.jpg
Notes:
8020006 I think this is a white-fringed Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis, but cf. E. tristis) skipper butterfly visiting a composite flower (maybe Gaillardia?) in our garden. That yellow "sun" in the background is a flower petal. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2004)

I admit I'm showing off! I bought my new Nikon D70 camera and 105mm micro lens this week and I'm learning what I can do with it. I know that closer isn't always better for natural history photos where story and context are more important. But it sure is fun seeing what I can do with my new (and beloved) camera! It was breezy again today in our canyon. These photos are all hand-held using manual focus.

File.Name: 4708b_4708-232.jpg
Notes: 8019947 I think this is an Ambush Bug (Phymatidae) - maybe the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica? Look for the eye up and right of center to sort it out. (It's prey has trouble too!) This critter is hiding between the flowers on a spray of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica), which gives you an idea how small it really is. Tiny but gnarly! It's front legs are swollen like crab claws to grab its victems and subdue them with its sharp straw-like beak. It can catch much larger insects like honey bees and butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2004)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug; California Goldenrod

File.Name: 4708c_4707-219.jpg
Notes: 8019913 Pretty little beetle deep inside a native flower of Weed`s Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus). This beetle has found the flower's sanctum sanctorum, the sweet nectar gland hidden at the inside base of the petal. It looks like it's standing on tippy-toes! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2004)

File.Name: 4708d_4705-084.jpg
Notes: 8019895 Tiny Wild Bee (Apoidea) peering over the edge of a composite flower petal in our garden. We're checking each other out! (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2004)

File.Name: 4708e_4708-222.jpg
Notes: 8019879 Tiny Wild Bee (Apoidea) visiting a Sage (Salvia) flower in our garden. Think how huge this little flower is for this critter! You can just make out the "pollen baskets" on its hind legs. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2004)

File.Name: 4708f_4707-208.jpg
Notes: 8019870 The rest of todays photos are insect shots, but this one sure looks critter-like. I reckon it's a fungus? Whatever it is, it's growing on ripe fruit of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family. The fruit are edible and sweet/sour at first, but they soon turn bitter. It's another "suck and spit" fruit, a great design for seed propagation! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2004)

File.Name: 4709g_4709-253.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8101929 Leafcutter Bee (family Megachilidae) working a daisy-like composite flower in our garden. These wild bees carry pollen under their abdomen instead of on their hind legs like most bees. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2004)

File.Name: 4709a_4709-249.jpg
Notes: 8025164 Beautiful opening buds of Pincushion Flower (Scabiosa atropurpurea?) in our garden. The composite-like flowers are related to Teasel in the Dipsaceae family. They lose most of that pink color when they open. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2004)

File.Name: 4709b_4709-272.jpg
Notes: 8025133 I found this unusual native Dense-flowered Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) flowering near the Tanbark Oak in the [Previous] photo. It's not common. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2004)

File.Name: 4709c_4709-275.jpg
Notes: 8025115 Ripening acorns of native Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus). These beautiful not-quite-real-oak trees have unusual acorns with Chestnut-like bristles on the cups. They remind me of something from the ocean. I was surprised to find any of these acorns close to home. Most of our trees declined to flower in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2004)

File.Name: 4709d_4709-286.jpg
Notes: 8025047 Bee-like Hover Fly aka Flower Fly (Syrphidae) visiting a daisy-like composite flower in our garden. Look at the bottom of the photo right below the fly's head. See the tiny yellow spider? It really is a jungle out there! (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2004)

File.Name: 4709e_4709-254.jpg
Notes: 8025011 I reckon this pretty little wild bee is a Leafcutter Bee (family Megachilidae) working a daisy-like composite flower in our garden. These bees cut out those perfect circles from leaves to line their nests. Honey Bees have special "pollen baskets" on their hind legs to carry pollen, but these wild bees carry pollen on the underside of their abdomen. You can see that this one is collecting yellow pollen on its legs to transfer to its "basket". (See my new photo at http://www.fotolog.net/bugsncritters/?pid=8101929.) (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2004)

File.Name: 4709f_4709-259.jpg
Notes: 8024969 I believe this little bee-size butterfly is a Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica). What a name! This one is working flowers of (I think) Oregano (Origanum vulgaris?) in our garden. It has moth-like colors, but club-like antennae. I have a photo of a different metalmark (family Riodonidae) at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=783144. The shape of the wings and how they hold them seems distinctive. This one has light brown colors which sounds more like the desert subspecies, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2004)
ScientificName: Calephelis nemesis californica; Origanum vulgaris; Riodonidae
associatedTaxa: Calephelis nemesis californica; Origanum vulgaris; Riodonidae

File.Name: 4710g_4710-083.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8107481 The mothership is landing! Male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly approaching a flower in our garden, and showing gaillardia
Locality: Zerene eurydice) butterfly approaching a flower in our garden

File.Name: 4710a_4710-037.jpg
Notes:
8029677 I can't look at these strange flowers of native Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) without telling stories! But I won't. Think of your own stories!

The "arms" are really stamens. They start folded up on top to meet a pollinator, and then spread out of the way to expose the stigma and allow cross-pollination from other flowers. That's the sky and ocean in the background. This is an unusual native poppy and bleeding-heart relative that only grows after wildfires and then only lasts a few years. I have more photos in the archives. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2004)

File.Name: 4710b_4710-019.jpg
Notes: 8029652 Gnarly Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, maybe Apiomerus?) hiding in a flower head of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum). These 3/4 inch long predators aren't exactly camouflaged, but they sit motionless waiting for their prey, ready to grab them with mantis-like forearms and suck them dry with that sharp beak. I try to use the same strategy of sitting still to photograph insects, but he was aware of my every move no matter how long I waited. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2004)

File.Name: 4710c_4710-003.jpg
Notes: 8029615 Large Grasshopper (family Acrididae) resting on charred ground that burned in a small wildfire last summer. It looks like he's wearing a leather cap over his head for a Renaissance Fair! He has bright blue hind legs. Note the black dot on its eye, looking right at us. I've noticed that in other insect photos. Is the black spot really there, or is it just an optical illusion? (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2004)

File.Name: 4710d_4710-055.jpg
Notes: 8029558 New flower of native Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora). I think it's beautiful, but it also illustrates a new photography word I've learned since getting my new camera. "Bokeh" is how out-of-focus points of light are rendered. My Nikon 105mm micro lens seems to have "bad bokeh" because it turns some pinpoint highlights into little doughnuts, visible to the right of the stamens in this photo. It's not a huge problem, but now I'm starting to notice it. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2004)

File.Name: 4710e_4710-047.jpg
Notes: 8029530 Fasciated California Chicory aka California Plumseed (Rafinesquia californica). This is a rather weedy native composite that usually has a slender stem and white flowers. But when a plant becomes fasciated, it grows wide and weird like this. The coiled top looks like a real fiddle head! Many plants become fasciated and some gardners collect specimens. Why does this happen? (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2004)

File.Name: 4710f_4710-072.jpg
Notes: 8029505 The upper wings of a male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly leaving a composite flower in our garden, and showing his "dog face" as he goes. I know it's not in focus, but neither is the photo in my "Butterflies Through Binoculars" book! They always fold their wings together when they land, so you have to catch them in the air, like this. My new Nikon D70 can take 3 photos per second until I fill my memory card, so I just pushed the button down and waited until he left the flower. I think I'm a little gun-shy, and I flinch as the camera fires away. I'll keep trying to get a better shot. Focus has to be right, but also lighting. The "dogface" pattern is actually yellow, but it has that iridescent magenta cast that constantly changes. It's a remarkable creature. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2004)

File.Name: 4711a_4711-088.jpg
Notes: 8034903 Some hummingbird flowers rub your head and some tickle your chin. I'm not sure what this chin-tickler plant in the garden is, but the hummers sure like it. I guess that's a female Anna's Hummingbird, but we also have Black-Chinned and Allen's fighting it out in the garden right now. I was trying to sneak up on butterflies when I spotted this pretty lady through the Salvia. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)

File.Name: 4711b_4711-084.jpg
Notes: 8034840 Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) hovering at a Sage (maybe the Salvia "Indigo Spires" hybrid?) flower in our garden. The wings really are clear. It's an unusual kind of Sphinx Moth (family Sphingidae) related to "tomato worms". But this moth works the dayime shift, is colored like a bumblebee, and hovers like a hummingbird! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Sage; Sphinx Moth

File.Name: 4711c_4711-097.jpg
Notes:
8034787 Eye to eyes with a Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus) taking water at our little wetland at home. It's just a 6x6x1 foot fiberglass tub that catches the overflow from our hot tub, which is strictly a "cold tub" in summer we share with the frogs.

Closer isn't always better, but I can't help trying with my new camera, and it shows interesting details. Most insects have "compound eyes" made of dozens or hundreds of simple eyes. But most insects also have a triangle of three simple eyes known as ocelli on their forehead set between the compound eyes. You can see them clearly in this photo. What's it like to see the world through all those eyes looking in different directions? I can't imagine, but it sure is hard to sneak up on these critters with a camera! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)

File.Name: 4711d_4711-106.jpg
Notes: 8034717 Sunburned new foliage of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) growing on the dry edge of our garden. The leaves should be silvery-gray, but everything is different in this dry summer, even close to the garden we water. I'm learning some new tricks that our natives use to deal with drought. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)

File.Name: 4711e_4709-263a.jpg
Notes:
8034692 This is a nature-who-am-I photo. Stop reading now and try to guess!

.

.

.

This is looking close at a water drop resting on a broccoli head in our garden! The broccoli is ready to cut now before it bolts in this hot weather. It looks like a snow globe. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)

File.Name: 4711f_4710-036.jpg
Notes:
8034664 Here's another photo of the unusual flowers of native Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) taken from a bit further away than the [Previous] photo. The flowers are borne in fist-sized clusters on long 6-8 foot wand-like stalks that rise from glaucous poppy-like foliage.

This photo doesn't show the plant, but at least it shows that it IS a plant and not a meeting of supplicants or aliens! I`m always so intrigued by the flowers that I don`t have one good photo of the whole plant. I`ll get a photo next week, and some seeds as well. This might be one of those seeds we have to scorch before planting! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2004)

File.Name: 4712g_4711-134.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8120251 BIG Orb-Weaver spider (Araneidae) wrapping its prey. The victim happens to be a large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) which may give a sense of scale. This spider had a web stretched 20 feet from our porch to the oak trees. I held my camera up at arms-length to get this photo at night, with flash. Don't think about the movie Aliens! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)
ScientificName: Araneidae) wrapping its prey. The victim happens to be a large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) wrapping its prey. The victim happens to be a large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis

File.Name: 4712a_4711-123.jpg
Notes:
8040575 White-Lined Sphinx (Hyles lineata) moth hovering over a yellow Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flower in our garden after sunset. She's having trouble guiding her long proboscis into the deep ovary tube below the petals. If you look close and follow her proboscis, you can see that she missed, and it is curling back out. But see the rest of today's photos to understand the process. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)

This is a night shot with flash. It was almost dark when I noticed a flutter in the garden, but I couldn't quite see what it was. I grabbed my week-old Nikon D70, and learned some new tricks. The flash and autofocus really do work at night thanks to the "AF-Assist Illuminator" that shines a brief burst of bright light on the scene to set focus and exposure. I couldn't see anything without that. I didn't realize there were TWO different species of Sphinx Moths working the flowers until after I downloaded the photos. These flash pictures are really sharp. I prefer natural light, but I have to learn more about this!

I did get out today - we did a long hike at Arroyo Hondo Preserve up a ridge that burned to the ground during the Gaviota Fire last June. Maybe tomorrow.

File.Name: 4712b_4711-120.jpg
Notes: 8040489 White-Lined Sphinx (Hyles lineata) moth pushing deep into a summer bulb flower in our garden. We lost the tag for it, so I don't know what it is. If you look close, you might be able to trace her proboscis probing deep into the flower trying to reach the nectar at the bottom. Isn't it wonderful how her antennae fold back to do this! This garden flower is not native, so I'm not sure if she made it all the way down. Native flowers MUST be just the right length - think about it! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)

File.Name: 4712c_4711-131.jpg
Notes:
8040448 This is the first shot in a series of four. This fist-sized gray Sphinx Moth (maybe Sphinx chersis?) is hovering like a hummingbird with his proboscis fully extended trying to find the right place to enter the flower. These yellow Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flowers in our garden have a very deep ovary tube below the flower, and it must be hard to find the entrance while hovering in midair. The [Next] photos show what happens after this. This is a night shot with flash. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)

If you saw the last shots of this series first, work the [Previous] button now to see the photos in order. I didn't shoot these as a sequence, but I did take 60+ photos standing in the dark last night in the garden and pieced it together. Julie didn't know I was there and turned the sprinkler on me!

File.Name: 4712d_4711-125.jpg
Notes:
8040385 This is the second shot in a series of four. This fist-sized gray Sphinx Moth (maybe Sphinx chersis?) has his proboscis in place in the flower, but he is still hovering up above. If you only saw this one photo, you might wonder how the moth helps pollinate the flower. But these Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flowers in our garden have a very deep ovary tube below the flower that the moth wants to get to the bottom of for the good stuff. See the [Next] photos. This is a night shot with flash. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)

BTW note that this gray Sphinx Moth has red-eye but the White-Lined Sphinx moth in the first two photos today doesn't. What a surprising species difference!

File.Name: 4712e_4711-127.jpg
Notes: 8040351 This is the third shot in a series of four. This fist-sized gray Sphinx Moth (maybe Sphinx chersis?) has his proboscis in place and is now moving into an Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flower in our garden. There's no mystery how pollination takes place with this flower and moth. This is a night shot with flash. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)

File.Name: 4712f_4711-130.jpg
Notes: 8040335 This is the last shot in a series of four. This fist-sized gray Sphinx Moth (maybe Sphinx chersis?) is diving deep into an Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flower in our garden. This is a night shot with flash. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)

File.Name: 4713a_4712-170.jpg
Notes:
8045391 Purple Needle Grass (Nassella pulchra) crown-sprouting after the Gaviota Fire last month. This native perennial bunch grass has become a symbol for protecting the Santa Barbara foothills. It's good to see it re-sprouting so soon after the 7,500 acre wildfire. It will be interesting to see how the natives compete with weedy annual grasses and mustards (etc) next year. (Gaviota Coast, 13 July 2004)

All of today's photos are from The Arroyo Hondo Preserve at the eastern edge of the fire zone where the wildfire was finally contained along the eastern front. These are "before" shots for the "after" shots I will get next spring.

File.Name: 4713b_4712-166.jpg
Notes: 8045346 We saw hundreds of toasted snails in the foothills along our trail into the burn area of the Gaviota Fire. Except for some old pre-wildfire bones, they were the only animal casualties we found. The snails are non-native invaders that are real pests. This was on the eastern edge of the wildfire in foothills dominated by "Wild Mustard" (Brassica spp.) and other introduced weeds that are even greater pests. The fire did some good! This area may have been burned by an intentional backfire set along the road below. I don't think it burned very hot here. (Gaviota Coast, 13 July 2004)

File.Name: 4713c_4712-194.jpg
Notes: 8045288 These charred hollow stumps are all that remain of the chaparral shrubs on this ridge after the Gaviota Fire last month. I`m not sure what plant this is, but I found many of these stumps with two "arms" left up in the air. There`s no sign of these shrubs stump-sprouting. (Gaviota Coast, 13 July 2004)

File.Name: 4713d_4712-204.jpg
Notes: 8045264 Charred branches of chaparral shrubs after the Gaviota Fire last month. Maybe this odd trunk is Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum)? The area behind me was burned to the ground, but there is lots of charred wood here. These are "before" shots for the "after" shots I will get next spring. (Gaviota Coast, 13 July 2004)

File.Name: 4713e_4712-182.jpg
Notes: 8045234 "Like the mythical Phoenix that periodically consumes itself and emerges from the ashes anew..." Nah, it's not the same. This Bush Sunflower (Encelia californica) is stump-sprouting after the Gaviota Fire last month. Many of our native chaparral shrubs survive underground as burls and re-sprout vigorously if the fire isn't TOO intense. It's still exciting to see it happen! (Gaviota Coast, 13 July 2004)

File.Name: 4713f_4712-228.jpg
Notes: 8045164 This is not a pineapple or an agave that's been pruned by machete for mezcal. It is a native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) that survived the Gaviota Fire last month. It is recovering well with new green leaves on top. It's an interesting fire adaptation how the burned leaves form these neat scars and prune themselves to just the right length. You can see the process with those whitish dead leaves on top. All of today's photos are from The Arroyo Hondo Preserve at the eastern edge of the wildfire zone. (Gaviota Coast, 12 July 2004)

File.Name: 4714a_4713-247.jpg
Notes: 8051489 Eye to eye with a Pacific Chorus Frog (Pseudacris regilla) aka Pacific Tree Frog (Hyla regilla). Our redwood hot tub becomes our cold tub in summer, and we share it with the frogs. They move over when I get in and wait until I'm done. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2004)
CommonNames: Pacific Chorus Frog; Pacific Tree Frog

File.Name: 4714b_4713-264.jpg
Notes:
8051453 I was out in the garden after dark last night chasing bees and moths, when I noticed a white blotch on the edge of a Dahlia flower. I couldn't see what I had until I got back to my computer. This is an Ambush Bug (Phymatidae) - maybe the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica. I think of a horse with armour plates.

This critter is really tiny. Those are yellow Dahlia stamens in the foreground. Is it hiding in a safe place for the night, or is it really hunting? What would he be hunting for at night? (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2004)
CommonNames: Dahlia; Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug

File.Name: 4714c_4713-276.jpg
Notes:
8051404 This fist-sized gray Sphinx Moth (maybe Sphinx chersis?) is hovering like a hummingbird with proboscis fully extended approaching this night-blooming yellow Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flower in our garden. This should be the first photo in the series that begins at

< >< >< >http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8040448

and goes [Previous] from there. You can see the full proboscis and measure it against the long inferior flower tube down to the ovary. It fits. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2004)

File.Name: 4714d_4713-261.jpg
Notes: 8051369 Large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) frantically loading up on sticky pollen from the night-blooming yellow Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flowers in our garden that open as twilight fades. The bees are going nuts collecting pollen, sometimes three at a flower, bumping into me with my camera. Then they leave, and the Sphinx Moths take the night shift. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2004)
CommonNames: Carpenter Bee; Evening Primrose; Sphinx Moths

File.Name: 4714e_4713-262.jpg
Notes: 8051340 This large Bumblebee (Bombus) was out late working the yellow Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flowers in our garden along with the Carpenter Bees. This is a night shot with flash. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2004)

File.Name: 4714f_4711-115.jpg
Notes: 8051312 Mom was right - this is what happens to bees who stay out too late. The [Next] photos show why that bee was up past sunset. This is a huge Orb-Weaver spider (Araneidae) wrapping its prey. The victim is a large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) which may give a sense of scale. This spider has a web stretched 20 feet from our porch to the oak trees. I held my camera up at arms-length to get this photo at night, with flash. Don't think about the movie Aliens! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2004)
ScientificName: Araneidae) wrapping its prey. The victim is a large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) wrapping its prey. The victim is a large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis

File.Name: 4716a_4716-305.jpg
Notes:
8062220 Face to face with a Scavenger Fly (Sarcophagidae). It looks stitched together like Leatherface! These native flies look like overgrown house flies with bulbous red eyes and a red-tipped butt and a gray/white striped thorax. They are noisy and sometimes bug me when I'm hiking, but they have their own work in the woods as decomposers and mostly leave me alone. Flies mostly look like - uh - flies, but they are incredibly diverse when you start looking close and noticing the differences in looks and life. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2004)

Went kayaking with Julie at Avila yesterday on a perfect summer day. I'm glad for these times I have to leave my camera ashore and just enjoy the moment. There were endless birds, sea otters, seals, and sea lions just to tease me!

File.Name: 4716b_4716-292.jpg
Notes:
8062098 Native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly at a Sage (maybe the Salvia "Indigo Spires" hybrid?) flower in our garden. The caterpillars have a taste for the introduced and invasive Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) along our road, but this is a native butterfly that also lives on native umbels like Lomatium. This butterfly posed like it wants to end up in a museum collection! They have the same yellow and black colors as the Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly, but they are smaller and brighter and have a black band along the front edge of the forewing. They also have some special tricks I hope can capture for future photos. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2004)

I have a photo sequence of the caterpillars at
  http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=7976443

and [Previous] to that.
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Sage; Fennel; Western Tiger Swallowtail
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Salvia; Foeniculum vulgare; Lomatium; Papilio rutulus
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Salvia; Foeniculum vulgare; Lomatium; Papilio rutulus

File.Name: 4716c_4716-298.jpg
Notes: 8062025 Little Gray Hairstreak aka Common Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly on the non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata?) flowers in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2004)

File.Name: 4716d_4716-295.jpg
Notes: 8061995 Unknown succulent (Crassulaceae) flower in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2004)

File.Name: 4716e_4716-310.jpg
Notes: 8061987 I rescued this metallic blue Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae) from our "garden wetland" today. It's just a little fiberglass tub with water plants that catches the overflow from our hot/cold tub. This pretty wasp lost it's footing and was floundering in the water until I let him crawl up my finger. That's my gold wedding band behind him. I worry about getting stung by honey bees, yellow jackets, and tarantula hawks. Most wasps have no interest in us. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2004)

File.Name: 4716f_4716-328.jpg
Notes:
8061963 This insect egg on a stalk is anchored to the tip of one spine of an almost ripe fruit of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii). Some insects lay their eggs in masses. Other insects (including most butterflies) drop them here and there like this one. Different strategies, different folks. Either way, the odds are against any single egg! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2004)

This is tiny. I shot this photo handheld with manual focus at 1:1 magnification using my 105mm micro-Nikkor lens. I stopped down to f/22 for depth of field and let the flash stop the motion. But I was in "aperture priority" exposure mode, so the slow shutter speed made a ghost-image of my jiggles. Now I know. I should be able to get flash-sync at 1/500th second in manual mode for less background light. I'm still working this learning curve!

File.Name: 4717a_4717-003.jpg
Notes:
8066060 Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working a Dahlia flower in our garden. The black marks on the wings all run perpendicular to the front whereas the Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) has a black band along the leading edge. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2004)

Compare the [Previous] photo and yesterday's shot at
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8062098.One trick of the native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly is to work the flowers from behind. Our other native Western Tiger and Pale Swallowtails always (usually?) hang from the flowers and feed from the front. This flower is a Cleveland Sage (Salvia clevelandii) in our garden. (It's a California native, but not native here.) It sure looks like this butterfly is pulling the flower back to make it easier to enter, but it may just be hanging on in the wind. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2004)
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Anise Swallowtail; Anise Swallowtail; Western Tiger; Pale Swallowtails; Cleveland Sage
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Dahlia; Papilio zelicaon; Papilio zelicaon; Salvia clevelandii
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Dahlia; Papilio zelicaon; Papilio zelicaon; Salvia clevelandii

File.Name: 4717b_4716-291.jpg
Notes: 8066037 One trick of the native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly is to work the flowers from behind. Our other native Western Tiger and Pale Swallowtails always (usually?) hang from the flowers and feed from the front. This flower is a Cleveland Sage (Salvia clevelandii) in our garden. (It`s a California native, but not native here.) It sure looks like this butterfly is pulling the flower back to make it easier to enter, but it may just be hanging on in the wind. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2004)

File.Name: 4717c_4716-322.jpg
Notes: 8066006 Flowers are full of small (to us) dramas. Here a little Wild Bee (Apoidea) and a tiny spider play "cat and mouse" on a flower of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica). There is no safe place. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2004)

File.Name: 4717d_4716-321.jpg
Notes: 8065989 Yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae?) hiding under the flowers of native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica). (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2004)

File.Name: 4717e_4717-006.jpg
Notes: 8065968 Large wasp with beautiful golden fur and bright orange legs and abdomen visiting Pennyroyal (Mentha pulegium) flowers in our garden. She is larger than our common Paper Wasps. She has a very narrow neck and a raised "collar" around the front of the thorax, so I guess this is some sort of Digger Wasp (family Sphecidae).
Locality: Mentha pulegium) flowers in our garden. She is larger than our common Paper Wasps. She has a very narrow neck and a raised "collar" around the front of the thorax

File.Name: 4717f_4709-282.jpg
Notes: 8065848 Another interesting fly - maybe a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae)? - visiting a daisy-like composite flower in our garden. All flies have just two wings ("di-ptera" = "two wings"), unlike most insects with four. Most flies have little sensory knobs called "halteres" where the hind wing should be. This fly seems to have paddles instead! (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2004)

File.Name: 4718a_4717-061.jpg
Notes: 8070887 Looking up into the crown of a huge Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree with late-afternoon sun. We were at a birthday party/festival/BBQ last night on one of the original Mexican Land Grant Ranchero properties that starts along the lower Santa Ynez River and then goes and goes up into the mountains. This was maybe the biggest Coast Live Oak tree I've ever seen. Note to self: don't JUST bring my telephoto/macro lens when we go visiting! (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 July 2004)

File.Name: 4718b_4717-060.jpg
Notes: 8070816 Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees with Lace Lichen (aka Treebeard Lichen!) (Ramalina menziesii) catching the last rays of the afternoon sun. These native oaks usually have rough dark bark, but sometimes it is smooth and light gray like this. It's a spooky-looking woodland at dusk! (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 July 2004)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Lace Lichen; Treebeard Lichen

File.Name: 4718c_4717-051.jpg
Notes: 8070785 Lace Lichen (Ramalina menziesii) hanging from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. The lichen is often called "Spanish Moss", though it's not a moss and this is California. I favor the name Treebeard Lichen! I got my "Treebeard" nickname 30 years ago when I started working as a naturalist at the Santa Barbara County Schools' Outdoor School. Sure the name is from Tolkien, but I was thinking of this lichen. It still matches my scraggly beard. Be careful picking a nickname - it might stick! (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 July 2004)
CommonNames: Lace Lichen; Coast Live Oak; Treebeard Lichen

File.Name: 4718d_4717-016.jpg
Notes: 8070730 Flowers of Fish's Milkwort (Polygala cornuta var. fishiae). This is an uncommon native shrub with odd flowers that is in it's own Milkwort (Polygalaceae) family. I usually find it in shady north-slope canyons not too far from the river. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 July 2004)

File.Name: 4718e_4717-041.jpg
Notes: 8070666 Male flowers of Giant Creek Nettle (Urtica dioica ssp. holosericea) with the sun behind. You're in trouble if you unknowingly walk into a patch of this plant! It has tiny hairs with an intense stinging toxin that will get you if you brush against it and the hairs break off in your skin. Here's a fun challenge. If you reach out and grab a leaf hard, you crunch the hairs and don't get stung. If you hesitate just before grabbing, you're done for. Fortunately an instant remedy, Curly Dock (Rumex crispus and kin) nearly always grows nearby. Crush a leaf and hold it against the sting for instant relief. Don't pull your punches! (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 July 2004)

File.Name: 4718f_4717-043.jpg
Notes: 8070606 Looking up at native Box Elder (Acer negundo var. californicum) leaves. This is a maple relative that grows on bottomland along the lower stretch of the Santa Ynez River. It has maple-like seeds, but it looks like someone took a scissors to the leaves. It has an odd local distribution. It's very common below Buellton, but it stops there. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 July 2004)

File.Name: 4719g_4719-099.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8163511 Pretty yellow aphids hanging under a leaf of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden. They remind me of little Pokemon creatures! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2004)

File.Name: 4719a_4719-096.jpg
Notes: 8076810 Every year we get these pretty yellow aphids with black appendages (maybe Aphis nerii?) on the Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden. They don't kill their host or spread to other plants, so I leave them be. I wonder if they store milkweed toxins like Monarch Butterfly caterpillars? I see no sign of parasitic wasp or fly activity. They look like "cattle on a thousand leaves", but maybe they taste yucky? (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2004)

File.Name: 4719b_4719-117.jpg
Notes: 8076739 Leafcutter Bee (family Megachilidae) working this Mexican Hat (Ratibida columnaris) flower in our garden. These wild bees carry pollen in a basket under their abdomen instead of on their hind legs like most bees. They do seem to have a special structure on their hind legs to gather pollen and transfer it to the basket under their butt. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2004)

File.Name: 4719c_4719-112.jpg
Notes: 8076701 My favorite photo today, but you have to look twice to see it. This is a perfectly camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae?) waiting in a volunteer Tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa) flower in our garden. Nature can be a bitch! I had to lay on my back to get this photo. I've been finding these crab spiders hidden in many flowers, garden and wild. I wonder if they can change their color to match the host plant, or was it just luck that this Mexican-import vegetable was just the right color for this native spider? Tomatillos are the essential ingredient in chile verde and much of my favorite food. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2004)

File.Name: 4719d_4719-116.jpg
Notes:
8076629 Yipes, don't read this if it gives you the creeps!

This is a nest of the Golden Paper Wasps (Polistes fuscatus) who live under the eaves of our front porch. I used flash since it is always in the shade. You can see all the wasp life-stages in the neat hexagonal cells of the nest: little ovoid white eggs, white larvae with faces, the pupae enclosed with a paper cap where they become adults, and the tending adult on top. The cell in the lower-left seems to have a drop of honey, but I believe these wasps provide mostly animal food such as "caterpillars, which are skinned by the adult wasps". I don't want to taste it, but I wonder what it is! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2004)

BTW I've lived with these wasps for decades and never been stung. They are beautiful and they get the cutworms. Just give them some space! (Yellow Jackets are different!)

File.Name: 4719e_4719-109.jpg
Notes: 8076560 I believe this is a Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly visiting the Oregano (Origanum vulgaris?) flowers in our garden. Her upper wings are checkered black and white, but she always folds her wings up after landing. I've been seeing the same ol' beautiful butterflies lately, so I have my eye out for any new visitors like this. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2004)

File.Name: 4719f_4719-126.jpg
Notes: 8076528 Large fly with pretty wings, probably a Hover Fly aka Flower Fly (family Syrphidae), resting on Yarrow (Achillea) flowers in our garden. Many Syrphid flies mimic bees, but the eyes and antennae give them away. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2004)

File.Name: 4720g_4720-130.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8169662 Male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly on a composite flower (maybe Gaillardia?) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2004)

File.Name: 4720a_4720-136.jpg
Notes: 8081968 Incoming parasitic fly with egg-layer ("ovipositor"?) extended from its abdomen and looking for prey! See [Previous] photos for some of the action. I'm not sure what kind of fly this is. It's not bristly like a typical Tachnid Fly (Tachinidae), or bee-colored like a typical Flower Fly (Syrphidae). Whatever it is, it's a mighty preditor. If you poison your garden, you will lose these friends. Don't do it. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2004)

File.Name: 4720b_4720-153.jpg
Notes: 8081902 Parasitic fly giving the goods to a yellow Milkweed Aphid (maybe Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a leaf of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden. I saw the fly reach under the leaf with its extended abdomen, and that yellow aphid literally jumped to the top side. I've never seen an aphid move so fast! I wonder if it did him any good. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2004)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 4720c_4720-146.jpg
Notes: 8081872 A parasitic fly visiting a colony of yellow Milkweed Aphids (maybe Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a leaf of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden. Parasitic wasps have a sharp "oviposter" to insert their eggs into their prey, but I believe these flies attach their eggs to the outside of their prey and let the larvae do their own work. Either way it's a horrible fate for the victim. I got more photos like this that look like the fly is laying eggs on the leaf near the aphids rather than on them. Maybe the larvae find their prey on their own? (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2004)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 4720d_4720-142.jpg
Notes: 8081780 This is one of those "lucky shots" you get when you squat for an hour trying to follow one fly (see [Next] photo) on a plant with manual focus. This small wasp landed on a milkweed leaf right in front of me with its struggling prey, trying to deliver the coup de grace, and took off a moment later. Everything happened so fast, I only saw the drama through my lens. I'm not sure if that's an ant or an ant-mimicing spider. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2004)

File.Name: 4720e_4720-134.jpg
Notes: 8081718 Intricate flowers of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden. It's an American native, but not here in California. The beautiful milkweed flowers have orchid-like complexity, and sometimes trap insects to their death. The rest of todays photos show some of the drama that happens on this plant every day. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2004)

File.Name: 4720f_4720-132.jpg
Notes: 8081688 Tiny Wild Bee (Apoidea) approaching a non-native yellow Columbine (Aquilegia) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2004)

File.Name: 4721g_4721-181.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8176294 Little bee-like Hover Fly aka Flower Fly (family Syrphidae) doing a VTOL (Vertical Takeoff and Landing) maneuver from a daisy-like composite flower in our garden. These insects fly more like helicopters than airplanes. They vibrate their wings instead of flapping them. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2004)

File.Name: 4721a_4721-179.jpg
Notes: 8087649 "My what a big proboscis you have!" These large Carpenter Bees (Xylocopa tabaniformis?) are probably the most common insect in our garden that's bigger than a nickel. No surprise, they drill all those neat nest-holes in the faschia boards all around our roof! I see them in the garden every day, but I never realized what a long proboscis they have! This bee was lapping the florets of this little daisy-like composite flower in our garden, then it seemed to wipe its proboscis with its legs to get the pollen. It only tried once or twice, and then flew away to find more rewarding flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2004)

File.Name: 4721b_4721-166.jpg
Notes: 8087596 I thought bee when I photographed this little critter, but now it looks more wasp-like. Either way it's resting on a pretty clown-colored Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flower in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2004)

File.Name: 4721c_4721-183.jpg
Notes: 8087581 Little bee-like Hover Fly aka Flower Fly (family Syrphidae) resting on a Zinnia flower in our garden. It is tiptoeing on the florets! (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2004)

File.Name: 4721d_4721-177.jpg
Notes: 8087564 This pretty little fly looks more like a wasp than the bees that these flies usually mimic. It has a narrow abdomen like a wasp, but it does have enlarged rear legs like the pollen basket of a bee. Is it an unusual Flower Fly (Syrphidae) or something else? It is visiting a daisy-like composite flower in our garden. Check the last photo I post today to get a sense of scale of that same flower. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2004)

File.Name: 4721e_4721-167.jpg
Notes: 8087523 Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly working a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). What a name! I wonder if there's more to it than the dismal colors. It looks like it is casting its proboscis in into that composite flower to see what it catches. This small butterfly worked the same flower for at least 15 minutes, unlike most of our butterflies that flutter by here and there. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2004)

File.Name: 4721f_4721-173.jpg
Notes: 8087507 Minimalist flowers of the Concord Grape (Vitis labusca) that covers the front of our house every summer until we hack it back in the fall. It just started to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2004)

File.Name: 4722g_4722-206.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8183321 Maybe this is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae) working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. It has bristles on its thorax and a large "post-scutellum" lobe, but it has a bare abdomen. Note the unusual flattened rear legs with spines. (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2004)

File.Name: 4722a_4722-192.jpg
Notes:
8093588 "Yum flies!" This Blue Belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis?) on our deck would like the rest of todays photos which are all Flies (Diptera)! (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2004)

If you suffer from "Insect Fear" then skip the [Previous] five photos! I'm really interested in flies. They all look like - uh - flies, but there is so much variation in form and life style. I reckon that's life!
CommonNames: Blue Belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Flies

File.Name: 4722b_4722-214.jpg
Notes:
8093574 Unusual fly working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. Maybe it`s a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae). It has bristles on its thorax and a large "post-scutellum" lobe, but it has a bare abdomen. It held its wings in an unusual way, and it had unusual flattened rear legs with spines. We went to the river for a dip after cleaning house today. It was VERY WINDY, but I did what I could. (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2004)

I have another photo that shows the unusual legs at:

&nbsb;&nbsb; http://www.fotolog.net/bugsncritters/?pid=8183321

It resembles the fly I photographed attacking the aphids in our garden two days ago, see:

&nbsb;&nbsb; http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8081968

File.Name: 4722c_4722-210.jpg
Notes: 8093533 Interesting Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae) with characteristic bristles and that large "post-scutellum" lobe at the rear of the thorax. It is working a flower cluster of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. The adult flies feed on flowers, but the larvae are important parasites of other insects. These flies (and many wasps) are the basis of natural pest control. Kill them, and you have to kill everything! (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2004)

File.Name: 4722d_4722-207.jpg
Notes: 8093458 Green Bottle Fly aka Blow Fly (Phaenicia sericata or kin, family Calliphoridae). These flies are handsome but nasty. They breed in garbage and carrion, but they feed on plants like this pretty flower of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum). This is near a popular picnic area and swimming hole at the river. I guess some critter has to clean up after all those people who won't do it themselves. (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2004)
CommonNames: Green Bottle Fly; Blow Fly; Scale Broom

File.Name: 4722e_4722-201.jpg
Notes: 8093386 This tiny fly with long legs and metallic colors on a leaf of Summer Squash in our veggie garden is smaller than a house fly. The color changes from metallic green to gold depending on the angle of the light. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2004)

File.Name: 4722f_4722-196.jpg
Notes:
8093366 Is this a Blue Bottle Fly aka Blow Fly (Eucalliphora lilaea, family Calliphoridae) or something else? My books show a wider gap between the eyes with inward-pointing bristles. There are lots of these flies in the garden right now. Maybe bad stuff is getting into the compost pile, or maybe we finally got that dang Wood Rat that's been trying to move in! (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2004)

If you suffer from "Insect Fear" then skip the next four photos! I'm really interested in flies. They all look like - uh - flies, but there is so much variation in form and life style. I reckon that's life!

File.Name: 4723g_4723-253.jpg
Notes: [flowerpower] 8028900 Toloache (Datura wrightii). (Santa Ynez River, 23 July 2004)

File.Name: 4723a_4723-235.jpg
Notes:
8098572 Beautiful cone of Smooth Scouring Rush aka Horsetail (Equisetum laevigatum) aglow with sunlight from behind like a paper lantern. This part of the river is still running. (Santa Ynez River, 23 July 2004)

In this drought summer, our river is mostly dry and dangerously overused. Has anyone ever TESTED the shrinking swimming holes for E.coli or hepatitis or polio?? I have to think this is a public health matter that's being ignored as a matter of policy. You can still find clean flowing water and spring-fed pools if you explore away from the usual picnic areas. All of today's photos are from such a secret place.

Please DON'T buy an Adventure Pass to visit YOUR National Forest. It just encourages them. And don't stay home either. The right way to protest this fee is to prominently ignore it. Julie taught me not to get mad. Just smile and explain to the gate-keeper that you won't buy the pass. They probably agree. I'm all for a fee to visit a recreation area like our river, but someone got greedy trying to extend it to the whole forest and backcountry. Don't buy it.

File.Name: 4723b_4723-278.jpg
Notes: 8098459 The last red flowers on a head of native Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. occidentale) that's going to seed. Our native thistles are beautiful and uncommon.
