Don't even think about clearing weeds in the backcountry unless you can tell the natives from the invaders! (Santa Ynez River, 23 July 2004)

File.Name: 4723c_4723-277.jpg
Notes: 8098419 "You lookin' at me?" This Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus) was hanging almost motionless on this Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) acorn near the river. I wondered if it was paralyzed. Then it raised its head and glowered right at me [*SNAP*] and flew away. I was happy enough just to get a photo of the acorn. Very few native oaks are making acorns in this drought summer, and they are fundamental to the food chain. (Santa Ynez River, 23 July 2004)
CommonNames: Golden Paper Wasp; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 4723d_4723-224.jpg
Notes: 8098391 I believe this is an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae), maybe immature. Look under its head at the backward-pointing hard-tipped poker it uses to drain its victims! (How can it bend so sharp without kinking??) The plant is interesting too. It's an insect gall on the native shrub Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the dry flood plane of our almost-dry river. I guess these galls are caused by Gall Midge (Cecidomyiidae) flies that also infect other composite shrubs with similar bud-like growths. (Santa Ynez River, 23 July 2004)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Palmer's Goldenbush; Gall Midge

File.Name: 4723e_4723-250.jpg
Notes: 8098296 This very dark - almost black - Damselfly (Zygoptera, maybe a Dancer (Argia?) likes to rest on rocks near the water. I saw several of these, and they let me get close as long as I moved very slowly. You can't sneak up on a critter with eyes like that! It was watching every move. I've learned I have to keep my camera up in the air as I approach almost all insects. Raising the camera spooks them every time. Those bristly leg hairs are for catching prey in the air. (Santa Ynez River, 23 July 2004)

File.Name: 4723f_4723-292.jpg
Notes: 8098266 Little blue Damselfly (Zygoptera, maybe a Bluet (Enallagma?) resting on native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) away from the river. I have a book, but I still can't tell these little dragonflies apart. The wing anchors remind me of RC helicopters. I can almost imagine little servos and gears making them work. But they sure are aware of everything! (Santa Ynez River, 23 July 2004)

File.Name: 4724g_4722-211.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8195266 This is not our usual Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus), but it must be a close kin in the Vespidae family. The flowers are native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2004)

File.Name: 4724a_4724-005.jpg
Notes: 8103289 Tiny Wild Bee (Apoidea) hugging a single anther of a summer bulb flower in our garden. This beautiful plant was a gift, we don't know what it is. Maybe a species Gladiolus? (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2004)

File.Name: 4724b_4724-012.jpg
Notes:
8103271 "After the party..." This is a flower of native Weed`s Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus). They have a short time flowering in the heat of summer. Petals and sepals are now twisted and dry, stamens are empty of pollen, but there's still work to do. The ovary in the middle is starting to swell with seeds for next year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2004)

I have more photos of the open flower in the archives, eg

   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8014775

File.Name: 4724c_4722-211.jpg
Notes:
8103239 This is not our usual Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus), but it must be a close kin in the Vespidae family. The flowers are native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. This shrub is one of a series of late blooming composite flowers on the mostly dry river bed that fuel a lot of insects through the dry summer and into autumn. This is one of my favorite native plants. It's just starting to bloom when everything else is done. I don't think I've ever seen so much insect activity on anything else. (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2004)

Even in a drought year, there's always something flowering. (Though I wonder in this drought summer when everything is early.) I believe insects and flowers (etc.) evolve together, so I reckon the whole ecosystem needs these "late bloomers" to keep the insects alive over the dry summer for next year's wildflowers. I have a stumper about this at:

   http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/12oct01.html.

File.Name: 4724d_4722-208.jpg
Notes:
8103143 I think this is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae) working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. I have another photo and more info at:

   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8093574

This fly has unusual flap-like structures behind the wings. (Modified halteres?) The backlit abdomen looks like it's full of golden honey! (Santa Ynez River, 22 July 2004)

File.Name: 4724e_4723-288.jpg
Notes:
8103084 Deer remains on the river flood plain, well away from the trail. I'm pretty sure this is a Mountain Lion aka Couger (Puma concolor) kill. Everything was here, including the skull a few feet away. It's not fresh. There is grass growing up through the skeleton, which dates it to last year, at least. You can see there are missing ribs on the top side, and I believe this is typical of how they feed. I know there are lions in this area. We have friends a mile or two away from this place who have seen a big cat jump off their roof! We hear them at home too. Our heroic dog Mojo was seriously sliced a few years ago defending us. (Santa Ynez River, 23 July 2004)

I've been taking lots of insect photos lately, and darned if there isn't a blue Damselfly (Zygoptera) watching me take this photo. Can you find it?!

File.Name: 4724f_4723-262.jpg
Notes:
8102983 Yes we do have Beaver (Castor canadiensis) along the Santa Ynez River, but they are not native. (No jokes please.) I believe they were introduced by the Forest Service in the early 1900s. We also have introduced Oppossums and Knobcone Pines in the mountains dating back to about the the same time. Someone had a different idea of what these mountains should be!

I've never seen a live beaver along the river, though I often see their tracks and dams and slides and circumstantial evidence like this. Cutworms for trees! This is a six inch thick Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii), that was gnawed through again further up the trunk. Why? I sometimes see river dams made of stones. Could beaver do that? Beaver-sign is a good indicator of perennial water even in drought years like this. (Santa Ynez River, 23 July 2004)

BTW, I think the first time I used a chainsaw, I cut straight through like this and pinched my blade. Then I learned to make a notch on the falling side and cut through on the opposite side. I hope that beaver was careful at the end!

File.Name: 4725a_4725-049.jpg
Notes: 8108299 This is the first photo in a series of five. This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is working a pretty flower of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden. It seems normal enough, but look closer at those chains of black and yellow things on its toes. Milkweed is an aggresive flower that almost trapped this bee for good! See the [Previous] photos for the rest of the story. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2004)

File.Name: 4725b_4725-059.jpg
Notes:
8108247 This is the second photo in a series of five. Milkweed seems commonplace, but milkweed flowers (family Asclepiadaceae) are second only to orchids for complexity. This is a little Wild Bee (Apoidea) on a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden. The photo gives a good view of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2004)

Milkweed flowers have sepals and petals like most flowers, but there is another ring of unusual structures above that called the CORONA. There is sweet nectar in the cups that make up the HOODS. The HORNS are slippery to guide a visiting bee's foot down between into the STIGMATIC SLIT. When the bee tries to get free - if it can! - it will pick up the POLLINARIUM GLAND aka CORPUSCULUM which is attached to two POLLINIA or pollen sacks by two thread-like TRANSLATOR fibers arranged like saddlebags. It's hard for the insect to get free, because the horns block passage of anything that large. When/if the pollinator does get free, they carry the male POLLINARIA to the female STIGMA of another flower and pollination happens. Whew! And it seems hard for us to get a date!

*Gulp* I'm on summer vacation, but this sure feels like teacher stuff. I can't resist sharing when I learn something new, I hope I got it right. Try a web search on "milkweed pollination" to learn a lot more. Milkweeds are especially developed in Africa, and there is incredible diversity.

File.Name: 4725c_4725-053.jpg
Notes: 8108043 This is the third photo in a series of five. This struggling Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is caught by a flower of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden. You can see yellow milkweed "pollinia" aka pollen sacks hanging from almost every leg. I've found broken-off bee legs stuck in native milkweed flowers. Native bumblebees are much larger, so they can pull free from the flower's trap. These introduced Honey Bees sometimes get stuck. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2004)

File.Name: 4725d_4725-054.jpg
Notes: 8108008 This is the fourth photo in a series of five. This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is caught in a flower of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden. By now she is really pissed off! She has the milkweed "pollinia" aka pollen sacks hanging from almost every leg. Look close and you can see that she is trapped by the "horns" of the flower. It almost looks like the horns closed tighter after she took the pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2004)

File.Name: 4725e_4725-056.jpg
Notes: 8107961 This is the last photo in a series of five. This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is caught in a flower of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden. Just after I got this photo, she managed to get away by crawling over other nearby flowers to get some leverage. It's interesting that she has fewer pollen bags on her toes now than when she started. But I don't think that counts as pollination unless the pollen is transfered to a different plant. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2004)

File.Name: 4725f_4725-040.jpg
Notes: 8107923 This is something weird. I've been checking the Milkweed Aphids (maybe Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on the Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden everyday since I noticed them. There are fewer and fewer every day. (See http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8081968 and [Previous].) Today this "thing" appeared under a leaf of the same plant. It seems to have wing veins like a potential moth or butterfly. It reminds me of the monolith in the movie 2001! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2004)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 4726g_4725-037.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8208394 Nickle-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider wrapping up a little Wasp (probably a Vespidae) caught in its web. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2004)
ScientificName: Araneidae) spider wrapping up a little Wasp (probably a Vespidae
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) spider wrapping up a little Wasp (probably a Vespidae
Families: Araneidae) spider wrapping up a little Wasp (probably a Vespidae

File.Name: 4726a_4726-075.jpg
Notes:
8114194 Crab Spider (Thomisidae?) perched on a flower of Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in our garden. The yellow ray flowers are open, but the dark inner disk flowers are just starting to release a few pollen grains. Note how this spider has double legs on the left, but it's missing the second leg on the right. These little preditors sometimes tackle BIG prey and pay for it! (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2004)

I spent most of the day in town trying to run a few errands that took forever. I'd rather be out in the woods with my camera!

File.Name: 4726b_4726-066.jpg
Notes: 8114140 Sometimes a fly is not just "a fly". I think this is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae) resting in our garden. These pretty flies are parasites that lay their eggs on aphids and other garden pests, but the adults visit flowers for nectar. This one seems dusted with golden pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2004)

File.Name: 4726c_4725-038.jpg
Notes: 8114116 Nickle-size Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider wrapping up a little Wasp (probably a Vespidae) caught in its web. I guess this is fair play. Many wasps prey on spiders, but sometimes the spiders get their own. Look at that spider's eyes! My book says eight eyes, but I only count seven. These are simple eyes - just pixels - they can't make much of an image, but maybe it's enough to sense direction? I imagine spiders live in a world of vibrations, sensed through their web, maybe as rich as music. Does the doomed prey sing to the spider's sensors as it struggles? My spidy sense is tingling! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2004)
ScientificName: Araneidae) spider wrapping up a little Wasp (probably a Vespidae
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) spider wrapping up a little Wasp (probably a Vespidae
Families: Araneidae) spider wrapping up a little Wasp (probably a Vespidae

File.Name: 4726d_4722-204.jpg
Notes: 8114051 It's a party! Tiny Wild Bees (Apoidea) sharing brunch on a Floribunda Rose in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2004)

File.Name: 4726e_4719-119.jpg
Notes: 8114018 Tiny new Mexican Hat (Ratibida columnaris) flowers in the garden. Hot cross buns? Drill bits? The florets will split open along these yellow lines to make tiny disk flowers on this composite. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2004)

File.Name: 4726f_4724-015.jpg
Notes: 8113957 A lizard turned inside out! This is the shed skin of a Blue Belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis?). Lizards grow, but their skin doesn't, so they have to move into a larger suit now and then. The opening is the vent - you know, down there. One of its hind legs is visible on top of the photo, the tail is to the right. (Santa Ynez River, 24 July 2004)
CommonNames: Blue Belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard

File.Name: 4727a_4726-160.jpg
Notes:
8119464 Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo - maybe Apodemia mormo virgulti?) butterfly visiting a flower of the well-named Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in our garden. It looks so delicate on this intricate flower as it sips sweet nectar from the hood in the corona of this flower. Meals should be always this beautiful! Bees sometimes get trapped by these milkweed flowers, but this pretty little butterfly seems to have no trouble, even though its middle leg is stuck right in the stigmatic slit between the hoods. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2004)

I have some info on the anatomy of milkweed flowers at

   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8108247

and photos of a trapped bee [Next] and [Previous] to that.
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 4727b_4726-146.jpg
Notes:
8119424 I've been chasing this furry Wild Bee (Apoidea) around the garden all week. It hovers like a Hover Fly aka Flower Fly (family Syrphidae), but it is definately a bee. I'm still trying to get a photo of it hovering - but it's just as fast in the air as hummingbirds and dragonflies! *Sigh* I'll keep trying.

Even grounded on this Mexican Hat (Ratibida columnaris) flower in the garden, it's a remarkable bee. It seems to be collecting pollen with its proboscis, face, hind legs, AND abdomen. What beautiful eyes! I call it a "Hover Bee". (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2004)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Hover Fly; Flower Fly; Mexican Hat

File.Name: 4727c_4726-103.jpg
Notes: 8119381 Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working a flower of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. They look dull and camouflaged with wings folded up, but you see those startling eyespots on the inner wings when they open up. I mostly see them flying back and forth just to taunt me. But this one stopped for a snack on the dry river bed. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2004)

File.Name: 4727d_4726-137.jpg
Notes: 8119351 This sand-colored Grasshopper (family Acrididae) on the dry sandy riverbed saw me trying to sneak up with my camera, but it didn't instantly fly away like all the others. Instead it climbed up on this river rock and posed for a while. The lesson is that individual insects in the same species might act differently. Keep looking for one that is not as shy as the rest. That amazing body looks sculpted with super-fine detail, like a micro-chip. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2004)

File.Name: 4727e_4726-130.jpg
Notes: 8119299 This pretty blue Damselfly (Zygoptera, maybe a Bluet (Enallagma?) would not perch for me, so I tried to catch it in the air. It was carefully inspecting every leaf on a little Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) seedling near a rare section of running water at our mostly dry river, moving constantly. Look on the stem for the ant tending a colony of aphids. The damselfly is checking this out, but it kept flying. What is it looking for? (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2004)

File.Name: 4727f_4726-087.jpg
Notes:
8119269 Julie found this huge finger-sized Pine Sawyer (Ergates spiculatus) beetle hiding under a chair cushion on the porch this morning. It's our largest beetle, though the California Prionus (Prionus californicus) is nearly as large. Both beetles come from 2-3 inch white grubs that I sometimes find hidden when I split firewood logs. This one favors pine, the other favors oak. We don't have pines at home, but there are native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) up the canyon not too far away. (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2004)

Sometimes these giant beetles bang on the window screens at night. They are very strong and hard to hold, though it never actually bit. It didn't like the sunlight hen I tried to get photos. I hoped it would fly away, but it ran (faster than I could focus) to a shady blackberry bramble and disappeared. Note the strange mouth parts hanging below it's jaws. I'll post another closer shot at Bugsncritters when I finish these photos.
CommonNames: Pine Sawyer; California Prionus; Coulter Pines
ScientificName: Ergates spiculatus; Prionus californicus; Pinus coulteri
associatedTaxa: Ergates spiculatus; Prionus californicus; Pinus coulteri

File.Name: 4728a_4728-166.jpg
Notes: 8124444 Some kind of Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae) working flowers of Dusty Miller (Senecio cineraria) in the garden. The plant was one of those gifts I get as a teacher in a small pot that is really happy here and got big. The are many kinds of skipper butterflies that are really hard to tell apart. I'll "skip her" proper name, but she is a beautiful critter to admire in the garden! (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2004)

File.Name: 4728b_4728-176.jpg
Notes: 8124357 Immature Long-Horned Grasshopper (family Tettigoniidae) - maybe a Katydid? - munching flowers on an Oregano (Origanum vulgaris?) plant in our garden. If this is a katydid, the immature critters are much more colorful than the adults! Those antennae that go out of the photo are as long as body and hind-legs combined. The [Previous] photo shows an immature Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae). (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2004)
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; Katydid; Oregano; Short-Horned Grasshopper

File.Name: 4728c_4728-180.jpg
Notes: 8124311 Immature Grasshopper (family Acrididae) on a leaf in our garden. You can tell it's *almost* mature by those short wings. I didn't notice when I took the photo, but it's missing a hind leg. That's pretty important for a hopper like this! (This one crawled away from me instead of jumping. What if it jumped?!) Here's a stumper: will that missing leg reappear when it molts again to become an adult? (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2004)

File.Name: 4728d_4728-178.jpg
Notes: 8124256 "Me and my shadow..." Pretty little Wasp (Hymenoptera) resting on a spent Foribunda Rose flower in the garden. I don`t know what this is. It seems to have an extra segment between thorax and abdomen. Is it scratching its head with that right-front leg? (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2004)

File.Name: 4728e_4727-097.jpg
Notes: 8124200 Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly in alert position, with head high and upper wings showing those startling eyespots. (Why so many??) They look dull and camouflaged with their wings folded up, until you get too close. Usually they just fly away, but this one made a statement. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2004)

File.Name: 4728f_4726-085.jpg
Notes:
8124158 "Now open wide and say b_l_a_h!" I'm holding this huge finger-sized Pine Sawyer (Ergates spiculatus) beetle in my left hand and trying to frame the photo with camera in right hand. It never did bite me, but it sure is threatening. I don't understand all those strange dangling mouth-parts. (San Marcos Pass, 26 July 2004)

I have another photo of this impressive beetle at

   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8119269

File.Name: 4729g_4729-171.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8226958 Life is beautiful... This is a Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly visiting flowers of some species Oregano (Origanum) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2004)

File.Name: 4729b_4729-195.jpg
Notes: 8130262 Butterflies get all the glory, but there are also diurnal (or daytime) moths that visit flowers rather than dive-bomb the lights at night. All moths have slender antennae without knobs ("apical clubs") at the end. This little moth is working the flowers of Pennyroyal (Mentha pulegium) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2004)

File.Name: 4729a_4729-200.jpg
Notes:
8130068 I got too close to this immature Grasshopper (family Acrididae), and he hopped away. I saw where he landed and waited with camera ready until he poked his head up over this cluster of Pennyroyal (Mentha pulegium) flowers in our garden to check me out. Got him! Why are insects so camera shy?! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2004)

I hope these comments make sense tonight. I've been uploading and listening to politics on TV in the oher room at the same time. We need priorities! (And C-Span will play it again.)

File.Name: 4729c_4729-212.jpg
Notes: 8129945 Slender Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) - maybe a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracantus) or kin? - waiting on a flower head of Yarrow (Achillea) in our garden. It's a gnarly preditor in the Hemiptera "True Bug" family waiting on a flower head of Yarrow (Achillea) in our garden. Note the forelegs poised in the air to grasp its prey and that strong sucking mouthpart folded under its head ready to inject a powerful venom and suck it dry. Nature is beautiful, but not easy. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2004)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Four-spurred Assassin Bug; Yarrow; Yarrow
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Zelus tetracantus; Achillea; Hemiptera; Achillea
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Zelus tetracantus; Achillea; Hemiptera; Achillea

File.Name: 4729d_4729-223.jpg
Notes: 8129856 Little Flower Fly (family Syrphidae) with Popeye-like rear legs visiting a flower of Yarrow (Achillea) in our garden. The fly is already dusted with pollen, and it is now working a little disk-flower with its proboscis that couldn't possibly dust its back. It's an important factor in "pollination ecology" whether an insect stays with one species or shops around. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2004)

File.Name: 4729e_4729-210.jpg
Notes: 8129813 Incoming Bumblebee (Bombus) with loaded pollen baskets approaching an Iris/Gladiolus relative in our garden. I believe the flower is Montbretia (Crocosmia crocosmiiflora). (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2004)

File.Name: 4729f_4729-209.jpg
Notes: 8129728 Sweet nature... This is a close shot of the corona of a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flower in our garden. The red petals and sepals are all down below. These hoods and horns above the petals are unique to milkweeds. You can see the sunlight relecting from the sweet nectar that fills the cup-like hoods. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2004)

File.Name: 4730a_4730-270.jpg
Notes: 8135255 This looks like just another of my insect photos, but study it for a moment to appreciate this amazing creature. It has the body and dark wings and arching abdomen of a wasp, but that is definately the face of a fly. (I didn't photoshop it!) Maybe this wasp-mimic fly is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae), but I don't know. It's visiting new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. This composite shrub grows on the summer-dry flood plain of the river. I've never seen so much insect diversity on any other plant. I reckon this (and other) summer-bloomers are necessary to keep the insects alive so there will be wildflowers next year. (Santa Ynez River, 30 July 2004)

File.Name: 4730b_4730-278.jpg
Notes:
8135215 This slender Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) has skewered its beetle prey with it's proboscis to suck it dry. It didn't like me getting close with my camera, so it flew off with prey hanging just after I snapped this. native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica). (Santa Ynez River, 30 July 2004)

Depth of field becomes about as thick as a playing card when you get near 1:1 magnification like this. I need shutter speed to stop motion, and I need f/stops for depth of field, and low ASA speed for quality. It was windy today at the river, so I didn't have much choice. Is this shot dreamy or a nightmare? I like it.

File.Name: 4730c_4730-243.jpg
Notes: 8135153 Remarkable fly on new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. I usually call all these strange flies Tachinid Flies (family Tachinidae), but I'm not sure about this one. It caught my eye for the way it holds its wings out flat after landing. The bare abdomen and broad halteres and the black comb-like structure on it's rear legs are distinctive. I have photos of similar flies in the recent archives. (Santa Ynez River, 30 July 2004)

File.Name: 4730d_4730-229.jpg
Notes: 8135111 Very punk-looking Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, maybe the Spiney Tachina Fly (Paradejeania rutiloides?) working native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) flowers that are starting to bloom on the dry river flats. This bristly fly is a classic preditor/parasite that lays its eggs on caterpillars and other insect larvae so they can hatch and devour them alive. Grisly story, but not as nasty as insecticides. (If the caterpillars had their own flog, they'd tell the story differently!) These beneficial flies lay their eggs on larvae for protein food, but the adults visit flowers for pollen and nectar energy food. (Santa Ynez River, 30 July 2004)
CommonNames: Tachinid Fly; Spiney Tachina Fly; Scale Broom

File.Name: 4730e_4730-233.jpg
Notes: 8135076 Furry bee-like Hover Fly aka Flower Fly (family Syrphidae) landing on a flower cluster of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. These pretty flies do real VTOL (Vertical Takeoff and Landing) maneuvers. They fly more like helicopters than airplanes. (Santa Ynez River, 30 July 2004)
CommonNames: Hover Fly; Flower Fly; Scale Broom

File.Name: 4731a_4730-249.jpg
Notes:
8140350 I believe this is an American Rubyspot (Hetaerina americana) damselfly, in the Pond Damsel (Calopterygidae) family of the order Odonata. I had to wade very slowly and carefully with my new $$$ camera. He watched my every step. This is along one of the few sections of the river that is still flowing in this drought summer. (Don't ask - find it yourself!) (Santa Ynez River, 30 July 2004)

I'm busy, we're getting ready for an Oregon trip in a couple of days.

File.Name: 4731b_4730-274.jpg
Notes: 8140278 Here is another yellow and black wasp, but that extra-long abdomen is really different. Like the [Previous] wasps, it is working the flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. I've never seen it anywhere else. (Santa Ynez River, 30 July 2004)

File.Name: 4731c_4730-259.jpg
Notes: 8140259 Another pretty large wasp like the [Previous] two, but this one is mostly orange. I guess this is another wasp in the family Vespidae? It is working the flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. Each of these large wasps has it's own way of living. I find them visiting this flower, but I don't know what they do the rest of the time. There is so much to learn even in our own backyard! (Santa Ynez River, 30 July 2004)

File.Name: 4731d_4730-264.jpg
Notes: 8140225 Here's another large yellow and black wasp that's different from the [Previous]. I guess this wasp is family Vespidae? It is working the flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. (Santa Ynez River, 30 July 2004)

File.Name: 4731e_4730-256.jpg
Notes: 8140209 There are too many kinds of these large yellow and black wasps, I can't keep the color patterns straight. I guess this wasp is family Vespidae? It looks painted. It reminds me of Imperial Storm Troopers. (Santa Ynez River, 30 July 2004)

File.Name: 4731f_4730-237.jpg
Notes:
8140188 Another shot of this furry bee-like Hover Fly aka Flower Fly (family Syrphidae) at rest on a flower cluster of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. (Santa Ynez River, 30 July 2004)

I have another photo of this pretty fly at

   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8135076
CommonNames: Hover Fly; Flower Fly; Scale Broom

File.Name: 4802c_4801-039.jpg
Notes: 8145457 Lots of bees! This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) hive is located in a crack at the base of a huge Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree at my parents' house. The hive has been there for years without trouble. The bees in focus are providing the ventilation system (with their wings) for the rest of the hive inside. This is when it's nice to have a macro lens with some telephoto reach like the 105mm lens on my Nikon! (Santa Monica Mountains, 1 August 2004)

File.Name: 4802d_4801-030.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Composite (Asteraceae) daisy-like flower in Grammy's garden. I don't know what it is. Composite flowers are all basically the same with different combinations of disk and ray flowers (and pappus, phyllaries, etc.), but there are so many different variations of the basic plan! (Santa Monica Mountains, 1 August 2004)

File.Name: 4802e_4801-031.jpg
Notes: Seed head of Matilija Poppy (Romneya) in Grammy's garden. There are two native species in the Southern California backcountry. I don't know which these garden varieties are. This is the seed head after the huge "fried-egg flowers" are done. (Santa Monica Mountains, 1 August 2004)

File.Name: 4802f_4801-033.jpg
Notes: My Mom's lilies in "Grammy's garden" have survived the deer attacks so far. We visited yesterday. I took a bunch of arty closeups, but sometimes simpler is better. I wish I could upload the sweet fragrance. (Santa Monica Mountains, 1 August 2004)

File.Name: 4820a_4806-041.jpg
Notes:
8248069 We had real rain on the first day of our two-week trip to Oregon, so I was only a little surprised to find this pretty frog resting on a leaf in the woods. I think this is a Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla). Our tree frogs at home don't have those neat yellow stripes. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2004)
We got back home last night after two weeks in Oregon. There was a small wildfire near our house, but everything is ok. I've just begun to sort through the 2,123 photos from the trip. More tomorrow!

File.Name: 4820b_4805-057.jpg
Notes: 8247989 I was face-to-face with this very large green grasshopper, maybe Schistocerca americana or kin. We checked each other out. I wonder what it saw with those amazing striped eyes? (Klamath River, California, 5 August 2004)

File.Name: 4820c_4805-066.jpg
Notes: 8247917 Beautiful blue Damselfly (Zygoptera), maybe a Northern Bluet (Enallagma cyathigerum). The yellow blur in the background is a cluster of Goldenrod (Solidago) flowers. (Klamath River, California, 5 August 2004)

File.Name: 4820d_4805-064.jpg
Notes: 8247849 Looking into a seed head of Salsify aka Oyster Plant (Tragopogon porrifolius. It's an introduced weed with slender flowers and huge dandelion-like seed heads. It's not common at home, but there were many at our favorite highway rest stop. (Klamath River, California, 5 August 2004)

File.Name: 4820e_4806-016.jpg
Notes: 8247798 Flower head of Queen Anne`s Lace (Daucus carota). This is a common weed in Oregon, but it doesn't grow back home near Santa Barbara. I love the geometry of these flowers, I took many photos. This was a gray day with some drizzle. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2004)

File.Name: 4820f_4806-012.jpg
Notes: 8247765 Pretty flowers of Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius). This is a common invasive weed in Oregon, but I only know one colony near home in the mountains behind Santa Barbara. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2004)

File.Name: 4821a_4810-163.jpg
Notes:
8253604 Some kind of Fritillary (genus Speyeria or kin) butterfly working the reduced oak-like flowers of this Bush Chinquapin (Chrysolepis sempivirens) near Lemolo Lake in the high Cascades on the upper stretch of the river. The wings are mostly orange on top. It is different from the fritillary in the [Previous] photo. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 10 August 2004)

Today's photos are all butterfly shots from our recent trip to Oregon.

File.Name: 4821b_4810-135.jpg
Notes: 8253570 Some kind of Fritillary (genus Speyeria or kin) butterfly working composite flowers of Pearly Everlasting (Anaphalis margaritacea). The wings are rich orange and black on top. I took this photo near Lemolo Lake in the high Cascades on the upper stretch of the river. Compare the [Next] photo. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 10 August 2004)

File.Name: 4821c_4811-200.jpg
Notes: 8253529 California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly resting on butterfly-like leaflets of Five-Finger Fern (Adiantum aleuticum). I just about gave up trying to catch this butterfly at rest. I was focusing on the fern when it flew into my photo and posed! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 11 August 2004)

File.Name: 4821d_4810-141.jpg
Notes: 8253492 Very dark Satyr (Satyrinae) butterfly with eye spots, maybe a Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele). It is working composite flowers of Pearly Everlasting (Anaphalis margaritacea). It looks like it is squinting in ecstasy. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 10 August 2004)
CommonNames: Satyr; Great Basin Wood-Nymph; Pearly Everlasting

File.Name: 4821e_4807-106.jpg
Notes: 8253432 Checkered White (family Pieridae) butterfly, maybe a Pine White (Neophasia menapia), working a flower cluster of Queen Anne`s Lace (Daucus carota). (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 7 August 2004)
CommonNames: Checkered White; Pine White; Queen Anne`s Lace

File.Name: 4821f_4806-067.jpg
Notes: 8253380 Some kind of Grass-Skipper (Hesperiinae) butterfly working flowers along the river. I think the plant is Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare. It's an invasive weed, but the local butterflies love it. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2004)

File.Name: 4822c_4809-098.jpg
Notes: 8259220 The old growth forest... These are Pacific Yew (Taxus brevifolia) trees along the North Umpqua Trail heading south from the Wright Creek bridge. You can usually recognize these trees by the accumulation of moss and lichen on the branches. Deciduous maple and ash trees also accumulate these epiphytes, which makes sense since they drop their leaves to let in the winter light. But why are the evergreen yews also so bedecked with life? "Taxol" is an chemical extract from the bark of these trees that is useful for treating certain cancers. I'm glad it's been synthesized so we can leave these wonderful old trees alone. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 9 August 2004)

File.Name: 4822d_4810-168.jpg
Notes: 8259120 The North Umpqua River drops 150+ feet over a basalt lava ledge at Lemolo Falls. It's a steep hike down that seems even steeper coming up. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 10 August 2004)

File.Name: 4823g_4808-162.jpg
Notes:
[mykologos] 8724794 Large Slime Mold (Myxomycophyta) on a branch in the Oregon woods. I poked it, and it feels like a perfectly toasted marshmallow - soft and sticky with a dry skin. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 8 August 2004)

These critters are like nothing else on Earth. Single celled amoeba-like protists live in decaying wood, but they get an "urge to merge" and stream together dissolving their cell-walls to form this multinucleate mass of protoplasm called a "plasmodium". This will soon dry and become puffball-like to release spores that will become more amoeba-like protists. It's a perfect strategy for single-celled organisms to move the relatively huge distance from one decaying log to another.

File.Name: 4823a_4809-024.jpg
Notes: 8264777 I was lucky to notice this perfect spider tucked under a petal of a Royal Rein Orchid aka Mountain Piperia (Piperia transversa) in a dry part of the Oregon woods near our cabin. Hah, I was lucky just to spot the orchid! The flowers are tiny, only a few millimeters across. I used a tripod to get this shot in dim natural light. Camouflage works against photographers and insects. We miss shots all the time, but the unwary insect will become dinner. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 9 August 2004)

File.Name: 4823b_4806-065.jpg
Notes: 8264649 Large Grasshopper (Acrididae) munching a pistil of non-native Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare). This invasive plant is rather common in the Oregon woods, even away from the roads. At least we found it scattered here and there instead of in pure stands that replace the natives. At this time of year, MOST of the flowering plants were non-native. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 6 August 2004)

File.Name: 4823c_4808-240.jpg
Notes: 8264555 I believe this is an introduced Mediterranean Mantid (Iris oratoria) because of the black and white patch under its front leg that is barely visible in this photo. It looks so sublime resting on this Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) sapling, as though it's playing music on the needles like a harp. It really has great camouflage in this dappled light. BTW, it's a "praying mantis" (as in "prayer") not a "preying mantis" (as in dinner), though both names are appropriate. I chased this one around for a while to get this photo. They are wonderful in the air! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 8 August 2004)

File.Name: 4823d_4810-142.jpg
Notes: 8264533 It's a beautiful world these woodland insects live in. Here is a golden pollen-dusted Long-horned Beetle (Cerambycidae) munching composite flowers of Pearly Everlasting (Anaphalis margaritacea) near Lemolo Lake. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 10 August 2004)

File.Name: 4823e_4805-054.jpg
Notes:
8264522 "Look at those chompers!"

We could hardly eat lunch at the Klamath River I-5 highway rest stop because the Yellow jacket (Vespula pensylvanica or kin) wasps were so aggresive. You have to be careful with every bite when these critters are around! We put a chunk of turkey meat on the other end of the picnic table for them, and they mostly left us alone. (Northern California, 5 August 2004)

File.Name: 4823f_4818-035.jpg
Notes: 8264467 Disturbed Hermit Crab in a marine snail shell at the beach during a minus low tide. "OK, now what?" (Northern California, 18 August 2004)

File.Name: 4824g_4809-043.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 8736551 A Polypore Bracket Fungus exuding droplets of *something* on a fallen log of Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii). I used flash in the dark woods. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 9 August 2004)

File.Name: 4824a_4814-113.jpg
Notes:
8271437 After a week on the wild North Umpqua River in southern Oregon, I felt kind of stranded when we went to visit family and friends in Portland, but I always found something interesting to photograph. This is looking straight down into a new Artichoke (Cynara scolymus) flower in a vacant lot in SE. Only the tips are in focus. Reminds me of a sea anemone. (Portland, Oregon, 14 August 2004)

I found many flowers, but few insects in the city - at least few kinds of insects. I think that`s a stumper. Don`t they go together?

File.Name: 4824b_4810-153.jpg
Notes: 8271347 Elegant Monkshood (Aconitum columbianum) flower in a meadow near Lake Lemolo in the high Cascades. This native plant is related to larkspur/delphinium. They are said to be toxic. We only saw a few. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 10 August 2004)

File.Name: 4824c_4811-251.jpg
Notes: 8271298 Bright red Willow (Salix) roots near the mouth of Susan Creek. Look how the creek picks up color from more roots under water. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 11 August 2004)

File.Name: 4824d_4817-252.jpg
Notes: 8271244 Ripe berry of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus). Back home in the mountains behind Santa Barbara, these plants hide in a few canyons and rarely set fruit. In Oregon and northern California, they form solid hedges reaching over my head. This particular berry from the coast was thin but tasty. Other berries from the High Sierras are rather bland, but always welcome. (Oregon Coast, 17 August 2004)

File.Name: 4824e_4811-245..jpg
Notes: 8271193 I was surprised to find this Scarlet Monkeyflower Mimulus cardinalis or kin) growing along the river in southern Oregon. That little beetle is interested too. These Oregon flowers have more color in the throat. Is this something else? (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 11 August 2004)

File.Name: 4824f_4818-059.jpg
Notes: 8271091 Strange flower of a native Yellow Pond-Lily (Nuphar lutea ssp. polysepala) flower in an old mill pond near the coast. The central structure is the "stigmatic disk" with fertile female lines radiating from the center, surrounded by male stamens. I wish we had more time on our trip to explore the remarkable marshes and lagoons on the California "Redwood Coast". (Northern California, 18 August 2004)

File.Name: 4825g_4807-091.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 8747769 Bird's Nest Fungus (Nidula niveotomentosa or kin) fruiting bodies on a twig in the Oregon woods. That's a fir needle on the right to give a sense of scale. Sometimes the little cups have "eggs" of spores inside, but those photos didn't come out in this dim woodland light. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 7 August 2004)

File.Name: 4825a_4815-197.jpg
Notes:
8277462 Eye to eye with a Skipper (Hesperiidae) butterfly working Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii?) flowers in the city. Looks like a bulldog - pissed because I'm stalking! (Portland, Oregon, 15 August 2004)

Today's photos are more butterfly shots from our recent trip to Oregon. I have many more photos from our trip, but it's time to bring my photos back home. Yipes, school meetings start next Monday!

File.Name: 4825b_4816-226.jpg
Notes:
8277396 Satyr Comma (Polygonia satyrus) butterfly in the woods near the river. It didn't seem interested in any flowers, but I managed to catch it resting on a blackberry leaf. (Umpqua River, Oregon, 16 August 2004)

Everyone knows a few distinctive butterflies, but I've always been bewildered by the multitude of orange butterflies with black and maybe white or blue spots like this and the two [Previous] photos and many more in the archives. (Don't even mention Skippers and Blues!) It really helps me to get photos and then flip through the pages in Jeffrey Glassberg's "Butterflies Through Binoculars" book. (There are East and West editions for the US.) The exact color patterns really do match when you look close. I've learned a lot this summer by taking and studying these photos!

File.Name: 4825c_4811-202.jpg
Notes: 8277239 California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly resting on a native Honeysuckle (Lonicera) vine. The butterfly's wings are very dark underneath. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 11 August 2004)

File.Name: 4825d_4817-250.jpg
Notes: 8277177 Beautiful West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly working flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in woodland near the beach in southern Oregon. "Vanessa annabella" is such a beautiful name! We have both butterfly and shrub back home near Santa Barbara, but the shrubs look different. (Northern California, 17 August 2004)
ScientificName: Vanessa annabella) butterfly working flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis
associatedTaxa: Vanessa annabella) butterfly working flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis

File.Name: 4825e_4813-044.jpg
Notes: 8277068 I reckon this is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly visiting flowers of Purple Loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria). This is a characteristic pose when disturbed. The butterfly lands with head down and "saws" its closed wings back and forth to draw attention to the bright eye-spots and horns on its hindwings. This species is a "Scrub-Hairstreak" that usually keeps its wings slightly open. This introduced plant has pretty flowers, but it`s an invasive weed in wetlands like this urban river bank along the Willamette River. (Portland, Oregon, 13 August 2004)

File.Name: 4825f_4808-227.jpg
Notes: 8276925 I believe this is a Common Checkered Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly working flowers of a Teasel (Dipsacus sativus) in an open disturbed area along a powerline easement in the woods. It usually kept wings closed at rest. It is quite dark on top. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 8 August 2004)

File.Name: 4826a_4826-114.jpg
Notes:
8284966 Spider Wasp (Pompilidae) with still-kicking prey that looks like a little Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider. I often see similar spiders sitting in their perfect webs in the woods. I think of these wasps as "Ninja Imperial Storm Troopers". They have sculpted metallic bodies and dark iridescent blue-black colors. They flick their wings nervously when they land. This wasp landed right in front of me carrying the unfortunate spider with its chompers. It only stayed for a moment and flew away. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2004)

This was a battle between mighty predators. Compare my photo at:
  http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8114116

It's good to travel, but I notice more at home!

File.Name: 4826b_4826-145.jpg
Notes: 8284775 I'm on a quest to get the perfect photo of the upper wings of a male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly! This isn't it. It's not quite sharp, and it doesn't show the amazing iridescence of the "dog face" pattern on the upper forewing. It's still pretty cool to see the face and capture TWO males in one shot fighting over the Fuschia flowers in our garden! If you look close, you might notice the incoming butterfly has his proboscis extended in a tight curl. They tangled and both left. This is the California State Butterfly for good reason, but the beautiful males always hide the glory by folding their wings when they land, and it's so hard to catch them in flight. I'll keep trying. These butterflies were common early this summer and then disappeared. I reckon this is a new brood. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2004)

File.Name: 4826c_4825-107.jpg
Notes: 8284632 Beautiful little Dotted Blue (Euphilotes enoptes ssp. bernardino, but compare E. battoides) butterfly visiting composite Dusty Miller (Senecio cineraria) flowers in the garden. This tiny butterfly is dark gray on top, not at all blue. It's shy. I've only seen it a couple of times, and I was lucky to get this shot. Said to mature on California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) which is nearby, but there's much to learn. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2004)
CommonNames: Dotted Blue; Dusty Miller; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Euphilotes enoptes; bernardino; E. battoides; Senecio cineraria; Eriogonum fasciculatum
associatedTaxa: Euphilotes enoptes; bernardino; E. battoides; Senecio cineraria; Eriogonum fasciculatum

File.Name: 4826d_4823-117.jpg
Notes: 8284555 Perfect wasp-mimic Hover Fly (Syrphidae) cleaning its mouth parts in the garden. You have to look very close at the face and antennae to notice that this is a fly and not a wasp. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2004)

File.Name: 4826e_4825-106.jpg
Notes: 8284508 I guess this is an immature Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, maybe Apiomerus?) in our garden. You can barely see it's sucking mouthpart folded back under its narrow head. One reason insects are so hard to learn is that they go through many different forms as they grow to become the adults we find in reference books and collections. Much (most?) of their lives is unknown. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2004)

File.Name: 4826f_4824-039..jpg
Notes: 8284448 Just in case you haven't looked at a spider's butt yet today, here it is! This is the rear end of a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a leaf of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden. The spider was on another leaf a moment ago, and somehow jumped or teleported to this leaf. (It's a problem with digital SLR's that you have to look through the viewfinder to frame a shot, so you sometimes miss the action.) However the spider got here, it left a web-trail behind. This photo shows the spinnerets and the surprising terraced structure of its abdomen. It looks like the trailing web wraps around the spinnerets rather than come from them, and maybe that's a clue. These spiders don't usually make webs. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2004)

File.Name: 4827a_4827-176.jpg
Notes:
8290468 Awesome Tarantula Hawk (Pepsis) wasp getting a nectar energy boost from native flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) before she goes back to work hunting California Tarantula (Aphonopelma reversum) spiders along the river. She will find and subdue her prey with a toxic sting. Then she will drag (or carry?) her prey to a burrow and lay her eggs on the unfortunate paralyzed spider. Think about the movie Alien.

This is our largest wasp, one of the biggest in the world. It's probably about as big at it looks on your monitor. My books say two-inch body length, but she looks bigger in the air. They fly like dragonflies or almost hummingbirds. This one had to straddle two stems to reach the flowers! (Santa Ynez River, 27 August 2004)

All of todays photos are from the dry flats along the Santa Ynez River. The river is mostly dry in this drought year, but you can still find clear spring-fed running water if you look for it. These photos all feature the remarkable Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub and a few more composites that flower on the dry river flats in late summer. They might be under water next year! I reckon these late flowers keep the insects alive in the hard times so they can pollinate the spring flowers next year and keep the ecosystem alive. See my "Late Bloomers" stumper at:
   http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/12oct01.html
CommonNames: Tarantula Hawk; Scale Broom; California Tarantula; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Pepsis; Lepidospartum squamatum; Aphonopelma reversum; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Pepsis; Lepidospartum squamatum; Aphonopelma reversum; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 4827b_4827-191.jpg
Notes: 8290290 I reckon this is a female Metallic Sweat Bee (Agapostemon) working composite flowers of native Cudweed-Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia, aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia - which feels so good to say!) on the dry wash along the river. This tiny bee has a brilliant metallic green body. She has almost loaded the pollen-collecting brushes (scopae) on her hind legs that the males lack. (Santa Ynez River, 27 August 2004)
ScientificName: Agapostemon; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; scopae
associatedTaxa: Agapostemon; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; scopae

File.Name: 4827c_4827-187.jpg
Notes:
8290206 This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) has captured a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae) - the same fly as the [Previous] photo, that face. The plant is the same native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) featured in all of today's photos. There is no safe place. It's the wrong promise. (Santa Ynez River, 27 August 2004)

BTW, if you count appendages, you will notice this spider has four legs on this side, but that extra "pedipalp" clutching the fly is almost a fifth leg!
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Tachinid Fly; Scale Broom

File.Name: 4827d_4827-169.jpg
Notes: 8290121 Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae) working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. These "muscoid" or "higher flies" (suborder Brachycera) have such interesting faces that have something to do with how they emerge from their larval pupa stage. The details are disturbing. (Santa Ynez River, 27 August 2004)

File.Name: 4827e_4827-183.jpg
Notes:
8290044 This pretty fly was working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river, but it took a break on this bare stem and let me get close. Maybe it`s a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae), but I'm not sure. It has bristles on its thorax and a large "post-scutellum lobe", but the abdomen is bare. (Santa Ynez River, 27 August 2004)

I have more photos of this interesting fly. Start with:
  http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8093574

File.Name: 4827f_4827-177.jpg
Notes: 8289967 Cute little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) working composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. The furry body, short antennae, and dark blotches on the wings are all characteristic. (Santa Ynez River, 27 August 2004)

File.Name: 4828a_4828-045.jpg
Notes: 8296292 Crab Spider (Thomisidae) poised on a flower head of composite Dusty Miller (Senecio cineraria) in the garden. Those dark stamens and the dark claws... that's camouflage! There is no safety in nature - or home. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2004)

File.Name: 4828b_4828-031.jpg
Notes:
8296254 Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flowers in our garden. Narrow depth of field is usually a problem taking macro photos, but sometimes it`s perfect. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2004)

How does this butterfly control its proboscis aka tongue?? The proboscis is actually formed of two separate parts zipped together, so it can`t be muscles. Something with hydraulics?

File.Name: 4828c_4828-009.jpg
Notes: 8296189 This is ecstasy - Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) wrapped around the intimate parts of a Crookneck summer squash(Cucurbita pepo?) flower in the garden. There must be nectar down there at the base of the flower. The bee picks up pollen as she goes around and around for the good stuff. Honeybees are workers. We all work, but is your workplace this beautiful? (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2004)

File.Name: 4828d_4828-057.jpg
Notes:
8296112 Ichneumon Wasp (Ichneumonidae) on a window screen. If you watch South Park, you can probably guess which episode involving Cartman I'm thinking of!

The window screen squares show how narrow the depth of field is at this close (almost 1:1) range. Madoak, I still wonder whether these wasps can retract their ovipositor. This looks more like a scabbard than a sword. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2004)

File.Name: 4828e_4828-025.jpg
Notes: 8296039 Single insect egg planted on the apical spine of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. Some insects lay their eggs in masses, others dabble here and there. There's another egg on a broken stalk in the lower right. So many eggs, so few survivors. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2004)

File.Name: 4828f_4828-050.jpg
Notes:
8295965 Lady Anna's Hummingbird hovering in midair. I always chase hummers, but I'm not proud of this photo for two reasons. I used flash in the shade. And we put up a feeder this week because the woods are so dry in this drought summer that the late-season hummingbird flowers like California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) are failing in our canyon. It took three days for the hummers to find it. The wasps were quicker.

I don't really approve of hummingbird feeders. Nutrition aside, they attract the wrong wasps, and the hummers get TOO aggressive, it's disturbing. But this is an extreme year. We also plan to put out a water trough for the bears and lions (etc.) in the canyon since the creek is so dry, even in the secret places.

But look close - you can see yellow pollen deposits on her bill, head, wings, and back! There's not much flowering in the woods now. I guess all that sticky pollen is from the Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) flowers in the garden. That's not a classic hummingbird flower, but I've see the hummers visit. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2004)
CommonNames: Anna's Hummingbird; California Fuschia; Evening Primrose

File.Name: 4829g_4829-126.jpg
Notes: [nature] 8156636 Hummingbird at the feeder dodging wasps, with fill-in flash. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2004)

File.Name: 4829h_4829-063.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8428706 Fresh Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly visiting flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2004)

File.Name: 4829a_4829-065.jpg
Notes:
8302209 Fresh Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly visiting flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. I don't usually find these beauties on flowers. Maybe all those "eyes" scare preditors, but they sure attract me! (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2004)

*Sigh* It's the end of summer. I have a planning meeting at school tomorrow, and classes begin next week. It sure has been fun recording our woods and garden this summer. I'll do my best to keep it going!

All of today's photos feature the remarkable Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub that flowers on the dry river flats in late summer. They might be under water next year! I reckon these late flowers keep the insects alive in the hard times so they can pollinate the spring flowers next year and keep the ecosystem alive. See my "Late Bloomers" stumper at:
   http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/12oct01.html
CommonNames: Common Buckeye; Scale Broom; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Junonia coenia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Junonia coenia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 4829b_4829-082.jpg
Notes: 8302118 Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo - maybe Apodemia mormo virgulti?) butterfly visiting a flower of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. Most butterflies won't let me get close, but this one likes to pose. Those eyes, that proboscis, the antennae, the colors! (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2004)
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 4829c_4829-109.jpg
Notes: 8302075 Female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. I only saw this one, and it was shy about posing. My book says the larvae feed on shrubby mallows, maybe Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) that grows nearby. This one is female. The males have more dark on top. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2004)
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; Scale Broom; Chaparral Mallow
ScientificName: Heliopetes ericetorum; Lepidospartum squamatum; Malacothamnus fasciculatus
associatedTaxa: Heliopetes ericetorum; Lepidospartum squamatum; Malacothamnus fasciculatus

File.Name: 4829d_4829-104.jpg
Notes: 8302014 Little wasp (Vespidae?) working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. I know "working like a bee" (or wasp) now means drudgery, but what a beautiful workplace these creatures have. Protect this great diversity of life at home. If you don't see it, look closer. If still not, find out why. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2004)

File.Name: 4829e_4829-096.jpg
Notes: 8301921 Cute little furry Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) working composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) along the river. I only got one shot before it flew away, but isn't that cool how you can see its hard body through the soft fur! Insects all have a rigid exoskeleton, so every hair must have a structural opening. I see more life now that I'm going out with my camera every day, thanks to fotolog. I'm amazed how many insects I see *just once* and never again. How do they live? What stories? Will we notice if they vanish? Do they (and us) matter? (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2004)

File.Name: 4829f_4829-102.jpg
Notes: 8301810 Spiney Tachina Fly (Paradejeania rutiloides) working native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) flowers on the flood plane along our almost dry river. This is one of the most impressive flies, it looks almost quarter-size with wings out. The larvae are internal parasites of caterpillars (maybe just woolly bears?). The face and mouth parts and bristly body are remarkable. I saw lots of them this morning, but they are camera shy, and it was hard to get a photo. I don't see them anywhere else. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2004)

File.Name: 4831c_4831-173.jpg
Notes:
8315347 Female Allen's Hummingbird (Selasphorus sasin) - or is she a Rufous Hummingbird (S. rufus)? Either way she is green above and reddish-brown ("rufous") on the sides and tail. She is very aggressive! She usually perches in an oak tree nearby to keep watch and make sure the other Anna's and Black-Chinned hummers don't get near.

We're put up a hummingbird feeder for the first time in 20+ years because it's so dry this summer. I really don't approve because:

  • Hummers become too dependent;
  • Feeder attracts bad wasps like yellow jackets;
  • Hummers and wasps become disturbingly aggresive!

It's still a nice photo-op. This female hummer is back-pedalling away from the feeder to avoid the wasps. (See [Previous] photo.) (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2004)

File.Name: 4831d_4831-151.jpg
Notes:
8315228 Who needs the Olympics? Here's a wrestling match between a Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus) on the bottom, and a Yellow Jacket Wasp (Vespula pensylvanica or kin) on top. The arena is the hummingbird feeder we put out in the garden last week. You can see that the wasp on the bottom is about to win this round! (But the other will be back!)

I don't approve of hummingbird feeders. (See [Next] photo.) We haven't had one for 20+ years. But it is so dry this drought summer that the late-season hummingbird flowers have mostly failed or shut down early in our canyon. We plan to use our well to fill a water trough in the mostly dry creek for the bigger critters like lions and bears and racoons. It's a tough summer in central California. (San Marcos Pass, 31 August 2004)

File.Name: 4831e_4830-136.jpg
Notes: 8315095 Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo - maybe Apodemia mormo virgulti?) butterfly. I'm surprised by the flowers, which are on a male Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in the canyon at home. It seems too early for these flowers. I think of them as one of the first NEW flowers rather than one of the last OLD flowers in our skewed California Mediterranean climate rain year that begins with our first fall rain. But here they are in this extreme drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2004)
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Baccharis pilularis
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Baccharis pilularis

File.Name: 4831f_4830-117.jpg
Notes: 8315032 We had a hatch of these LBMs at home. (That's "Little Brown Moths" - in winter this useful name becomes "Little Brown Mushrooms".) They are definately "diurnal" - active in the daytime - but they also collect by the nightlight on the stairs. Maybe they never rest. They flutter constantly while visiting garden flowers like this Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata), even with proboscis engaged. I was lucky to get a photo this sharp. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2004)

File.Name: 4901g_4901-192.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8453162 Male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly feeding on a flower head of Purple Coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) in our garden this morning. I love that almost purple band behind its head. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2004)

File.Name: 4901a_4901-190.jpg
Notes:
8322425 Brilliant butterfly working a brilliant flower. This is a male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly feeding on a flower head of Purple Coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) in our garden this morning. I never noticed before I started f'logging every day, but this is our most common butterfly throughout the summer. What luck - the most beautiful, and they let me get close! There's lots more photos in the archives. I can't resist! (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2004)

Heh - I just noticed there's an insect egg attached to one of the pappus bristles in the lower right. Look for the bright spot!

File.Name: 4901b_4901-211.jpg
Notes: 8322345 Dandelion-like seed head of Prickly Lettuce aka Compass Plant (Lactuca serriola) along our road in the woods. The mature leaves tend to line up east-west to face the sun, hence the name. It's a common weed, but it doesn't take over up here in the Santa Barbara mountains. The flowers aren't much, but each of those pretty seeds is an alien DNA packet built to travel and survive! (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2004)

File.Name: 4901c_4901-219.jpg
Notes: 8322271 Slender Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) waiting on a cluster of male Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) flowers in our canyon. Notice that impressive piercing tool folded under her chin. She's pissed that I'm blowing her cover trying to get a photo. She kept turning to face me and finally took off. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2004)

File.Name: 4901d_4901-230.jpg
Notes: 8322221 This is an Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) - maybe the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica - resting on a new flower cluster of male Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in our canyon. Notice those crab-like forelegs for grabbing victims! It sure looks like its eyes are pointing backwards, maybe because I'm looking over its shoulder. These bugs (Hemiptera) are very well camouflaged. I stood there at least ten minutes before I noticed this preditor right in front of me. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2004)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Phymata pacifica; Baccharis pilularis; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Phymata pacifica; Baccharis pilularis; Hemiptera

File.Name: 4901e_4901-233.jpg
Notes:
8322151 Pretty Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae) visiting new flowers of a male Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in our canyon. These flies are important preditors and parasites in the web of nature. They lay eggs on caterpillars and other larvae that will hatch and consume them. The adults like to visit flowers for a sugar hit. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2004)

*Gulp* I flipped this photo. The fly is really hanging upside down.

File.Name: 4901f_4901-234.jpg
Notes: 8322057 The male Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub has just started to flower in the canyon at home. This shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. The male shrubs produce stamens and pollen. It's one of the few native plants flowering now at the end of this drought year. I was surprised to find this large Grasshopper (Acrididae) getting ready to munch on the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2004)

File.Name: 4902a_4902-245.jpg
Notes:
8328780 This is another "nature-what-am-I" photo-stumper. This is what I saw looking straight down at a leaf of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden. Sure, it's a white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) - color-shifted (I think) to match the leaf.

But it's wrong. No, it's not swimming the crawl! I usually find these spiders hidding in flowers. (See two photos [Previous] and many more in the archive.) So why is this crab spider perched under the edge of the leaf like this? (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2004)

See the answer in the [Previous] photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8328711

File.Name: 4902b_4902-244.jpg
Notes:
8328711 This is the ANSWER to the "nature-what-am-I" stumper in the [Next] photo. I had to lay on my back to get this photo of the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hiding under a leaf of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2004)

Do you see it? This spider is missing both claws on its left side. It can't grab prey like it should (see [Previous] photo), so it's doing the best it can, perched on the edge of the leaf so it can maybe pinch its prey from just one side and pin it against the leaf. It's doing the best it can!

I don't want to start an argument about animal (especially arthropod) intelligence, but this is the spirit of life. This crippled spider is trying to adapt in a new way to make do with what it has. May we all do the same.

It chokes me up.

File.Name: 4902c_4825-082.jpg
Notes: 8328690 Crab Spider (Thomisidae) poised on a flower head of Pennyroyal (Mentha pulegium) in the garden. They hide among the flowers and take on their colors to grab prey with those crab-like claws. Look close and you will probably find many of these spiders hiding in your garden flowers. This photo goes with the next two photos. Look at the newest photo first. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2004)

File.Name: 4902d_4901-239.jpg
Notes: 8328571 A "Heritage Tomato" (Solanum lycopersicum) in the garden. We *finally* have lots of ripe tomatoes, but these beautiful old varieties are running late as our days get noticeably shorter. I don't think they'll make it. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2004)

File.Name: 4902e_4901-225.jpg
Notes: 8328521 It's surprising what happens in an extreme drought year like this. Some plants shrivel, but others thrive. I've never seen so many fruit on our native California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) trees. They are related to avocado and cinnamon in the Laurel (Lauraceae) family. The fruit are REALLY intense, like chewing on the bay leaf in the stew, only more so. The large nuts inside were an important food for native Americans after leaching with water. I haven't tried preparing them. It might be a good year for squirrels at least, but there are very few acorns. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2004)

File.Name: 4902f_4930-147.jpg
Notes: 8328388 New fruit of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) come in colors. Yellow fruit are unripe; red fruit are getting there; and the dark purple fruit are ripe. Ripe means edible for birds and animals. Hermit Thrushes and a new flock of migrating yellow warblers(??) are grabbing them as they ripen. The berries are an important late summer food for wildlife, but I've never been tempted. This plant is cousin of Cascara Sagrada (R. purshiana), a powerful laxative. I sometimes find animal scat made entirely of the coffee bean-like seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2004)

File.Name: 4904g_4904-053.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8474876 It feels like fall. Few butterflies, but lots of diurnal (daytime)moths, like this one on Butterfly Weed(Asclepias tuberosa) in the garden. It's very nervous, fluttering constantly while it feeds. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2004)

File.Name: 4904a_4904-004.jpg
Notes:
8340890 Incoming wasp-mimic Flower Fly (Syrphidae) caught in the air approaching flowers of Clary Sage (Salvia sclarea) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2004)

It's hot, but it feels like autumn in this drought year. The woods are shutting down, even the garden is quiet. I see more life when I look close. Today's photos are all VERY close.

File.Name: 4904b_4904-009.jpg
Notes: 8340832 Pretty little wasp-mimic Flower Fly (Syrphidae) sucking moisture from a wet leaf of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden that we watered this morning. Look close and see how its proboscis divides into two pads at the bottom. What a beautiful world lies just outside the door. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2004)

File.Name: 4904c_4904-035.jpg
Notes: 8340809 Depth of field gets paper-thin when I get this close to an insect with my macro lens. I usually try to focus on the eyes. I missed on this furry golden Bee Fly (Bombyliidae), but I got an intimate shot of the fly inserting its proboscis into a new flower of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2004)

File.Name: 4904d_4904-018.jpg
Notes:
8340770 Tiny parasitic wasp - maybe an Ichneumon Wasp (Ichneumonidae), though there are many other kinds - resting on a leaf of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden. You can see its wicked ovipositor sticking out the rear ready to lay eggs IN its prey that will consume and destroy them from the inside out. These are the wasps that did the deed in the [Previous] two photos. These tiny insects keep the balance of nature working as long as we don't destroy them with poisons intended for their prey. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2004)

The hairs on the leaf show what depth of field (DOF) is like with close focus. It's a bitch. You have to think about the plane of focus as well as the point. I take lots of photos handheld and hope to get a keeper like this.

File.Name: 4904e_4904-023.jpg
Notes:
8340710 Julie is raising native shrub seedlings at home for local restoration projects. This morning she said her Purple Sage (Salvia leucophylla) seedlings were looking sad and showed me some bumps under a leaf that I couldn't quite see. I set up my camera and macro lens on a tripod and got as close as I can for this shot. It took me a moment to understand it on the computer screen. Look for legs! Here are the husks of three aphids, but they have all been hollowed out by parasitic wasps or flies that blasted big holes out the rear when they exited. It looks like nature is taking care of the aphid problem on the sage plants! (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2004)

See my [Previous] photo for another close shot of natural pest-control at work:
  http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8340646

File.Name: 4904f_4904-031.jpg
Notes:
8340646 I believe this is an Oak Ribbed Casemaker (Bucculatrix albertiella or kin) pupa/cocoon - the adult is a little moth - on the upper side of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. The tiny moth larvae "skeletonize" oak leaves by eating the soft tissue between the top and bottom surfaces of the leaves. That gives you a sense of scale! This photo shows something really tiny, taken (with a tripod) at 1:1 magnification and then cropped even further. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2004)

Look closer. You can see the characteristic starfish-like "stellate hairs" on the Live Oak leaf. The cocoon is surrounded by a circular fence of bristly hairs pointing inwards. This cocoon has a tiny hole on the upper end, no doubt the exit hole of the even-tinier parasitic wasp or fly that killed and devoured the pupa. Nature is grand!

NEVER use broad-spectrum insecticide poisons that might destroy the insects that will control the insects that bother you! (Not to mention your fish and frogs and lots more.) There's a huge chemical industry devoted to this with lots of advertising and political influence, but you can do better with simple non-toxic remedies. Our home garden is full of life. I show the proof every day here in my f'log. It's spooky to go to a big city (like Santa Barbara just down the hill; or Portland, Oregon this summer) and find so few insects among so many flowers.
Locality: like Santa Barbara just down the hill; or Portland
CommonNames: Oak Ribbed Casemaker; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 4905a_4904-057.jpg
Notes:
8346039 Ripe Tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa) fruit bursting from its papery cover. Tomatillos are the essential ingredient in chile verde and much of my favorite food. We have a good garden crop this summer. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2004)

I did a good morning hike, and came back with few photos. I`m too jittery and pre-occupied with school starting next week. My photos today are all green. No disturbing insects! Green is a soothing color. It`s hard to capture without ending up too light or too dark.

File.Name: 4905b_4828-021.jpg
Notes: 8345979 Ripening Tomatoes (Solanum lycopersicum) in the garden. Right now it's a race between us and the jays and wood rats and the low sun and shorter days that come with autumn. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2004)

File.Name: 4905c_4830-141.jpg
Notes: 8345942 Ripening fruit on a native California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2004)

File.Name: 4905d_4904-040.jpg
Notes: 8345914 Developing seed pod of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the canyon. May the seeds live long and prosper! (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2004)

File.Name: 4905e_4830-149.jpg
Notes: 8345902 Green acorn on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. Acorns are scarce in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2004)

File.Name: 4905f_4904-015.jpg
Notes: 8345878 Horsetail aka Smooth Scouring Rush (Equisetum hyemale ssp. affine) stems in the little wetland (actually a well-disguised 4'x4' fiberglass tub) that catches the overflow from our hot/cold tub. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2004)

Locality: as teacher) after a grand summer.  We have classes and a potluck dinner tomorrow

File.Name: 4906b_4905-072.jpg
Notes: 8352074 There are small colonies of these tiny ants hiding inside the Tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa) flowers in the garden. They sure look like the horrible Argentine Ants (Iridomyrmex humilis) that are invading our mountains and state. But they don't act right. There are a dozen or two ants per flower, and I don't see any moving up and down the stems. They do have a bump (more like a ring) on the "petiole" that connects abdomen to humped thorax. I remember splitting open an Elderberry stem once to show kids they are hollow, and there were golden ants inside I've never seen anywhere else. Some ants specialize and find secret places, others take over. Hmm, this is a garden plant, not a native... (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2004)

File.Name: 4906c_4905-061.jpg
Notes: 8352002 Shed bark of Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) on the ground, rolled up like a stick of cinnamon. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2004)

File.Name: 4906d_4905-077.jpg
Notes: 8351985 Little Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae) dancing over the florets of a composite Chrysanthemum flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2004)

Notes: 8351953 Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) inside a Dahlia flower in our garden. It's a beautiful world! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2004)
