File.Name: 4911a_4909-190.jpg
Notes: 8383598 Sunset over the fog, from the top of Fremont Peak in the Gabilan Mountains behind Salinas. We went on the road for the first week of school to Steinbeck country. We camped on top of Fremont Peak in the Gabilan Range overlooking Salinas and Monterey and Hollister. The fog is a blessing. It's been very hot this week. We hit 108 degrees near King City! It was cooler on the mountain. We had a wonderful trip to new country. (Fremont Peak, 9 September 2004)

File.Name: 4911b_4909-197.jpg
Notes: 8383468 A sea of fog s_u_r_g_i_n_g in slow motion through the pass between the Salinas Valley (left) and the distant Santa Cruz Mountains (center) into the Santa Clara Valley (right). The fog is making waves and canyons as it flows inland. (Fremont Peak, 9 September 2004)

File.Name: 4911c_4909-082.jpg
Notes:
8383418 Sittin' on top of the world! This is the summit of Fremont Peak in the Gabilan Mountains east of Salinas, California. I reckon that's even further east of Eden. We camped nearby for our first week of school, and we all climbed to the top. American author John Steinbeck might have sat on this same perch with his dog Charley to reflect after his "you can't go home again" moment in Monterey. It's the crux of his book "Travels With Charley". It's a grand view and a chance to reflect. (Fremont Peak, 9 September 2004)

"I drove up to Fremont's Peak, the highest point for many miles around. I climbed the last spiky rocks to the top. Here among these blackened granite outcrops, General Fremont made his stand against the Mexican army, and defeated it. When I was a boy, we occasionally found cannon balls and rusted bayonets in the area. This solitary stone peak overlooks the whole of my childhood and youth, the great Salinas Valley stretching south for nearly a hundred miles, the town of Salinas where I was born now spreading like crab grass towards the mountain... I felt and smelled and heard the wind blow up from the long valley... Here on these high rocks my memory myth repaired itself." (John Steinbeck, Travels With Charley, 1962)

File.Name: 4911d_4909-093.jpg
Notes: 8383215 Hiking across the grassy south-facing slopes of Fremont Peak in the Gabilan Mountains. The city of Salinas and Monterey Bay are visible in the distance. The gray rock outcrops are sharp but slippery marble. We camped on the north-facing slope in a beautiful forest of Black Oak (Quercus kelloggii), Valley Oak (Quercus lobata), Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri), and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii). (Fremont Peak, 9 September 2004)
CommonNames: Black Oak; Valley Oak; Coulter Pine; Madrone
ScientificName: Quercus kelloggii; Quercus lobata; Pinus coulteri; Arbutus menziesii
associatedTaxa: Quercus kelloggii; Quercus lobata; Pinus coulteri; Arbutus menziesii

File.Name: 4911e_4909-096.jpg
Notes: 8383142 This is an abandoned mine near the summit of Fremont Peak behind Salinas that dates back to Spanish colonial times. The exposed summit rock is mostly marble. This vein was excavated to extract the lead-carbonate mineral cerussite which could be burned with charcoal to extract lead for gun shot. Now the only evidence is these deep chasms in the rock. (Fremont Peak, 9 September 2004)

File.Name: 4911f_4910-062.jpg
Notes: 8383079 We took our school on the road for the first week. We camped on top of Fremont Peak in the Gabilan Range overlooking Salinas and Monterey and Hollister. This is John Steinbeck country, so of course we visited the National Steinbeck Center in Salinas. This is the REAL Rocinante, Steinbeck's camper in Travels With Charley, now parked forever in the museum. I just happened to read that book again last summer after 40 years. It choked me up to stand by the real thing. Julie and I both have that gleam in our eyes! (Salinas, California, 10 September 2004)

File.Name: 4912a_4909-054.jpg
Notes: 8389820 Maybe you have to study insects to appreciate this remarkable creature. This is a perfect yellowjacket-wasp-mimic Flower Fly (Syrphidae) working new flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). The eyes, proboscis, antennae, and wings all give it away as a non-stinging fly (rather than a wasp with a sting) if you know what to look for. Nature is grand! (Fremont Peak, 9 September 2004)

File.Name: 4912b_4909-056.jpg
Notes: 8389733 Face to face with a remarkable wasp-mimic fly working new flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). It's a fly, not a wasp. It doesn't sting. How does the world look through those speckled eyes? (Fremont Peak, 9 September 2004)

File.Name: 4912c_4909-138.jpg
Notes: 8389702 Huge nickel-sized Horse Fly (Tabanus punctifer or kin) working new flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). They have a painful "bite", but they never bothered us in camp. Like most insect pests, they need a protein "blood meal" to make eggs, but they rely on sugar from flower nectar for energy. (Fremont Peak, 9 September 2004)

File.Name: 4912d_4909-140.jpg
Notes: 8389645 I was confused by the native deciduous oaks on Fremont Peak because there are both Valley Oaks (Quercus lobata) and Black Oaks (Quercus kelloggii). Both have deeply lobed leaves, but only Black Oak has spines on the lobes like this. It's an uncommon tree back home in the Santa Barbara mountains that only grows on high peaks in the backcountry. (Fremont Peak, 9 September 2004)

File.Name: 4912e_4910-058.jpg
Notes:
8389609 We went to John Steinbeck country on our back-to-school camping trip to Fremont Peak in the Gabilan Range, so of course we visited the National Steinbeck Center in Salinas. It's worth a visit. This is peeking in the back of the REAL Rocinante, Steinbeck's camper in Travels With Charley, now parked forever in the museum. There's root beer, 7up, maybe some real beer, and a bottle of Courvoisier on the table by the typewriter. It looks like home. (Salinas, California, 10 September 2004)

"I set my cabin in order, heated and ate a can of chili con carne, made sure the beer was cold, and even picked a bouquet of autumn leaves and put them in a milk bottle. The roll of paper cups laid in for just such an occasion had got squashed flat by a flying dictionary my first day out, but I made coasters from folded paper towels. It's amazing what trouble you will go to for a party. Then Charley barked them in and I was host in my own house. Six people can squeeze in behind my table, and they did. Two others beside me stood up, and the backdoor was wreathed with children's faces... I opened beer for the big ones and pop for the outsiders... In due course these people told me quite a bit about themselves..." (John Steinbeck, Travels With Charley, 1962)

I have another photo of Rocinante at
  http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8383079

File.Name: 4912f_4909-195.jpg
Notes: 8389442 We saw the sun setting into the deep fog from the top of Fremont Peak in the Gabilan Range behind Salinas. We were camped "east of Eden", looking west towards Monterey and the ocean. There are fog-waves slowly moving over the surface of the fog bank. That lone tree is Black Oak (Quercus kelloggii). (Fremont Peak, 9 September 2004)

File.Name: 4913a_4913-009.jpg
Notes: 8398315 Me and my shadow - this "me" is a not-quite-mature last-instar Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) on an iris leaf in the garden. It looks so delicate and embryonic. I could kill it with a gesture. You can see the upturned wing buds matching the curve of its scorpion-like abdomen and the serious sucking proboscis folded under its head. So many eggs, so few adults to make the next generation for next year's photos. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2004)

File.Name: 4913b_4913-011.jpg
Notes: 8398273 The first Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed seed pods popped open in the garden while I was at school today. They look electric! I got home just in time to get this photo. We're already losing our afternoon sun on our north-facing side of the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2004)

File.Name: 4918a_4918-043.jpg
Notes:
8431172 "Life is a bitch, and then you're dead." I was happy to catch this Flower Fly (Syrphidae) at rest on a cluster of male (staminate) Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis)flowers in our canyon. I didn't notice why it was at rest until I loaded the photos on my computer. Look close for extra legs under the front of the fly! It was just captured by a camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae). The rictus of its death is remarkable. Note how the fly's rear legs are extended in perfect form around the abdomen, and its compound eye is deflating like a punctured balloon. This drama happens all the time just out of sight, even on city streets. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2004)

I took a photo of what I'm pretty sure is the SAME fly a few minutes earlier happily working the flowers:
  http://www.fotolog.net/bugsncritters/?pid=8582520
CommonNames: Flower Fly; Coyote Bush; Crab Spider

File.Name: 4918b_4918-025.jpg
Notes: 8431025 This tiny spider has a lifeline across this composite flower of Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) along the road in our canyon. The spider has dark long front claws like a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), but the shape and colors don't seem right. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2004)

File.Name: 4918c_4918-016.jpg
Notes: 8430981 Tiny Flower Fly (Syrphidae) working the male (staminate) flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in our canyon. You can see its extended proboscis working the composite flowers. Is it after pollen or nectar? This shrub is either the last or the first flowering plant of the rain-year depending on the weather. After this extreme drought summer, it feels like the last to flower. Coyote Bush is a much-maligned weedy native shrub, but it feeds the insects during our hardest season. It's an important plant for the ecosystem. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2004)

File.Name: 4918d_4918-039.jpg
Notes: 8430910 Tiny native Ant exploring a female (pistillate) flower cluster of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in our canyon. I only saw a few ants in these shrubs. I wonder what they were looking for? (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2004)

File.Name: 4918e_4918-059.jpg
Notes:
8430880 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) squeezing into a flower of Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius) at home. We did some fire clearing last summer, and this native plan is flowering again on the ground we cleared. The bee's knees and toes look like totems! (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2004)

I have a close crop of The bee`s knees at:
