File.Name: 4918f_4918-049.jpg
Notes: 8430822 Tiny orange pumpkin-like insect eggs on a new stump-sprout of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. I guess these are Butterfly or Moth (lepidoptera) eggs. I'll keep an eye on them. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2004)

File.Name: 4919g_4919-070.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8591078 Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting on cluster of Spearmint (Menthe spicata?) flowers in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2004)

File.Name: 4919a_4919-070.jpg
Notes:
8438430 Well-camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting on cluster of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata?) flowers in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2004)

Funny how you get one crab spider photo, and then see crab spiders everywhere! A "dry storm front" passed over today, and it was windy all day. A tripod doesn't help when everything else is moving! When I looked for flowers and critters that weren't moving so much, I got this spider and the [Previous] photo. I hope the next storm brings some rain to relieve our drought.

File.Name: 4919b_4919-063.jpg
Notes: 8438394 This well-camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has captured a Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) on the composite Dusty Miller (Senecio cineraria) flowers in our garden. See [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2004)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Bee Fly; Dusty Miller

File.Name: 4919c_4918-060.jpg
Notes: 8438368 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) sticking its tongue out after visiting a flower of Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the garden. (See [Previous] photo.) Actually it's the bee's "proboscis". Note those hand/foot-like structures on either side of the proboscis - maybe "maxillary palps"? I don't understand insect mouth parts! (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2004)

File.Name: 4919d_4918-057.jpg
Notes: 8438283 Inviting new flower of Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius) at home. The pistil and single stamen look like bait - and they are. We did some fire clearing last summer. This native perennial usually flowers in spring, but it's flowering again on the ground we cleared. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2004)

File.Name: 4919e_4918-011.jpg
Notes: 8438248 Shy flowers of Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii). This is a native mint-relative that grows here and there in the woods according to its own rules that I can't quite figure out. It grows well from cuttings, like this plant on a rock wall in our garden. It has a wonderful minty aroma and spicy taste. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2004)

File.Name: 4919f_4919-068.jpg
Notes: 8438181 We have bigger tomato pests than insects in the garden. Look at the bottom fruit. I suspect a deer mouse or wood rat. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2004)

File.Name: 4925a_4925-008.jpg
Notes:
8477823 Male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly with wings wide open, working Fuschia flowers in our garden. This is the official State Butterfly of California, but I reckon only lepidopterists know why. It always folds its wings up tight when visiting a flower posed to photograph. Capturing it in-flight with wings open has been my summer-long challenge. This is still not right. Focus is off, and it only shows a hint of the iridescence. I'll keep trying. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2004)

I used to think these brilliant California Dogface butterfles were uncommon. Fotolog has me out looking for new photos every day, so I've noticed this is probably our most common butterfly over the summer! (Don't generalize - we live in a pristine canyon in the mountains miles away from town!)

File.Name: 4925b_4925-002.jpg
Notes: 8477729 This photo might look like a happy clown face in the thumbnail, but it's really a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) sucking the life from an unfortunate moth. I'm sure the cartoon face on the spider's abdomen is designed to startle its own preditors. Scarecrow? Freddy? Strange how this face of fear works across phyla into our own dark imagination. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2004)

File.Name: 4925c_4925-037.jpg
Notes: 8477667 This inch-long Spider Wasp (Pompilidae) is working the flowers of a male Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in our canyon. It is so blue/indigo that it's almost black. It twitches its wings constantly in a nervous way. It seems to be feeding from the flowers rather than looking for prey. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2004)

File.Name: 4925d_4925-021.jpg
Notes: 8477626 Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) hiding among the flowers of a male Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in our canyon. You can see its straw-like proboscis folded under the narrow head. I bet this critter doesn't follow the rule to "Never eat anything bigger than your head"! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2004)

File.Name: 4925e_4925-030.jpg
Notes: 8477611 Straw-like mouthpart of an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) hiding among the flowers of a male Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in our canyon. This veteran is missing segments and limbs on the camera side. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2004)

File.Name: 4925f_4925-032.jpg
Notes: 8477593 There's danger in the flowers! Looking up at an Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) hiding among the flowers of a male Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the canyon. This preditor keeps its needle-like proboscis folded under its head until it's time to grab and suck. It's now officially autumn after the equinox, the end of the season, and I seem to find more preditors than prey in the woods. This is nature, not politics. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2004)

File.Name: 4a01a_4930-133.jpg
Notes: 8518796 Sea Anemone exposed by the Harvest Moon low tide at Refugio Beach yesterday. We went around the point for my after-school DMS Hike Club. I'm still trying to figure out how to use my Nikon D70 digital SLR to get photos of kids and critters. Changing lenses in the field is risky because sand and dust can get onto the sensor. (Gaviota Coast, 30 September 2004)

File.Name: 4a01b_4930-152.jpg
Notes: 8518689 Live Fingernail Limpet (Collisella scabra or C. digitalis) giving a "hands up" (I think) around the point at Refugio Beach. My after-school DMS Hike Club kids had fun with these mollusks because they stick to fingers and face! I`m fussy with the names of flowers and mushrooms, but I just have fun at the beach! (Gaviota Coast, 30 September 2004)

File.Name: 4a01c_4930-174.jpg
Notes: 8518685 Surf Grass (Phyllospadix) washed up on the beach at Refugio. It`s a real "flowering plant" in the Eel-Grass family (Zosteraceae) that grows under water in the ocean. I wonder if that foam is a mass of swimming pollen/sperm? (Gaviota Coast, 30 September 2004)

File.Name: 4a01d_4930-137.jpg
Notes: 8518678 "Stand still, he`s pointing the camera, never mind that breaking wave, be cool and pose!" Gull on a rock at Refugio Beach. (Gaviota Coast, 30 September 2004)

File.Name: 4a01e_4930-159.jpg
Notes:
8518672 Concrete seawall around the point at Refugio Beach. A marker on top dates it to "1944". What are all those iron spikes bent back by wave action? A nearby surfing beach south of Refugio is called "Hazards" because of the iron weirs hidden in the water. The railroad runs on top of the bluff, so maybe this seawall was made to protect the tracks? But I suspect these "hazards" are the remains of a "homeland security" project dating back to the Japanese submarine attack on nearby Goleta on February 23, 1942? Old security can become modern danger! (Gaviota Coast, 30 September 2004)

See http://www.mcssb.org/goletahistory/japanesesubattack.htm

File.Name: 4a02a_4a02-088.jpg
Notes: 8524734 Looking straight down on a Mexican Hat (Ratibida columnaris) flower in the garden - an incoming bee's view - like a golden ring holding a deadly gem. Did you spot the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on top poised to kill? I suspect these spiders can change color to match a flower. But this one also has yellow tips on its claws that look just like the tiny florets! Did this spider collect pollen from the flowers to perfect the illusion, or is the lure part of its anatomy? (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a02b_4a02-064.jpg
Notes:
8524608 I dropped a pencil once when I was a kid, and it landed on the carpet standing straight up. I had the deep thought this will never happen again for me!

Here's another of those moments. I saw a green spot on a seed head of Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) and moved in close for a quick photo. I snapped and blinked and it was gone. I guess this is a Jumping Spider (Salticidae). It jumped away just after the photo. The photo is OK, but everything else is wrong.

The spider looks so strange with that monkey-mask face, and all those legs seem to come from top in an impossible way. This may be another one-time moment, but I captured it! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a02c_4a02-047.jpg
Notes: 8524453 What a hose! This Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) is sucking pollen from the stamens of a Dahlia flower in our garden, while a little beetle looks on. I wonder how these order of magnitude different-sized critters perceive each other. They sure seem to notice ME at yet another order of magnitude when I get close with my camera! (Hint: raise your camera BEFORE you approach!) (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a02d_4a02-042.jpg
Notes: 8524407 Pretty bee-like Flower Fly (Syrphidae) using its front legs to gather pollen to its proboscis on this Fuschia flower in the garden. Pollen grains are hard to digest, but this fly must know the secret. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a02e_4a02-009.jpg
Notes: 8524366 Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) munching a composite flower head of Dusty Miller (Senecio cineraria) flowers in the garden. So intimate! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a03h_4a03-119.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9218259 This is the first 'shroom I've seen for months in our summer-dry mountains. It came up in the garden and I don't want to disturb it just to learn what it is. I hope this is a good omen for the coming winter season! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a03g_4a03-104.jpg
Notes:
[bugsncritters] 8698851 Little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) trapped by a milkweed flower of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa, family Asclepiadaceae) in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2004)

Check my flog for more info:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8531266

File.Name: 4a03a_4a03-109.jpg
Notes:
Milkweed seems so commonplace, but milkweed flowers like this yellow form of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa, family Asclepiadaceae) in our garden are second only to orchids for complexity. This little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) learned the hard way that the flowers are a trap. It is caught between the HORNS and the STIGMATIC SLIT of the flower. I managed to free it unharmed by pulling the "petals" back. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2004)

I have more milkweed photos and info I have more milkweed photos and info on how this works at
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8108299
and [Previous] to that.

File.Name: 4a03b_4a02-051.jpg
Notes: 8531142 Little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) dancing over a meadow of stamens in this Dahlia flower in the garden. It's a beautiful world! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a03c_4a02-026.jpg
Notes: 8531125 Wild Bee (Apoidea) working a Fuschia flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a03d_4a02-092.jpg
Notes: 8531105 Immature Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) on a Sage (Salvia) leaf in the garden. Its body is soft like a marshmallow, but that folded proboscis is hard as steel. I think I understand. The seasons are changing. We still have some flowers, but the summer insect buzz is getting quieter. I have to look for insects to photograph rather than letting them come to me. Reproduction is past, and now it's time for the preditors to mature and take out the old. That's why I'm now finding so many spiders and insect preditors like this. These are the "final days" for these critters. It's an interesting thought that every *other* critter in the world ends up being ripped apart or sucked dry like this skipper. We don't expect that. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a03e_4a03-130.jpg
Notes: 8530993 Crab Spider (Thomisidae) grasping an unfortunate Skipper (Hesperiidae) butterfly that it captured on this flower head of Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) along our road. Look close - the moth seems to have a leg coming out of its screen-left eye, evidence of a great struggle. I often find these spiders with missing legs, now I have an idea where they go. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a03f_4a02-054.jpg
Notes: 8530929 And now for something completely different... we've been doing some work at home. This spray can of insulation foam leaked overnight. Demons and clouds! (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a09a_4a09-006.jpg
Notes: 8569608 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) deep inside a flower of this Crookneck Squash (Cucurbitaceae) in the garden with the sun behind. This is natural studio lighting! The bee isn`t really interested in the fat stamens, but it has to crawl over them to get to the nectar down below, and maybe it will carry some pollen. What a beautiful place to work! (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a09b_4a09-009.jpg
Notes: 8569575 Male Anna's Hummingbird working new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in our garden. This native shrub should not be flowering now, but here it is. It's an unusually large 10+ foot tall specimen growing where it gets some summer water in the garden. I hope this isn't a sign of trouble ahead. Several other natives have unseasonably late flowers on the edge of our garden. The native plants are confused in this drought year, finishing or starting early. I'm confused too. It's still hot, but it feels like fall. I'm ready for rain. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a09c_4a09-031.jpg
Notes: 8569507 It looks green here, but I pretty sure this is a Blue Bottle Fly aka Blow Fly (Calliphora vicina or kin, family Calliphoridae). These flies are handsome but nasty. They breed in garbage and carrion and dung. (Our neighbors have horses.) Adults feed on flowers like this native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2004)
CommonNames: Blue Bottle Fly; Blow Fly; Coyote Bush

File.Name: 4a09d_4a02-072.jpg
Notes: 8569453 Spiny Tachina Fly (Paradejeania rutiloides) working female ("pistillate") flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in our canyon. These large bristly flies are at least nickle-size. The larvae are said to be internal parasites of the "woollybear" caterpillars that become tiger moths. The preditor and prey share a family resemblance! Maybe it's not a coincidence that both flies and moths are now appearing. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a09e_4a02-065.jpg
Notes: 8569371 Mature seed pod of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the canyon. You can see the flat seeds stacked up like poker chips inside, but they are held back by that fibrous net between the split sides of the pod. It's an adaptation. The pods are at the tip of 6-10 foot stalks. It will take a good wind or bump to knock the seeds loose, so they will fall away from the parent plant. With drought and preditors, only a few of our lilies have made seeds this year. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a09f_4a09-018.jpg
Notes: 8569333 This California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) is a native late-season Hummingbird flower that is still flowering in the canyon despite the drought. It has sticky pollen like many plants in the same Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. I reckon these flowers can self-pollinate. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a10a_4a02-099.jpg
Notes:
8574983 Beautiful waxy flowers of an unknown Stonecrop (Crassulaceae) succulent in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2004)

Blah, I'm catching a cold. I didn't get far today, but there's always something new to see.

File.Name: 4a10b_4a09-026.jpg
Notes: 8574945 Spines of San Pedro Cactus (Trichocereus Pachanoi) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a10c_4a09-055.jpg
Notes: 8574916 This is what "glandular hair" means! These pin-shaped hairs on the flower bracts of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) each hold a drop of fragrant sticky resin. This is a spring wildflower that should not be flowering now, but some natives get confused when they get summer water on the edge of our garden. I hope it's not an ill omen. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a10d_4a10-080.jpg
Notes: 8574864 Ripe spore-bearing sporangia on a frond of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta). (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a10e_4a10-082.jpg
Notes: 8574826 A leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree, with wounds. It looks like a map. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a10f_4a07-269.jpg
Notes: 8574784 My view of the backcountry driving home from school last week. These mountains come in layers. That`s the bottom of Fremont ridge in the shadows up front; then the oak savanna across the river; then Loma Alta catching the sun - a "loma" is a grassy peak, and this is a high one; and then Santa Cruz Peak (right) and 6,593 ft. high Mission Pine Mountain (aka San Rafael Mtn., left) marking the skyline. There`s still lots of wild country to explore! The path of the sun changes more near the equinoxes than any other time of year. The shadows are different every day, revealing new detail. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a11a_4a11-116.jpg
Notes: 8580831 Long-horned Katydid (Tettigoniidae, maybe the Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata)?) on a Dahlia flower in the garden. I love those two-toned eyes! I believe that oval depression on the front leg just below the knee is its tympanum or "ear". Not so strange - I've been to a few rock shows where I heard the music through my feet! (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a11b_4a11-113.jpg
Notes: 8580743 Long-horned Katydid (Tettigoniidae, maybe the Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata)?) drooling pollen on a Dahlia flower in the garden. I don't understand their mouth parts. They have lots of little "hands" (aka palps) on their mouth in addition to the six legs that all insects have. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a11c_4a11-100.jpg
Notes: 8580671 Strange three-petal flowers of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron villosum) growing as a parasite on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a11d_4a11-106.jpg
Notes: 8580649 There are not many acorns in this drought year, but this Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) near Lake Cachuma is doing fine. Acorns are the staple food for much wildlife at this time of year, so it's a big impact when the oaks fail. A mature California Black Bear wandered onto my school campus a week ago looking for food or water or fun. It was treed and killed. D'oh, I wasn't there to get the photo. Anyway, it's good to find some oaks with acorns for the critters. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a11e_4a10-095.jpg
Notes: 8580569 Some pollinator is about to make a fatal mistake! This is a nasty-looking Crab Spider (Thomisidae) poised to attack on a Composite (Asteracea) flower in the garden. Insects are getting scarce in the woods and garden as we move into fall. Reproduction time is over, and the preditors are picking them off. I almost see more preditors than prey, but that won't last. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a11f_4a10-085.jpg
Notes: 8580496 We have young Blue Belly Lizards aka Western Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis) everywhere right now. Our cat got this one. I found it dead as an offering in the doorway. I'm holding it in my hand touching my gold wedding ring that also has an engraved lizard/salamander. Cats chase deer mice and wood rats, but there is a penalty. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2004)
CommonNames: Blue Belly Lizards; Western Fence Lizards

File.Name: 4a16a_4a16-044.jpg
Notes: 8614236 Oh boy, the weather is turning, there's a chance of a real drought-busting rain tonight! We cut firewood, covered piles, and closed windows that have been open since April. I'm wearing long pants for the first time in months! We also harvested these beautiful ripe "Heritage Tomatoes" from the garden. They come in colors. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a16b_4a16-007.jpg
Notes: 8614176 Morning drizzle brings out the delicate colors of these new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum). This particular shrub is on the edge of our garden, but native currants in the woods are also starting to flower. This seems too early, but here they are. There's a chance of real rain tonight. Maybe these shrubs had a premonition! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a16c_4a16-041.jpg
Notes: 8614136 Pretty bee-colored Flower Fly (Syrphidae) depositing eggs on flowers of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. The eggs will hatch and the larvae will feed on the yellow Milkweed Aphids (maybe Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?). Parasitic wasps inject their eggs into the aphids, but these flies just seem to leave them on the plant. What happens next? (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2004)
CommonNames: Flower Fly; Butterfly Weed; Milkweed Aphids
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Asclepias tuberosa; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Asclepias tuberosa; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens

File.Name: 4a16d_4a16-013.jpg
Notes: 8614086 Seeds escaping from Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a16e_4a16-009.jpg
Notes: 8614066 It was drizzly this morning for the first time in months. This Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius) leaflet is a good umbrella for these tiny Aphids (family Aphididae). It's not raining yet, but clouds are settling in, and there's a good chance for a drought-buster tonight. I'm so ready! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a16f_4a16-030.jpg
Notes: 8613994 Peeling bark of native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii). We had a drizzly morning to bring out the fall colors, and there's a chance of rain tonight - I'm so happy! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a17a_4a17-070.jpg
Notes: 8621229 Somehow the mushrooms know when rain is coming! We had our first rain since March last night, and this fresh Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) fungus growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) is already finished. It looks like something from the beach. It's an odd puffball with a tough outer rind that splits into the star and a thin inner sack that holds the spore mass and soon breaks apart and disappears. Oh boy, it's the end of summer drought and the start of mushroom season! (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a17b_4a17-059.jpg
Notes: 8621112 Native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) flower with raindrops from our first rain since March. This native should NOT be flowering yet. I've noticed some other "early bloomers" including Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum), Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius), and California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus). I reckon summer drought and occasional sprinkling on the edge of the garden is confusing them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2004)
CommonNames: California Honeysuckle; Chaparral Currant; Wild SweetPea; California Blackberry
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; vacillans; Ribes malvaceum; Lathyrus latifolius; Rubus ursinus
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; vacillans; Ribes malvaceum; Lathyrus latifolius; Rubus ursinus

File.Name: 4a17c_4a17-054.jpg
Notes: 8621037 Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius) flowers, with raindrops from our first rain since March. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a17d_4a17-064.jpg
Notes: 8621012 Soggy female "pistillate" flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in our canyon, drooping after the first rain since March. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a17e_4a17-052.jpg
Notes: 8620934 The world reflected in a raindrop - actually many raindrops - on a leaflet of native Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius) on the edge of our garden. It rained today for the first time since March! That's a blessing and a stumper too. The big drop has a right-side-up "fisheye" reflection of the forest and sky, but the small drops all show it upside-down. What gives? (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a17f_4a17-051.jpg
Notes: 8620863 The view from the porch this morning - we had our first rain since March last night, over an inch, and more is on the way. This shows Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) with ripening orange berries and Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) on the ridge. What a relief after the most wildfire-ready summer I can remember. (*Knock*) (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2004)
CommonNames: Madrone; Coast Live Oak; Coulter Pines
ScientificName: Arbutus menziesii; Quercus agrifolia; Pinus coulteri
associatedTaxa: Arbutus menziesii; Quercus agrifolia; Pinus coulteri

File.Name: 4a23a_4a23-037.jpg
Notes: 8660358 I hike the same trails in our wild canyon week after week - and I still manage to find something strange and wonderful like this! It's not in the ocean. This is the streaming plasmodium of a Slime Mold (Myxomycophyta) on a decaying log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). See my [Previous] photo for more info on Myxomcetes. This critter is smaller than a pencil erasor. I have no idea what those bubbles are. I used flash, so they all become eyes looking back. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a23b_4a23-032.jpg
Notes: 8660281 I was surprised to find this plasmodium of a Slime Mold (Myxomycophyta - maybe Lycogala epidendrum?) so soon after the rain last week - our first rain since March. It's a remarkable critter that's part fungus and part animal. These Myxomcetes live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in a substrate like this decaying oak log. But sometimes they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" that can crawl along like a slug. It finally dries and forms a mushroom-like structure to release tiny spores that will disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying log to another. This slime mold plasmodium is soft and gooey, like touching a perfectly roasted marshmallow. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a23c_4a23-020.jpg
Notes: 8660073 Butter Bolete (Boletus appendiculatus?) mushroom coming up under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. It has a tan cap and yellow pores (not gills) that instantly stain blue when you scratch them. It's fun to leave messages like this in the woods! This Bolete is firm and delicious, but some others that stain blue are dangerous. Know what you eat! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a23d_4a23-012.jpg
Notes: 8660002 Mushrooms! Probably some species of Mycena. We had our first rain since March last week - over 8 inches up here in the mountains, about half of our total rainfall last year. The woods are coming alive! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a23e_4a23-017.jpg
Notes: 8659751 Fungus growth on a single leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. It`s a tiny fairy ring - the decomposer fungus started in the center and is working outwards, leaving concentric rings to mark its progress digesting the leaf. Trees develop growth rings in their wood that separates the seasons. I wonder what environmental conditions these tiny rings demarcate? (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a23f_4a23-060.jpg
Notes: 8659690 Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) waiting on a leaf in the garden. You can see its deadly sucking mouthpart folded under its head ready to strike. The woods are changing after our first rain since March last week, and the insects are changing too. I've been seeing immature forms of this assassin for weeks, but this is the first adult I've seen. I reckon these late-season preditors are one reason why the pollinators are getting scarce. Nothing is wasted. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a24g_4a23-020.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9485325 Butter Bolete (Boletus appendiculatus?) mushroom coming up under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. It has yellow pores (not gills) that instantly stain blue when you scratch them. It's fun to leave messages like this in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a24h_4a23-023.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9485276 Butter Bolete (Boletus appendiculatus?) mushroom coming up under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. It's our first real mushroom after the rain last week! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a24i_4a23-033.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9485212 I was surprised to find these Slime Molds (Myxomycophyta - maybe Lycogala epidendrum?) so soon after our first rain since March last week. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a24a_4a24-077.jpg
Notes:
8666386 Insects have 6 legs, but this Katydid (Tettigoniidae, maybe the Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata)?) also has 4 helping hands (aka maxillary and labial palps) to get food to its mouth. The plant is a Pelargonium in the garden. I love those two-toned eyes and lobster-like antennae. It looks happy. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2004)
Heh, this reminds me of the Futurama episode where Bender plays with Beck!
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Scudderia furcata; maxillary and labial palps; Pelargonium
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Scudderia furcata; maxillary and labial palps; Pelargonium

File.Name: 4a24b_4a24-074.jpg
Notes: 8666305 Dandelion-like seeds of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) leaving home. For seeds and kids, it's a big step! (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a24c_4a24-064.jpg
Notes: 8666260 Crookneck summer squash hiding under spent leaves in the garden. The seasons are turning. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a24d_4a24-078.jpg
Notes: 8666235 I only noticed this tiny spider on a Pelargonium in the garden because I was stalking the Katydid a leaf or two away in today's top photo. Even looking close, I'm sure I miss almost everything in nature that's right in front of me. This spider has the long front legs of a Crab Spider (Thomisidae), but its haunt and color make me unsure. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a24e_4a23-048.jpg
Notes: 8666109 Immature last-instar Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) on a composite flower in our garden. How can something so small and alien look *so pissed off*?! Insects are getting scarce in the garden since the weather changed last week. I reckon these late-season preditors are one reason why the pollinators are getting scarce. Nothing is wasted. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a24f_4a23-029.jpg
Notes: 8666078 Feather from a Red-Shafted Flicker woodpecker in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a30a_4a30-024.jpg
Notes: 8709306 Tiny LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) with the sun behind. That's an oak leaf in the foreground for scale. We've had 11+ inches of rain in the last two weeks here in the mountains, almost as much as Santa Barbara had ALL last year! Today's photos are all mushrooms - mostly tiny decomposers that are starting to appear after the long summer drought and heavy October rains. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a30b_4a30-006.jpg
Notes: 8709242 Tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - coming up in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). There is a single spine of an oak leaf in the foreground for scale. I think this is the same 'shroom as the [Next] photo. I look for these tiny critters, but I reckon it's good I don't see them all or I couldn't walk anywhere in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a30c_4a30-004.jpg
Notes: 8709203 Tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). The oak leaves give a sense of scale. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a30d_4a30-001.jpg
Notes: 8709168 Tiny Inky Cap (Coprinus) mushroom in the woods. The cap of these mushrooms is paper-thin, and the gills "deliquesce" or liquefy into black ink leaving these pretty dark tracks accented with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a30e_4a30-034.jpg
Notes: 8709115 Some kind of Agaricus mushroom on the dry native side of our garden. It doesn't stain yellow, the stipe is not bulbous, and it tastes good (with a nibble). You can see the "partial veil" that used to cover the gills but tore free to become a ring on the stem. I think I see the fragments of a second veil on the inner edge of the ring. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a30f_4a30-011.jpg
Notes: 8709009 Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica or kin) mushroom and some brackets of Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor aka Coriolus versicolor) on a decaying oak log. I took several photos, and I can see those dark specks hop from photo to photo. They are tiny Springtail (Collembia) insects. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2004)
ScientificName: Tremella mesenterica; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor; Collembia
associatedTaxa: Tremella mesenterica; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor; Collembia

File.Name: 4a31g_4a31-079.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9580415 Tiny LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a31a_4a30-049.jpg
Notes: 8716275 Here's a scary photo for Halloween! It's a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) wrapping its still-living prey captured on a Composite (Asteracea) flower in the garden to sustain its young. You know, like the Alien movies. Judging from the eyes and antennae (under wraps), I reckon the unfortunate victim is a Flower Fly (Syrphidae). Unfortunate fly, fortunate spider. At this time of year, it's endgame. All these critters will soon die and maybe spawn a new generation for next year. It's a harsh reality being played out in the garden today that puts our Halloween stunts in perpective. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a31b_4a31-072.jpg
Notes: 8716132 This pretty Wild Bee (Apoidea) is dusted with pollen from working these Composite (Asteracea) flowers in the garden. They work like coal miners, but they work a more beautiful mine going from flower to flower. It's hard to tell one bee species from another, but this one seems new to me. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a31c_4a31-086.jpg
Notes: 8716079 Pretty bee-like Flower Fly (Syrphidae) working Composite (Asteracea) flowers in the garden with its stout proboscis. Pollinators are getting scarce after recent rains and cooler weather, but there are still aphids to be had. These flies feed on flowers, but they lay eggs near aphids so the larvae will hatch and consume them. I'm finding fewer pollinators but more preditors as the seasons change. I guess this pretty fly counts as both. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a31d_4a30-059.jpg
Notes: 8716008 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with almost full pollen baskets on its hind legs, working these Composite (Asteracea) flowers in the garden. That pollen mass looks so sticky and solid! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a31e_4a30-061.jpg
Notes: 8715967 This Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus fultoni or kin) was laying flat all morning on this Composite (Asteracea) flower in the garden. It finally woke up when the sun got to it and I disturbed it with my camera. These are the crickets that chirp-chirp-chirp all night long. They drive me nuts when they get in the house because they are so loud but they always stop when I get close to finding them. They are sometimes called "Thermometer Crickets" because pitch and frequency of the chirps is a function of temperature. (Temp F = 40 + chirps per 15 seconds?) They're chirping slower now after recent rains and cooler weather. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2004)

File.Name: 4a31f_4a31-075.jpg
Notes: 8715806 Tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia), with the sun behind. The sun is getting so low in the sky that it's always perfect light. The bigger 'shrooms in the back look like doting parents! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2004)

File.Name: 4b06h_4b06-028.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9662480 Tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" (probably Mycena?) - growing among the fallen leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b06g_4b06-058.jpg
Notes:
[bugsncritters] 8960161 Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) - it's pissed because I picked it up from the driveway and moved it to the side. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

Notice that red spot on the screen-left side? Check my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8759121
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8759089

File.Name: 4b06a_4b06-033.jpg
Notes: 8759190 A seedling in the woods after our recent rains. It's a "dicot" with two cotyledons or seed leaves. I don't know what it will become. We're blessed with early rain after several drought years. This is the image I wanted after the election. As Robert Frost said about life, "It goes on." (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b06b_4b06-019.jpg
Notes: 8759138 Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila) mushrooms fruiting under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b06c_4b06-065.jpg
Notes: 8759121 Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) trying to get away from me. I don't know if these mollusks have muscles, but this is a beautiful move. Did you notice that red dot in the lower right? I didn't see it when I took the photo, but I had fun discovering what it is. See the [Previous] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b06d_4b06-059.jpg
Notes: 8759089 From macro to micro - look at the Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) in the [Next] photo, and notice the red spot on its body in the lower right. I took several photos of this native terrestrial mollusk and noticed that the red spot moved around! I cropped in to an 80x80 image from the orginal 3008x2000 photo, and then enlarged the crop to 500x500 and tried different filters and enhancements to try to bring out some detail - think of Gene Hackman in the classic movie "The Conversation" or Antonioni's "Blow Up". I discovered the red dot is a Mite (Acari) - a very tiny 8-legged spider relative. At first I wondered how a mite could stick to the slippery banana slug - then I wondered how he could lift his legs and move over that slimy critter! I suspect this kind of mite ONLY lives on Banana Slugs and has special adaptations that allow it to manoeuver. Nature is like that. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b06e_4b06-029.jpg
Notes: 8758952 Tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" (probably Mycena?) - growing among the fallen leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The spines on the oak leaves in the photo are a millimeter long or less. These tiny 'shrooms are way too small to eat (if edible?), and just crouching down to get this photo I probably crushed 50 more. But these tiny decomposers are elegant and beautiful, and a delight to discover. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b06f_4b06-075.jpg
Notes: 8758867 Another LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - maybe Common Psathyrella (Psathyrella condolleana or kin) growing from the fallen leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I think a new grass leaf got in the way of my lens to make the pretty green color. These 'shrooms are hygrophanous - they change color and texture as they dry out. You can see the color transformation from the wetter top to the drier margin. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b07h_4a31-069.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 8967413 Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a Composite (Asteracea) flower in the garden. Spiders have eight legs, but they also have those "palps" up front. (31 October 2004)
Locality: Asteracea) flower in the garden. Spiders have eight legs

File.Name: 4b07g_4b06-085.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9673893 New cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b07a_4b07-082.jpg
Notes: 8765177 Stately new cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) on the edge of our garden. The green leaves are native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata). The mushrooms are edible, but they are named for their color not their taste. I'd rather have them in the garden than the dinner table. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b07b_4b07-080.jpg
Notes: 8765127 Even Aphids (family Aphididae) have the good sense to get under cover on a drizzly day like today. Their umbrella is a native Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius) flower on the dry side of our garden. I guess this flower is more like a tent for these tiny critters! (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b07c_4b07-086.jpg
Notes: 8765074 Maybe this is true Mexican Sage (Salvia Mexicana) in the garden on a cloudy day. It just started flowering and will probably be cut down soon by early frost. The flowers are a deeper-than-blue midnight-purple. We've been collecting Salvia species - native and not - what amazing variety! Anyone have Salvia divinorum rootings to share? (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b07d_4a31-084.jpg
Notes: 8764927 Native Bedstraw (Galium sp.) with backlight and fall colors. We have many species and subspecies of this genus. I'm really confused by them all, so I won't even guess what species this is. That's evolution in action! Even without a proper latin name, it sure is pretty. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b07e_4b06-012.jpg
Notes: 8764873 Some kind of Agaricus mushroom on the dry native side of our garden. It doesn't stain yellow, the stipe is not bulbous, and it tastes good (with a nibble). This one looks like a perfectly toasted marshmellow! (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b07f_4b06-031.jpg
Notes:
8764834 Mature Slime Molds (Myxomycophyta - maybe Lycogala epidendrum?) spilling their seed. I'd think this is a puffball mushroom, but I caught it in a younger stage a couple weeks ago so I know better. See:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8660281

These Myxomcetes live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in a substrate like this decaying oak log. But sometimes they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" that can crawl along like a slug. It finally dries and forms a mushroom-like structure to release tiny spores like this that will disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying log to another. It's spooky to think how much genetic info is locked in those powdery spores. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b12a_4b11-156.jpg
Notes:
8799734 Looking at the arch of Cold Springs Bridge from underneath. People have climbed that arch that rises 600+ feet above the canyon! A few years ago there were names and dates written in pencil at the base of the east buttress, but they were painted over during earthquake-recontruction on the bridge. I've hiked the creek under the bridge. My favorite find was HALF of a bowling ball that bounced! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2004)

You've probably seen this beautiful bridge in commercials or the Disney movie "The Cat From Outer Space" (1978). I have a photo (and a stumper) at:
   http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/01feb02.html

File.Name: 4b12b_4b11-158.jpg
Notes: 8799661 Detail of Cold Springs Bridge in our mountains. It's a classic arch bridge over a 600+ foot canyon. This is a close shot of one of the vertical supports that holds up the roadway. They are not bolted together! They are only connected by a "ball joint" so that the bridge has some play to flex during winds or an earthquake. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b12c_4b11-153.jpg
Notes: 8799609 This photo is rich with memories for me. This is a huge girder under the east end of Cold Springs Bridge in our mountains. I like to bring my DMS Hike Club kids here. We sit under the roadway that rocks when cars and trucks pass over! We also "spit write" our names on the dusty girders. Spit on your finger and write in the dust, don't lick! This is "spit-writing" on the dusty girders from hike club kids back to 1992 that is still visible. Treebeard was here, and my sons Ewan and Ry! There was major earthquake-recontruction on the bridge a few years ago, but our spit-names survived. Wow, that's history! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b12d_4b11-173.jpg
Notes: 8799569 Cold Springs Tavern catching autumn leaves from Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum). It's a 100+ year old stagecoach stop that still offers good food and music up here in the mountains. It has special meaning for me. Back in the 1970s, I used to live a mile or two up the road when I was working on my doctoral thesis (in philosophy) at UCSB. One night at the tavern, I got to talking with some folks who worked at the Santa Barbara County "Outdoor School" outdoor education program for local 6th grade kids. They needed a substitute naturalist/teacher the next day, and I agreed. I've been teaching middle school kids ever since. I still have a milk-crate full of unfinished thesis notes! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b12e_4b11-174.jpg
Notes: 8799478 Deciduous fallen leaves of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) and White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia) in the water of Cold Springs Creek. The leaves are so think on the ground and creek that I stepped in by mistake more than once. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b12f_4b11-175.jpg
Notes: 8799459 Fallen leaves of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) and California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) along Cold Springs Creek. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2004)
CommonNames: Bigleaf Maple; California Bay; Oregon Myrtle

File.Name: 4b13h_4b13-022.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9752377 Tiny LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" (probably Mycena?) - growing from the fallen leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b13g_4b13-017.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 9016080 I don't know what this critter is. It's posed on a leaf of Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in the garden. At first I thought it was just the hind-part husk of some insect, but then it hopped away. Maybe a Leafhopper (Fulgoroides)? (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b13a_4b13-043.jpg
Notes: 8806339 Tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" (probably Mycena?) - growing from the fallen leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. Look close at the base of the stalk of the mushroom on the right - see that tiny insect egg on a stalk? I look close for mushrooms - and discover much more! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b13b_4b13-045.jpg
Notes: 8806311 I guess this is a Ringed Panaeolus (Panaeolus semiovatus aka P. separatus) mushroom growing on old horse apples in our canyon. It has a viscid cap, black spores, and mottled gills with whitish margins. We had an issue with our neighbors across the canyon last summer when they let their horse wander "free-range" through the woodland. No way! The mushrooms may be edible, if you really want to eat something that grows from horse dung. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b13c_4b13-031.jpg
Notes: 8806228 Tiny seedlings raising their cotyledon seed leaves in hope - they will become next year's wildflowers if I don't crush them trying to get my next mushroom photo. Our woods are coming alive after early rains this year. When I walk through the woods, it feels like I take dozens of lives with every step. But nature is profligate, and there will be more. Nature is a tough mother - at best only one of this cluster of seedlings will survive to flower and bear fruit for next year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b13d_4b13-067.jpg
Notes: 8806146 California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) with the fertile male stamens releasing sticky pollen-balls and the fertilized female stigma on the right receiving them. This is a beautiful native late-season Hummingbird flower that is still flowering in our canyon and providing nectar for the hummers. It looks like it's OK with self-pollination. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b13e_4b13-013.jpg
Notes: 8806104 Looking close at a single flower of early-flowering Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum). The stamens looks like a circle of angels guarding something precious in the center. The fertile female pistil really is what matters. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b14h_4b14-068.jpg
Notes:
[bugsncritters] 9023802 Since our first rain of the season a couple of weeks ago and cooler weather, pollinator insects have gotten scarce, but there are lots of tiny new flies that dance in the sunbeams and never land. Here are the trails of some of those Dance Flies and Fungus Gnats during a 1 second @ f/20 exposure, handheld with manual focus and no flash. Intricate trails, but not bright enough. I'll keep experimenting with this. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

I have another shot at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8813003

File.Name: 4b14g_4b14-085.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9765201 Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila) mushroom under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees catching some late-afternoon light. Notice the wrinkled cap. The ground is still damp after recent rains, but the humidity is down and the leaves are getting crunchy again. I hope the rains keep coming so the 'shrooms follow! (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b14a_4b14-081.jpg
Notes: 8813054 An elegant cluster of Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila) mushrooms fruiting under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. We've had some good rain after our unusually harsh summer drought. Now it feels like all seasons are happening at once in central California, with winter mushrooms, new seedlings and buds, and fall foliage all at the same time. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b14b_4b13-069.jpg
Notes:
8813003 This is a photo experiment that worked. Since our first rain of the season a couple of weeks ago and cooler weather, pollinator insects have gotten scarce, but there are lots of tiny new flies that dance in the sunbeams and never land. Here are the bright trails of some of those Dance Flies and Fungus Gnats during 1/6 second @ f/7 with manual focus and no flash, with an oak tree in the back. Bright, but the trails are too short. Reminds me of a chromosome squash slide! I'll keep experimenting with this. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

I have another shot at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/bugsncritters/?pid=9023802

File.Name: 4b14c_4b14-092.jpg
Notes: 8812503 New seedlings of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the canyon. It's a harsh reality for these seedlings so full of hope for the new year. A shift in the weather or a wayward step will take them out. "Many are called, but few are chosen." (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b14d_4b14-078.jpg
Notes: 8812451 Elegant new leaves of Coffee Fern (Pellea andromedifolia) growing on a sandstone outcrop in our canyon. The leaflets are not "fern-like", but they have an embryonic quality that says they are something different. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b14e_4b13-039.jpg
Notes: 8812406 New buds on summer-deciduous Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii). It's autumn approaching winter by the calendar, but it's spring for this native shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b14f_4b14-089.jpg
Notes: 8812365 It's autumn in the canyon. Our Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) trees up here in the mountains don't get as huge as trees in the foothills, but the fall colors stand out. The mottled bark is wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b15a_4b13-025.jpg
Notes: 8819424 Native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) flowering on the dry edge of our garden. It is usually a spring wildflower, but the natives get confused with a bit of summer water (and early rain) on the edge between our garden and the woods. Right now we have an interesting overlap between late-season summer flowers, early winter-flowers, and what-am-I flowers like this wood mint. Every year is different! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b15b_4b13-055.jpg
Notes: 8819412 Individual florets of the native composite Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa). The days are getting short and winter is coming, but there are still a few late-season natives with flowers. Maybe the early rain this year helped keep the going after a drought summer. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b15c_4b06-049.jpg
Notes: 8819399 Late-season flower of the native composite Cudweed-Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia with a tiny spider hugging the ray flower in the lower-right. The whole flower-head (with rays) is about a half-inch across. What tiny prey is this little preditor stalking? (San Marcos Pass, 06 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b16a_4b06-061.jpg
Notes:
8826922 Ooh that tickles! This Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) is setting course to "hard a-starboard" after hitting an obstacle. These critters that walk on their bellies - also including snakes, sea lions, and lots more - must be both tough and sensative "down under". (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2004)

School conferences this week. Long days sitting indoors, but it's a chance at night to pull out some recent photos from the "good stuff" folder!

File.Name: 4b16b_4b14-087.jpg
Notes:
8826855 New seedling in the woods after recent rains. Most seedlings look the same with one or two similar-looking cotyledons or seed leaves. They get more character when the first "true leaves" appear. I recognize this is a seedling of Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum), but there are many risks between sprout and flower! (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2004)

I'm careful in the woods with my camera, but I wonder how many other tiny seedlings I crushed taking this photo. "Many are called, but few are chosen." Maybe this seedling will mature to make Fiesta Flowers like this:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=7621216

File.Name: 4b16c_4b13-064.jpg
Notes:
8826778 Opening seedpod of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. This is one of our late-season native plants that is still flowering to fuel the hummingbirds. Hmmm, most (all?) of our late-season wildflowers have dandelion-like seeds. Is that an adaptation to ride the storm winds as autumn turns to winter? (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

I have many photos of the flowers in the archives, starting at:
  http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8806146

File.Name: 4b16d_4b13-041.jpg
Notes: 8826746 Another LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - maybe Common Psathyrella (Psathyrella condolleana or kin) growing from the fallen leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) near home. These 'shrooms are hygrophanous - they change color and texture as they dry out. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b16e_4b13-028.jpg
Notes: 8826742 Another Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila) mushroom under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees catching the late-afternoon light just right. It almost glows. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b16f_4a30-056.jpg
Notes:
8826739 When Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica or kin) goes bad! Is it just a gooey mess or an active nebula of life-formation? (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2004)

Witch's Butter is a strange but pretty mushroom on decaying logs when fresh. Compare my earlier photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8709009

File.Name: 4b18a_4b18-135.jpg
Notes: 8841585 Late-season composite flower of native Cudweed-Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia) growing prostrate on a mowed athletic field at my school. This plant looks very different up in the mountains. We had an hour break from school conferences today (day 4 of 5), and I had the choice of taking a hike or a nap. *Sigh* Hiking the groomed fields at school is not like a walk in the woods, but there are surprises like this native aster trimmed to lawnmower height and still flowering. It's interesting how many natives - even uncommon native grasses - survive mixed in with the weeds. I have to look close to find them. This gives me some hope that our native floral biodiversity is not yet lost. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b18b_4b18-137.jpg
Notes: 8841548 Australian Saltbush (Atriplex semibaccata) is a weedy perennial introduced from Australia. It is so different from our many native Atriplex species (mostly shrubs) that it's hard to recognize any kinship. It has red fruit and can grow flat to survive mowing on this field at school. It's a serious invasive weed at the beach. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b18c_4b16-109.jpg
Notes: 8841532 Looking under the skirts of an upturned "California Agaricus" (Agaricus californicus) mushroom on a field at school. That twisty little seed pod is from one of those weedy Cheeseweeds or "Scissor Plants" that adapts to being mowed, maybe Malva neglecta. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b18d_4b16-144.jpg
Notes: 8841528 Looking along the edge of a fairy ring of "California Agaricus" (Agaricus californicus) on a field at school. They look good to eat when fresh, but they have an unpleasant chemical "phenol-like" smell and taste. It's sometimes called the "Fool's Agaric" - but we're all fools one time or another! (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b18e_4b16-097.jpg
Notes: 8841502 Fresh "Paltry Puffball" (Bovistes plumbea or kin) on a mowed field at school. At first I thought the "water drops" were morning dew, but they persist all day so they must be some sort of exudate from the mushrooms. This is a frequently mowed field at school. The green plant is one of those weedy Cheeseweeds or "Scissor Plants" that adapts to being mowed, maybe Malva neglecta. These little puffballs are perfect golf ball size. Yes, it`s great fun and a fine way to aid in spore dispersal! (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b18f_4b18-134.jpg
Notes: 8841437 "Paltry Puffball" (Bovistes plumbea or kin) on a mowed field at school. The ground is still damp after early rains this year and the fields are green, but the air is dry so i'm not surprised the cap is splitting like this. Bare ground that was mud a week ago is also fissured. Interesting geometry! (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b20g_4b20-013.jpg
Notes:
[mykologos] 9838860 An Agaricus mushroom on the native side of our garden. Did you notice that tiny bright-red LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - in the lower left corner"? (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2004)

I have a closer shot at: http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8851973

File.Name: 4b20h_4b20-001.jpg
Notes:
[mykologos] 9838821 Clump of fist-sized mushrooms growing on an old California Bay (Umbellularia californica) stump. I don't know what this is. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2004)

I have a photo of the gills at:
  http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8851854

File.Name: 4b20a_4b20-016.jpg
Notes:
8851973 I was trying to get a photo looking up "under the skirts" of this Agaricus mushroom on the dry native side of our garden. Then I noticed that tiny LBM - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - in the lower left corner. This LBM is bright-red. I usually guess Mycena for these tiny 'shrooms, I'm sure it's not Marasmius. It looks like a mouse under an elephant! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2004)

You can see the "partial veil" still tearing free from the gills of the agaricus to leave the ring behind on the stem. Makes be think of working out and tight abs. The little guy steals the show!

I have another photo of this pair at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/mykologos/?pid=9838860

File.Name: 4b20b_4b20-034.jpg
Notes: 8851892 Tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). We had our first good rain a couple of weeks ago. The ground is still damp, but the air is dry, and you can see that these tiny 'shrooms are feeling dry. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b20c_4b20-005.jpg
Notes:
8851854 We have a clump of these fist-sized mushrooms growing on an old California Bay (Umbellularia californica) stump on the edge of the garden. I first thought Pluteus cervinus because the slightly-notched gills look pinkish underneath, but the spores are definately white. I don't know what this is. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2004)

I have another photo of the mushrooms at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/mykologos/?pid=9838821

File.Name: 4b20d_4b20-051.jpg
Notes: 8851736 Unknown succulent (Stonecrop Crassulaceae family) growing in a pot on our deck at home in perfect afternoon light. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b20e_4b20-026.jpg
Notes: 8851698 Diaphanous sepals on the backside of a pretty Geranium (Geraniceae) that's been flowering all summer in the garden. Look close to see a couple of pioneering aphids. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b20f_4b20-027.jpg
Notes: 8851664 Yellow-orange Milkweed Aphids (maybe Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) grazing on a stem of non-native Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. I know aphids are a pest, but we only find these brilliantly colored aphids on this plant in the garden, and they are as interesting as the flowers! Now that I think of it, I hope we're not creating a source to propagate them into the woods. I haven't seen them on any native plant. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2004)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 4b21g_4b20-044.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] Looking under a Ringed Panaeolus (Panaeolus semiovatus aka P. separatus) mushroom growing on old horse apples in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b21a_4b21-106.jpg
Notes: 8858709 Ripe berries of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron villosum) growing as a parasite on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). You can see some of the strange three-petal flowers. The berries look like little balloons - or is it bazooms!? (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b21b_4b21-098.jpg
Notes: 8858658 New leaflets of California Maiden-Hair Fern (Adiantum jordanii) growing in the woodland near Lake Cachuma. This woodland species is different from the ferns that grow along creeks and waterfalls. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b21c_4b21-093.jpg
Notes: 8858617 Deer bones and seedlings - life and death in the woods. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b21d_4b21-120.jpg
Notes: 8858591 Native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) flower. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b21e_4b21-118.jpg
Notes: 8858571 Yellow-orange Milkweed Aphids (maybe Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) grazing on Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. The aphids shed their skins as they mature, so there's a pile of the husks on the leaf below. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2004)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 4b21f_4b21-123.jpg
Notes:
8858501 Marshmallow-sized Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) growing from the cut end of an oak log in our woodpile. It feels like a marshmallow too, mushy with a skin on the outside. It's more of a "them" than an "it". (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2004)

For more info, see
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8764834

File.Name: 4b22g_4b22-140.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9865249 Close look at the gills of Entoloma rhodopolium or kin in the canyon. I have at photo of the cap at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8865895. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b22h_4b22-143.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9865158 Agaricus mushroom coming up on the native side of our garden. That's soil on the cap, not scales. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b22a_4b22-127.jpg
Notes: 8866073 Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) going "under the skirts" of this Greek Sage (Salvia fructicosa?) flower in the garden. Many insects know this trick to get to the nectar without going the long way through the throat of the flower. You can see the shadow of the bee's proboscis under the calyx, but it doesn't look nearly long enough to get to the sweet spot. Look at that claw on the right leg - like a totemic figurine! (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b22b_4b22-129.jpg
Notes: 8865998 Dandelion-like seeds of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) caught in a spider web. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b22c_4b22-137.jpg
Notes: 8865895 This early mushroom is Entoloma rhodopolium or kin. It has a dark cap and pink spores. It smells "farinaceous" like linseed oil but not like bleach. Mushrooms *want* to come up, but the woods are getting crunchy and it feels dry even though the ground is still damp from early rain. I also found the first Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius) today when I dug into a "mushrump" mound in the woods. I left it in place to save for Thanksgiving dinner. We're ready for more rain. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b22d_4b22-132.jpg
Notes: 8865698 Stem gall on Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea). It's caused by the Coyote Bush Rust Gall Fungus (Puccinia evadens) rather than an insect. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2004)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Coyote Bush Rust Gall Fungus

File.Name: 4b22e_4b22-147.jpg
Notes: 8865675 Tiny legume seedling standing like a Celtic cross among the new grasses in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b22f_4b22-144.jpg
Notes: 8865609 Native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaflets with the sun behind. They never drop all their leaves at once, but they do show beautiful fall colors. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b23g_4b23-184.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin). They only have that breathing hole on the slug-right side. Are there other mollusks with slug-left breathing holes?? (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b23h_4b23-187.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9880684 Cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) on the edge of our garden, growing from a buried oak stump. You can see they are dropping white spores on the caps below. They get the name from the color not the taste. The shrooms are edible and delicious, but they are so beautiful I'd rather watch them mature! (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b23i_4b23-162.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9880534 Tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - on a compost pile in the garden. They have dark spores, maybe Common Psathyrella (Psathyrella condolleana or kin). (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b23a_4b23-169.jpg
Notes: 8874315 Little miracles, little mysteries... This is a tiny seedling Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the woods at home coming up through fallen Apple (Malus pumila) and native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) leaves. That's the miracle. The mystery is that the older leaf on the right is covered with dew drops, but the new palmate leaf right next to it on the left is dry. Que pasa? It was cloudy and gloomy today. This photo is 1/20 @ f/9 @ ISO 200, with tripod and no flash. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2004)
ScientificName: Lupinus latifolius; Malus pumila; Arbutus menziesii
associatedTaxa: Lupinus latifolius; Malus pumila; Arbutus menziesii

File.Name: 4b23b_4b23-165.jpg
Notes: 8874223 A different tiny LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - on the same compost pile in the garden as the [Previous] photo. What a beautiful ruffled skirt she has! (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b23c_4b23-159.jpg
Notes: 8874198 A line of tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - on a compost pile in the garden. They have dark spores, maybe Common Psathyrella (Psathyrella condolleana or kin). It's like a street scene. The two on the left have something going, the others are confused! (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b23d_4b23-173.jpg
Notes: 8874125 I found something strange in the woods today. This is a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) log that never made it to the woodpile. The cut face of the log is at the bottom, and you can see white and orange fungi growing there. This marshmallow-soft velvety-brown critter has crawled around the corner to the top of the log to release its spores. It looks like a puffball, but I reckon it's another Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) plasmodium. It's more of a "them" than an "it". (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b23e_4b23-193.jpg
Notes: 8873984 Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus fultoni or kin) with the blahs on a chilly morning in our garden. It was was laying flat at midday stretched out along the midrib vein of this Greek Sage (Salvia fructicosa?) leaf. Many plants orient their leaves to the sun, so I reckon this cricket was hoping for a free ride to face the sun and warm up enough to fly away. We're losing the sun here on the north-facing slope of the mountains as we approach the solstice. I guess this is what happens to all these late-season insects. One cold morning they (and we) don`t wake up... turn turn turn... (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b23f_4b23-178.jpg
Notes:
8873806 The Slug Ouroboros! Well, the ancient myth has the "Worm Ouroboros" biting its own tail and enclosing the world. It's a yin-yang thing. This "worm" is a Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) on a cloudy day. It was heading downhill until I tried to set up my camera and disturbed it. It demurely withdrew its eye stalks and v_e_r_y__s_l_o_w_l_y turned around to face uphill - and its hindquarters! These are the biggest terrestrial slugs in North America. They only have that breathing hole on the slug-right side. Are there other mollusks with slug-left breathing holes?? (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2004)

I worry about strange critters like these as the world becomes simpler. Fans root for banana slugs at UCSC, and I root for them in the woods. I think of them as an "indicator species" of where I want to live. So far I'm happy here in the Santa Barbara mountains!

File.Name: 4b24h_4b24-221.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9896672 Maybe <i>Lepiota<i>? This mushroom seems to have white spores on the gills, and such beautiful geometry. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b24g_4b24-224.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9896581 Fist-size Agaricus mushroom in the dry part of the garden. Looks tasty, but I think this is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) which is mildly poisonous. Edible or not, it is another beautiful living creature in the woods to be thankful for. Give thanks! (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b24a_4b24-227.jpg
Notes: 8882731 Fist-size Agaricus mushroom in the garden. I was hoping for "The Prince" (Agaricus augustus) which is edible and delicious, but I think this is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) which is mildly poisonous. *D'oh* It sure looks tasty! It has a pallid gills and a smooth cap without scales - though the sand grains on this cap look like fibrils - the cap margin stains yellow when I bruise it, and the thick ring stands out. I find the native agaricus mushrooms very confusing, and I once had a bad experience. I'll stick with the "safe" early Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius) that I found in the canyon for Thanksgiving dinner. But it sure is a beautiful living thing to be thankful for! (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2004)
ScientificName: Agaricus; Agaricus augustus; Agaricus hondensis; Cantharellus cibarius
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Agaricus augustus; Agaricus hondensis; Cantharellus cibarius

File.Name: 4b24b_4b24-205.jpg
Notes: 8882572 My mushroom book describes this yellow Bolbitius vitellinus mushroom as "fragile, flacid, putrescent" - but it sure is pretty when fresh. This one is growing with a new sprout of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the canyon. I also find it in the lawn at school. It has a sticky "slime veil" when fresh like this one. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b24c_4b24-216.jpg
Notes: 8882478 New fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) ferns on a bare sandstone boulder in the canyon, with shadows. These ferns seem to grow from nothing at all. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b24d_4b24-217.jpg
Notes: 8882435 New frond of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) fern opening in the woods. It looks like a hand clutching at nothing, but you can see new sporangia developing into fertile sori on the upturned undersides of the leaflets. Life goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b24e_4b24-208.jpg
Notes: 8882303 Tiny Liverwort (Marchantia?) getting ready to "flower" on a damp rock in the canyon. It looks like a little tongue with a treat! This moss relative is part of the micro-world we rarely notice. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b24f_4b24-198.jpg
Notes: 8882252 Face-to-face with a Scavenger Fly (Sarcophagidae) in the garden. That too-green leaf is a Coleus houseplant that Julie moved outside to get some sunlight. We live on the shady side of the mountains - good for mushrooms, but tough on the plants as we approach the solstice. Insects are getting scarce, but they are getting slower too! (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b25a_2111-034.jpg
Notes:
8890961 Today, I'm thankful that...

Nature is so glorious!

Sometimes you just have to sit and take it all in. This is looking across the Santa Ynez Valley from Figueroa Mountain at sunset. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 January 2002)

We have our Thanksgiving on Friday. I was busy cleaning house today. No time for new photos, so I pulled a few good ones from the archive that I don't think I've posted before. So many perfect moments. I give thanks.

File.Name: 4b25b_3b01-040.jpg
Notes:
8890884 Today, I'm thankful that...

There is still a backcountry to explore.

Here is Julie looking towards Figueroa Mountain from the mountains near home. The official wilderness area starts beyond that, but our backcountry starts just outside the door. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2003)

File.Name: 4b25c_3628-016.jpg
Notes:
8890805 Today, I'm thankful that...

There is a kinship in nature between all living things.

We went for a late swim at a secret swimming hole, and this "baby ducky" joined us. We finally left because it wouldn`t go away. I think it`s a Pied-billed Grebe (Podilymbus podiceps). "Pied" means "having sections or patches colored differently and usually brightly" which fits. This little guy could dive like a seal! (Santa Ynez River, 28 June 2003)

(More photos at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=287304 and [Previous].)

File.Name: 4b25d_3b14-286.jpg
Notes:
8890750 Today, I'm thankful that...

Nature provides and has a sense of humor!

Here are a couple of carrots getting chummy from Jacob's Organic garden near school. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 November 2003)

File.Name: 4b25e_3c20-026.jpg
Notes:
8890707 Today, I'm thankful that...

Some of the best things are still free if you know were to look!

This is a Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom in the canyon. Some local markets sell them for $20+ per pound, but you can find your own in the woods. Recreation has become a commodity. I'm glad there's still a DIY alternative. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2003}

File.Name: 4b25f_hope.jpg
Notes:
8890590 Today, I'm thankful for...

Hope.

Sometimes it's all we have. I'm not sure if I took this photo or my comrade at school, Cynthia. I know this was her prayer flag at school after 9/11. It's a good message from Emily Dickenson.

Cynthia's web page is at:
   http://www.zacatecanyon.com/homepage/homepage.htm
