Hope.

Sometimes it's all we have. I'm not sure if I took this photo or my comrade at school, Cynthia. I know this was her prayer flag at school after 9/11. It's a good message from Emily Dickenson.

Cynthia's web page is at:
   http://www.zacatecanyon.com/homepage/homepage.htm

File.Name: 4b26g_4b26-273.jpg
Notes: 9926878 [mykologos] Ringed Panaeolus (Panaeolus semiovatus aka P. separatus) mushroom still growing on old horse apples in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b26a_4b26-263.jpg
Notes:
8898636 Nickle-sized Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider weaving a silk nest under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf. This is the shady side of the leaf. I first noticed the upper surface wrapped with silk, see the [Previous] photo. She has gnarly bristles on her body, but she has delicate claws to control her silk. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)

Today's photos are all late-season spiders and insects trying to survive the short days and cold nights on our north-facing shady side of the canyon. It's a stumper how all these critters survive the winter from one year to the next. Will this spider lay eggs and die or snuggle in for the winter?
ScientificName: Araneidae) spider weaving a silk nest under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) spider weaving a silk nest under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 4b26b_4b26-252.jpg
Notes: 8898568 This is how I first noticed the Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the [Next] and [Previous] photos. It's a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf wrapped again and again with spider silk. You can see a bit of the hidden spider's leg sticking on the upper right. This weaving must represent a huge effort. I guess she is building a nest to carry her "silver seed to a new home in the sun" next year? (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)
ScientificName: Araneidae) spider in the [Next] and [Previous] photos. It's a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) spider in the [Next] and [Previous] photos. It's a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 4b26c_4b26-277.jpg
Notes: 8898018 Furry face of the Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider in the [Next] two photos. It seems to be working on a coccoon for winter. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b26d_4b26-245.jpg
Notes: 8897998 This is the first Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis or kin) that I've seen for a few days. It visited a few flowers, then sat here gathering sunlight to warm up, like our old cat under the window by the stairs. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b26e_4b26-242.jpg
Notes: 8897977 Sluggish-looking Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus fultoni or kin) with the blahs, hanging out (literally) on a chilly morning in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b26f_4b26-236.jpg
Notes:
8897954 Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus) catching some rays on a sage leaf in the garden. It's trying to warm up to "operating temperature" as the leaves track the low close-to-winter-solstice sun. We only get an hour or two of sunlight in the garden on our north-facing shady side of the canyon. That's good for mushrooms but not insects. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)

Insects and spiders are getting scarce, but I found a few today. It's a stumper how these critters survive the winter from one year to the next.

File.Name: 4b27a_4b26-265.jpg
Notes: This is another kind of insect gall on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf. They look like little mushrooms growing from the leaf. I believe these galls are caused by the Live Oak Gallfly (Callirhytis pomiformes). They have a complicated life cycle with alternating generations of tiny stingless wasps. Males and females will emerge from these leaf galls. They mate and lay eggs that develop into large "Oak Apple" type galls on stems. (See my photo at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=1804154) Parthenogenic females emerge from those galls and lay eggs without mating that will develop these into these leaf galls. It's a strange sort of genetic engineering in nature! (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Gallfly

File.Name: 4b27b_4b26-257.jpg
Notes: A different insect gall on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) twig. This might be the unisexual gall of the Gall Wasp Callirhytis agrifoliae. In the spring, tiny stingless parthenogenetic female wasps emerge and lay eggs that develop a different kind of bisexual gall on leaves. Male and female wasps then emerge and lay eggs that form the galls in the photo. What a complicated life story! (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b27c_4b26-255.jpg
Notes: Tiny insect galls on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) twigs. These galls are probably caused by tiny stingless Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) that develop inside. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b27e_4b26-258.jpg
Notes: Some kind of small insect gall on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) twigs. These galls are probably caused by tiny stingless Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) that develop inside. Notice the hole. Either the wasp escaped or a preditor got in. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b27e_4b26-269.jpg
Notes: Dandelion-like seed of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis var. consanguinea) caught under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b27f_4b22-148.jpg
Notes: Tiny "pixie cup" lichen growing on the ground. This is a Fruticose lichen (maybe genus Cladonia?). They appear to be dusted with white powder, so this may be a reproductive stage. Lichens are part algae and part fungus, so they generally reproduce vegitatively by breaking off little bits of each. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b28g_4b28-042.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9953988 Tiny Springtail (Collembia) insects hiding in the gills of this mushroom in the woods. They hop around like fleas. I have a photo of the mushroom at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?pid=8912910 (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b28h_4b28-056.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 9953881 Tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" (maybe Mycena?) - growing from the fallen leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees after a little cold rain yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b28a_4b28-019.jpg
Notes:
8913141 There are new flowers in the mountains! This is looking close at a cluster of BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) flowers. In a good year the flowers cover the mountains like snow and you can see them from the beach. Last year was dry, and the flowers came in February. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=6606020
There are thousands of flowers on each shrub, but they are each intricate and beautiful up close. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b28b_4b28-030.jpg
Notes: 8913047 There are new flowers in the mountains! This is Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). The little urn-like flowers remind me of those Easter eggs you look into to see something wonderful. We had early rain this year, and this is early for Manzanita flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b28c_4b28-023.jpg
Notes: 8913007 There are new flowers in the mountains, though this might be one of the old ones. This is Douglas' Nightshade (Solanum douglasii). The white star-shaped flowers have beautiful geometry. There are green nectar glands at the base, and the yellow anthers form sealed flat tubes that only open at the tip. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b28d_4b28-037.jpg
Notes: 8912910 Pretty clump of mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. The gills are dark, but the spore print seems to be white. That puts them into that vague "Tricholomatacea" group after you eliminate all the alternatives. The "spores" seem to jump around - see the [Next] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b28e_4b28-045.jpg
Notes: 8912848 Mushrooms are perfect shelters for lots of life - both umbrella and pantry! These tiny Springtail (Collembia) insects were hiding in the gills of the mushroom I turned over for the [Next] photo. They hop around like fleas. I've been trying to do a spore print of this 'shroom, and now they're hopping all over my desk! (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2004)

File.Name: 4b28f_4b28-053.jpg
Notes: 8912769 "This dry weather after the early rains cracks me up... Let's split!" Sorry ;=) We had a bit of new rain and cold yesterday. I walked the same ground the day before and didn't spot this mushroom cap, but here it is the day after. It's one of those little Boletus mushrooms under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia), maybe Boletus truncatus? I've been spotting a few mushrooms making vague "mushrump" mounds in the dry oak leaves. Maybe this bit of rain will bring them all up. (San Marcos Pass, 28 November 2004)

File.Name: 4c04g_4c04-010.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10042443 Pretty red lichens on the bark of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c04a_4c04-020.jpg
Notes: 8959958 New fronds of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) ferns. Here on the shady side of the mountains, I look for these narrow rays of sunlight. The frond in the light is reflecting to the rest. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2004)

File.Name: 4c04b_4c04-030.jpg
Notes: 8959940 Silk cocoon of a Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) on a twig of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). It looks like a little hedgehog hanging from the stem! The beautiful adult moths have wings up to seven inches across. This cocoon is the size of a lime. Alas it was parasitized. You can see a small entry hole on the "snout" in the lower right, and I can hear "something" rattling around inside when I shake it. The cocoon feels and sounds like touching the paper cone of a stereo speaker. It's a magical sound. Native American Chumash shamans made rattles from these cocoons. The adult moths are scarce, and the cocoons are a rare find. I've been collecting them for decades, and I have a basket full. I plan to make my own rattle. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2004)
ScientificName: Hyalophora euryalus) on a twig of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus
associatedTaxa: Hyalophora euryalus) on a twig of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus

File.Name: 4c04c_4c04-004.jpg
Notes:
8959659 I've been watching this marshmallow-sized Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) growing from the cut end of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) log in our woodpile. It started mushy like a marshmallow, but now it's hard and dry and releasing spores. It's more of a "them" than an "it". The brown spore mass feels like the inside of a full vacuum cleaner bag, but it's "flying mother nature's silver seed to a new home in the sun" to colonize a new log in the woods. The spores will become microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists that will colonize another log until it's time to move again. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2004)

For more info, see
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8764834
and many more in the archive.

File.Name: 4c04d_4c04-013.jpg
Notes: 8959529 Ripe seeds of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the canyon. It's spooky that new life is so tentative. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c04e_4c04-037.jpg
Notes: 8959489 It's always dark in our north-facing canyon on the shady side of the mountains now, so I hiked out today for a view. This is looking south from the mountain crest over the greenhouses in More Mesa between Santa Barbara and Goleta, towards Santa Cruz Island sitting under a storm cloud gathering across the channel. So many layers! The next landmass that way is Antarctica! Is that wind or water or shadow making those patterns over the ocean? (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c04f_4c02-122.jpg
Notes: 8959379 Looking from Figueroa Mountain as the sun sets over the western end of the Santa Ynez Mountains. That triangular peak to the right is Tranquillon Mountain, our only local volcano (long dormant). Still lots to explore! (Figueroa Mountain, 2 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c05g_4c05-055.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10058874 New Polypore "Bracket fungi" - maybe Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor aka Coriolus versicolor) - growing from an oak log in our wood pile. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c05h_4c05-047.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10058850 Drizzle today, and this ready-and-waiting Entoloma rhodopolium just couldn't wait any longer to emerge from under the oak leaves! (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c05a_4c05-056.jpg
Notes: 8968629 No, this is not under the sea, but a group of tiny "pixie cup" lichens (maybe genus Cladonia?) growing on a sandstone boulder in the canyon. This is about as close as I can get, and then cropped even closer. Lichens are part algae and part fungus. They are the classic example of symbiosis and mutualism, an arrangement that helps both species. But it looks more like agriculture in this photo, especially that translucent cap on the left. (Tripod, no flash, 1/25 @ f/6.3, ISO 200) (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c05b_4c05-057.jpg
Notes:
8968558 Tiny "pixie cup" lichens growing on a sandstone boulder. It has that "deer in the headlights" look because I used flash on this dark, drizzly day. But I learned something today...

This is a Fruticose lichen (maybe genus Cladonia?), but these fruiting cups are clearly growing up from the butterfly-shaped leaf-like Foliose leaves at the base. I've always wondered whether those little "leaves" are liverworts or fern prothalli. Now I'm sure they are a different form of this lichen. In another lifetime, I'd like to really learn the lichens! (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c05c_4c05-066.jpg
Notes: 8968478 Seedlings and LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - on a compost pile in the garden. The little 'shrooms have dark spores, maybe Common Psathyrella (Psathyrella condolleana or kin). (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c05d_4c05-064.jpg
Notes: 8968457 New seedling leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius), holding rain drops. (Tripod, no flash, 1 sec @ f/32, ISO 200!) (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c05e_4c05-041.jpg
Notes: 8968369 New leaves of a sprouting perennial native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) lifting an apple leaf into the air on the edge of our garden. It's been dark and drizzly all day, just what we need. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c05f_4c04-027.jpg
Notes: 8968328 It's been cold this week! I took this photo in the afternoon, about as warm as it gets here on the shady side of the mountains. The native plants make do. I like that thawed drop with the strange reflections! (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c11g_4c11-004.jpg
Notes:
[mykologos] 10148459 A cascade of white spores like snow on this cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea). (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c11h_4c11-044.jpg
Notes:

File.Name: 4c11a_4c11-050.jpg
Notes:
9017604 Pretty Cortinarius mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. I didn't collect this mushroom, but it has a very slimy cap, so maybe it's Cortinarius cylindripes (or kin). "Captain, there be mushrooms here!" Yes, mushrooms are starting to appear after our early rains this year! (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

We live on the north-facing slope of the mountains, away from the sun. That's good for mushrooms, but tough for photos where the sun never shines. I've been experimenting with flash and long exposures today. This photo is 1.5 sec @ f/20, ISO 200, no flash, with tripod and self-timer exposure. I've tried it both ways, and I prefer not to use flash. I have to learn more about "White Balance".

File.Name: 4c11b_4c11-012.jpg
Notes: 9017511 Tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - pushing up fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves after rain last week. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c11c_4c11-023.jpg
Notes: 9017467 These are Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) if you're collecting for dinner, but it's Oak Root FungusCalifornia Bay (Umbellularia californica) stump near home. The caps are dusted white with spores falling from the cascade of caps above. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

associatedTaxa: Armillariella mellea; Oak Root FungusCalifornia Bay (Umbellularia californica

File.Name: 4c11d_4c11-034.jpg
Notes: 9017290 We had early rains, and mushrooms are starting to appear. It's so easy to walk right over them without noticing! I believe this is pink-spored Entoloma rhodopolium or kin (maybe E. clypeatum?) pushing up through the oak leaves. It has a "farinaceous" oder, like linseed oil - you wouldn't want to eat it, and you shouldn't. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c11e_4c11-048.jpg
Notes: 9017188 Yellow-Staining Milky Cap (Lactarius chrysorheus or kin) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon. I pulled the mushrooms and sliced across the gills with the sharp spine of a convenient oak leaf. The "milk" starts white and turns yellow in seconds. The rule is to not eat any yellow-staining milky caps. This one had a spicy acrid taste, not quite pleasant. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)
CommonNames: Yellow-Staining Milky Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 4c11f_4c11-037.jpg
Notes: 9017073 Early Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom in the canyon. We had good early rains, then a dry spell, then a good rain last week. You can read our rain history in this hefty mushroom - large body, few gills, perfect for harvesting! (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c12g_4c11-002.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10159963 Huge cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) stump near home. These mushrooms are named for the color, not the taste, but the caps are delicious, like shiitake. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c12h_4c11-022.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10159906 Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from a stump of California Bay (Umbellularia californica). You can see the white spores making natural spore prints on the caps below. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c12i_4c11-013.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10159847 Elegant LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - growing from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves and twigs. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c12j_4c11-053.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10159798 It's not blue, but I still think this is a Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom on the edge of our garden - it's got that "blewit look". They usually start blue but lose their color as they age. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c12a_4c11-051.jpg
Notes:
9023861 Huge cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) stump near home. These mushrooms are named for the color, not the taste. You can see the white spores falling from top to bottom to make natural spore prints like cascading champagne glasses. The caps are edible, like shiitake, but I choose to leave them be for beauty and more photo-op as they decompose. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

We've been in the clouds all day. Too gloomy for photos, but a good chance to catch up with some work. Today's photos are from my hike with my dog Mojo yesterday.

File.Name: 4c12b_4c11-021.jpg
Notes: 9023758 Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) stump near home. It's named for the cap color, not the taste. The stems are tough, but fresh caps like these are tasty, like shiitake. We haven't tried this bunch yet. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c12c_4c11-009.jpg
Notes: 9023677 My rule is DON'T pick the flower or mushroom that is the only one of its kind. This pretty 'shroom is growing in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I reckon it's a white-spored Tricholomataceae. That's a big group! (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c12d_4c11-029.jpg
Notes: 9023608 Mushrooms push up through soil and leaves to make "mushrumps" in the woods. I cleared the leaves from the top of this one. It has a conical cap and dull-brown spores. My first guess is Inocybe. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c12e_4c11-039.jpg
Notes: 9023538 Looking close at "gills" of Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom in the canyon. It's a hefty mushroom, but the fertile part is tiny. That shows our rain hstory this year. We had good early rains, then a dry spell, then a good rain last week. I noticed this mushroom before Thanksgiving. It's been waiting underground for just the right weather. Lots of those "hidden mushrooms" are suddenly starting to appear after the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c12f_4c11-042.jpg
Notes: 9023478 New mushrooms are coming up in the woods, but yesterday I found this old cap that I somehow missed before. I think it is Laccaria laccata. See how tiny critters are eating it away, and remember that all mushrooms are the "fruiting bodies" of fungi, like apples from the apple tree. It is their role to release spores for the next generation and be consumed. May we all do as well! (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c14a_4c14-123.jpg
Notes: 9042536 Fall leaves of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata). We have our seasons all at the same time here in central California - leaves are falling while mushrooms and spring wildflowers are starting to come up! (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c14b_4c14-115.jpg
Notes: 9042506 New leaves and tendrils of native Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius) with the sun behind. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c14c_4c14-140.jpg
Notes: 9042494 Strange flowers of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron villosum) growing as a parasite on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c15a_4c15-158.jpg
Notes: 9050895 There's a glorious sunset off to the right. What caught my eye is this native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree flowering and catching that beautiful sunset light. This is several months too-early for oaks to be flowering! I also spotted some too-early wildflowers, including Shooting Stars. Native plants seem to be confused by early rain, then cold, and now warm. As a naturalist, I know that every year is different, and I try to record what I see and not over-simplify or cover fact with theory. (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c15b_4c14-142.jpg
Notes: 9050859 Tiny Aphids (family Aphididae) under a leaf of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron villosum). Well, they're not aphids any more, just aphid husks! I reckon internal parasites - probably tiny parasitic wasps - consumed them from the inside and made their escape out. Did you see the movie "Alien"? (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c15c_4c14-136.jpg
Notes: 9050845 Pretty LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - coming up with new grass. The woods are green with new growth after early rain this year. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 December 2004)

File.Name: 4c15d_4c14-135.jpg
Notes: 9050834 I walked right past this clump of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms yesterday, but I spotted it coming back in the shade. It grows on dead wood, sometimes buried. It's poisonous. It's orange, as is the delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius). You won't confuse them after you've seen them both. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 December 2004)
